But God is absolutely simple with no composition (Q3, Art. 7). There are no parts in God for some to remain stable while others shift. Change requires composition; God has none.

Third, anything that changes acquires something it didn't have before. But God, as infinite and containing all perfection (Q4, Art. 2; Q7, Art. 1), can't acquire anything. There's nothing for him to gain. Every perfection already belongs to him.

The objection from Scripture — passages where God "repents," "is angry," or "comes" — is handled as metaphor. God's actions change in their effects (the world changes), but God himself doesn't change. When the sun dries clay and melts wax, the difference is in the materials, not in the sun. When Scripture says God "repents," it means God's unchanging will produces effects that, from our perspective, look like a reversal.

The deeper point: immutability isn't a limitation. It's a consequence of absolute fullness. A being that is already everything it can be has nowhere to go. Change is always the mark of something incomplete reaching toward completion. God is already complete.
elegant: Change requires potentiality: something must pass from what it could be to what it now is. God has no potentiality. Change requires composition: one part persists while another shifts. God has no parts. Change implies acquiring what one lacked. God, who contains all perfection, lacks nothing. Immutability is not rigidity. It is the stillness of a being already so full that there is nowhere left to go. When Scripture says God repents, the change is in the world, not in God -- the way the sun dries clay and melts wax without itself altering.

refashioned: Question 10 examines eternity — what it is, whether God alone is eternal, how eternity differs from time and from the "aeviternity" of angels, and whether the whole of time is present to God at once.
elegant: From immutability to eternity. What is it? Does it belong to God alone? How does it differ from time, and from the duration of angels? Is the whole of time present to God at once? Aquinas maps the landscape of duration.

refashioned:
What is eternity? Boethius defined it as "the simultaneously whole and perfect possession of interminable life." Aquinas unpacks this definition by connecting it to immutability.

Time is the measure of change — we mark time by before and after in motion. Eternity is the measure of permanence — of what doesn't change. Just as time has succession (one moment following another), eternity has no succession. It's all at once.

Two features define eternity. First, what exists in eternity has no beginning and no end — it's "interminable." Second, eternity has no succession — no before and after, no sequence. It's not an infinitely long timeline; it's the complete absence of a timeline. Everything that belongs to eternity is present simultaneously. "Simultaneously whole" means it's not stretched out across moments the way a life in time is. It's the whole thing, all at once, always.

The difference between eternity and an infinitely long time is crucial. An infinitely long time would still have succession — one moment after another, endlessly. Eternity has no moments at all. It's not duration extended infinitely but duration transcended entirely.

Time measures change. Eternity measures what is beyond all change. Since God is absolutely immutable, eternity is God's proper measure — or rather, God is eternity.
elegant: Time measures change -- one moment following another. Eternity measures what does not change -- everything present at once. Not an infinitely long timeline but the complete absence of a timeline. "Simultaneously whole and perfect possession of interminable life." Eternity is not duration extended without limit. It is duration transcended entirely. No moments, no sequence, no before and after. The whole thing, all at once, always.

refashioned:
Is God identical with his eternity, or is eternity just something God has?

God is his eternity. And the argument follows the same pattern as the arguments that God is his essence (Q3, Art. 3) and his existence (Q3, Art. 4). In anything composite, the measure of duration is distinct from the thing measured — a person has a lifespan, but the person isn't identical with their lifespan. But God is absolutely simple, with no composition. So there can be no distinction between God and his duration. God doesn't have eternity — he is eternity.

Further, things are related to their duration the way they're related to their being. Things that have their being caused have their duration caused and measured by something external. But God's being is not caused; it's self-subsistent. So God's duration isn't measured by anything outside him. God's eternity is just God's own being considered under the aspect of permanence — and since God's being is God, God's eternity is God.
elegant: A person has a lifespan but is not identical with it. God, absolutely simple, cannot be distinguished from his duration. He does not have eternity. He is eternity. God's being is uncaused and self-subsistent, so his duration is measured by nothing outside himself. Eternity is simply God's being considered under the aspect of permanence -- and since God's being is God, God's eternity is God.

refashioned:
Is God the only eternal being? Strictly speaking, yes — eternity in the proper sense belongs to God alone, because only God is absolutely immutable.

But there are qualified senses. Some creatures receive a kind of immutability from God: the angels and the blessed never cease to exist and are fixed in certain operations. They share in eternity to a degree, but not fully, because they still undergo change in some respects — at minimum, they have successive thoughts and acts of will. Scripture sometimes calls things "eternal" loosely, meaning very long-lasting (the "eternal hills") or permanently enduring (the earth "stands forever"). But these are accommodations of language, not claims of true eternity.

The key distinction: eternity proper has no succession. Anything with a before-and-after — even if it never ends — doesn't possess eternity in the strict sense. Only God, whose being is absolutely unchanging and absolutely simple, is truly eternal.
elegant: Eternity in the strict sense belongs to God alone, because only God is absolutely immutable. Angels endure without ceasing but still undergo succession in their thoughts. The "eternal hills" of Scripture are merely very old. Anything with a before and after -- even if it never ends -- does not possess eternity in the proper sense. Only what is absolutely unchanging and absolutely simple exists in the eternal now.

refashioned:
How does eternity relate to time? They differ fundamentally, not just in duration but in kind.

Time measures change: the succession of before and after in motion. Eternity measures absolute permanence: no succession, no before and after. Aeviternity (the duration of angels) sits between them — it measures beings that don't change in their existence but do undergo succession in their operations (thinking one thought after another).

Some have tried to map aeviternity and time onto eternity the way a center-point relates to a circle: eternity is the still center, time is the moving circumference, aeviternity is somewhere between. Aquinas thinks this analogy is only partially helpful. The real distinction is structural. Eternity is simultaneously whole — no parts, no succession. Time is entirely successive — it has no part that exists simultaneously with another part. Aeviternity is compatible with both: in itself (as the measure of unchanging existence) it has no succession, but it can coexist with succession (in the operations of the beings it measures).

The practical point: God doesn't experience history the way we do — as one thing happening after another. From God's eternal vantage, the entire course of time is present. Not as a prediction or memory, but as direct, simultaneous awareness. God sees all of time the way you see an entire landscape from a mountaintop — everything at once, not one scene after another.
elegant: Time, aeviternity, and eternity differ not in length but in kind. Time is entirely successive: no moment coexists with another. Eternity is entirely simultaneous: no succession at all. Aeviternity sits between -- the angel's existence does not change, but its thinking moves from one act to another. God does not experience history as we do. From his eternal vantage, the entire course of time is present -- not predicted, not remembered, but seen. Everything at once, the way you see a whole landscape from a mountaintop.

refashioned: The objection that eternity and time must share some common measure — that they must be comparable the way two durations normally are — fails because eternity and time don't differ as two species within a common genus. They differ as the immeasurable differs from the measured. Eternity exceeds time not by being longer but by being fundamentally different in kind. You can't compare them the way you compare two time spans, because one of them isn't a time span at all.
elegant: Eternity and time are not two durations that differ in length. They differ as the immeasurable differs from the measured. You cannot compare them the way you compare two time spans, because one of them is not a time span at all.

refashioned: Question 11 examines whether God is one — and what "one" means. Four articles investigate the concept of unity itself, whether "one" and "being" are convertible, whether God is maximally one, and whether God is the only God.
elegant: Is God one? What does "one" even mean? Four articles investigate unity: the concept itself, its convertibility with being, whether God is maximally one, and whether there can be more than one God.

refashioned:
Does "one" add anything real to "being," or is it just being under another description?

"One" means "undivided." To say something is one is to say it's a being that isn't split into separate beings. This isn't a new reality layered on top of being — it's a negation applied to being. "One human" means "a human, not divided into two humans." So "one" and "being" are convertible: everything that exists is one (undivided in itself), and whatever is one exists.
elegant: "One" means undivided. To say something is one is to say it holds together as a single being, not split into separate realities. This adds nothing real to being -- it is being seen under the aspect of its own integrity. Everything that exists is one, and whatever is one exists. Unity and being are convertible: two words for the same reality, seen from different angles.

refashioned:
But if "one" just means "undivided being," why do we need the concept at all? Because division is logically prior to unity in the order of understanding. We grasp complexity first, then recognize when something isn't complex — when it holds together as a single thing. "One" captures that recognition.

The objection that "one" is the principle of number, and numbers belong to mathematics, not metaphysics, is handled by distinguishing two senses. The "one" that is the basis of counting (the unit) is a mathematical concept and does add something to being — it adds the notion of being a measure. But the "one" that is convertible with being — the "one" that means "undivided" — is a metaphysical concept that applies to everything and adds nothing real to being.
elegant: We grasp complexity before we grasp simplicity. Division is recognized first, and unity is the recognition that something is not divided. The metaphysical "one" -- undivided being -- is not the same as the mathematical one, which is a unit of counting. The first applies to everything that exists. The second belongs to arithmetic. Unity as a property of being precedes number; you need something to be one before you can count it.

refashioned:
Are "one" and "many" opposed? Yes — but in a specific way. "One" means undivided; "many" means divided. They're opposed as privation to possession: many is the absence of the unity that one asserts.

But this creates an apparent problem: how can multitude be a positive reality if it's defined as the privation of unity? Aquinas answers that division is logically prior to unity (we recognize things as undivided against the background of potential division), so multitude is prior to the mathematical one (the counting unit). But the metaphysical one (undivided being) is prior to multitude, because each member of a multitude must first be one thing before it can be counted as part of many.
elegant: "One" and "many" are opposed as possession to privation: one means undivided, many means divided. But the ordering is subtle. Division is logically prior to unity -- we recognize the undivided against the background of possible division. Yet each member of a multitude must first be one thing before it can count as part of many. Multitude presupposes the unity of its members.

refashioned:
The ordering runs like this: being comes first. Then division (the recognition that beings are distinct from each other). Then unity (each being is undivided in itself). Then multitude (many undivided beings taken together). Then the mathematical one (a single being taken as a measure for counting). Then number (the result of counting).

So "one" in the metaphysical sense — undivided being — doesn't presuppose number. It's the other way around. You need unity before you can count. The objection that "one" is opposed to "many" and therefore shouldn't be a universal property of being is resolved: the "many" that opposes the mathematical one is different from the "many" that opposes the metaphysical one. The metaphysical one applies to everything, including each member of a multitude.
elegant: The sequence runs: being, then division (beings are distinct from one another), then unity (each is undivided in itself), then multitude (many unified beings taken together), then the counting unit, then number. You need unity before you can count. The metaphysical one grounds the mathematical one, not the reverse.

refashioned:
Is God maximally one — the most unified being there is?

Yes, on three grounds. First, "one" means undivided. God is the most undivided being possible, because he is absolutely simple — no composition of any kind. Every other being has some form of composition (matter and form, essence and existence, substance and accidents) that introduces a potential line of division. God has none.

Second, God is undivided in the strongest sense. Other things may be indivisible in one respect but divisible in another — an atom is materially indivisible but formally complex. God is indivisible in every respect.

Third, every other unity is a unity of composition — parts held together. God's unity is a unity of simplicity — not parts united but a reality with no parts at all. This is the highest possible mode of being one.

God is not just one being among others. He is being in its most concentrated, undivided, simple form.
elegant: God is the most one thing there is. Every other being has some composition -- matter and form, essence and existence, substance and accidents -- that introduces a potential line of division. God has none. Other things are indivisible in one respect but divisible in another. God is indivisible in every respect. Other unities are assemblies -- parts held together. God's unity is simplicity itself: not parts united but a reality with no parts at all. The highest possible mode of being one.

refashioned:
Is there only one God? The philosophical and theological traditions converge: yes.

Three arguments. First, from divine simplicity. If there were two gods, they'd have to differ in some way — otherwise they'd be the same god. But any difference would mean one has something the other lacks, introducing privation into one of them. A being with a privation isn't absolutely perfect. So at most one being can be absolutely perfect — at most one God.

Second, from the infinity of divine perfection. God possesses all perfection without limit. If there were a second god, it would either have all the same perfections (making it indistinguishable from the first) or lack some (making it imperfect, and therefore not God). You can't have two infinite beings of the same kind, because there'd be nothing to distinguish them.

Third, from the unity of the world. The world is a coordinated, ordered whole. A coordinated order is better explained by one governing principle than by many — the more unified the source, the more unified the governance. Since the world does exhibit unified order, it points to a single source.

The historical objection — that many cultures have worshipped multiple gods — doesn't establish that multiple gods exist, only that humans have used the word "gods" for powerful beings or forces. Aquinas's argument is metaphysical, not anthropological: the very concept of an absolutely perfect, infinite, simple being admits of no plural.
elegant: There can be only one God. If two gods existed, they would differ somehow -- and any difference means one lacks what the other has, which means privation, which means imperfection, which means not God. Two infinite beings of the same kind would have nothing to distinguish them. And the world's coordinated order points to a single governing intelligence, not a committee. The concept of an absolutely perfect, infinite, simple being admits of no plural.

refashioned: Question 12 is one of the most epistemologically rich in the Summa. Having established what God is (in negative terms), Aquinas asks how — and whether — we can know him. Thirteen articles investigate whether any created intellect can see God's essence, what medium or "light" is required, whether bodily eyes can see God, what capacity is needed, whether comprehension of God is possible, and how natural reason can know God in this life.
elegant: Having established what God is by saying what he is not, Aquinas asks the epistemological question: can we know him? Can any created mind see God's essence? What light is required? Can bodily eyes see God? Is comprehension possible? How far can natural reason reach? Thirteen articles on the most consequential question a mind can ask about its own limits.

refashioned:
Can any created mind ever see God's essence — God as he actually is, not just through images or effects?

Yes. And this must be the case, because our ultimate happiness consists in this vision. Human intellect naturally seeks to know the cause behind every effect. When we observe effects in nature and trace them back to God as their cause, the mind isn't satisfied until it reaches the cause's essence — not just the fact that God exists but what God is. If the created intellect could never reach God's essence, it could never achieve its ultimate fulfillment. That would mean either the desire for happiness is futile (which contradicts the purpose built into our nature) or our happiness lies in something other than God (which contradicts everything established so far).

The objection that an infinite gap separates the finite mind from the infinite God is real but doesn't settle the question. Seeing God's essence doesn't mean comprehending it exhaustively. A created mind can see God without encompassing everything God is, the way you can see the ocean without seeing every drop. The key is that God elevates the created intellect beyond its natural capacity through grace — specifically through what Aquinas calls the "light of glory." Without that supernatural elevation, no created mind can see God. With it, the vision is real but not exhaustive.
elegant: The mind naturally seeks the cause behind every effect, and it is not satisfied until it reaches the cause's essence -- not just that it is, but what it is. If the created intellect could never see God's essence, its deepest desire would be permanently frustrated, and happiness would be impossible. So the vision must be possible. But not by our own power. An infinite gap separates the finite mind from the infinite God. Seeing God's essence requires supernatural elevation -- what Aquinas calls the light of glory. With it, the vision is real but not exhaustive: you can see the ocean without seeing every drop.

refashioned:
When the blessed see God's essence, do they see it through some intermediary — a likeness, an image, a concept — or directly?

Directly. No created likeness can represent God's essence adequately, because God's essence is infinite and any created likeness is finite. A likeness represents something by sharing its form in some way, but no finite form can represent an infinite essence. If the blessed saw God only through a likeness, they wouldn't see God's essence at all — they'd see the likeness.

Further, the whole point of the beatific vision is that God's essence itself becomes the intelligible form of the blessed intellect. In normal knowing, the mind grasps an object through a mental image or concept that represents it. In the vision of God, God's essence directly joins itself to the created intellect as the form by which it knows. No intermediary is needed because God himself functions as both the object known and the means of knowing.
elegant: The blessed see God directly -- not through a likeness, an image, or a concept, but through God's essence itself joined to the intellect as the form by which it knows. No created representation can capture an infinite essence. If the blessed saw only a likeness of God, they would never see God at all -- they would see the painting, not the person. In the beatific vision, God himself is both the object known and the means of knowing.

refashioned: This direct vision doesn't mean the created intellect becomes God or equals God. The divine essence is received in the created mind according to the mind's finite capacity — the way a vessel receives water according to its own shape and size. The mind sees God, but it doesn't see God the way God sees himself. The object is the same; the mode of seeing differs.
elegant: Direct vision does not mean equality. The divine essence is received in the created mind according to the mind's finite capacity -- the way water takes the shape of its vessel. The object is the same God; the mode of seeing differs. The finite creature touches the infinite, but it does not become infinite in the touching.

refashioned:
Can God's essence be seen with bodily eyes?

No. Sight perceives what has physical form — color, shape, dimension. God has no body and no physical form. So God's essence can't be the object of bodily vision.

But bodily eyes can see the effects of God's presence — a visible glory, a theophany, a physical manifestation. The difference is between seeing God (impossible for the eyes) and seeing something that displays or represents God (possible). The glorified bodies of the blessed may see God's corporeal effects with a new intensity and clarity, but even glorified eyes remain physical organs, and the essence of God remains non-physical.

The analogy: you can see the effects of intelligence in a person's actions, but you can't literally see their intelligence with your eyes. You'd need intellect to perceive intellect. Similarly, only an intellect — and specifically, an intellect elevated by the light of glory — can perceive the divine essence.
elegant: God's essence cannot be seen with bodily eyes. Sight perceives color, shape, dimension -- and God has none. Even glorified eyes remain physical organs; the essence of God remains non-physical. You can see the effects of intelligence in a person's actions, but you cannot literally see their intelligence. Only an intellect can perceive the essence of what is purely intellectual. The eyes will see God's glory displayed in creation; only the mind, lifted by grace, will see God.

refashioned:
Can any created intellect see God's essence by its own natural power, without supernatural assistance?

No. To know a thing naturally, you need a natural proportion between the knower and the known. The created intellect, by nature, is proportioned to created things — material forms for embodied minds, immaterial forms for angelic minds. But God's essence infinitely exceeds every created form. There's no natural bridge.

The supernatural elevation required is what Aquinas calls the "light of glory" — lumen gloriae. This isn't a physical light but a strengthening of the intellect that raises it above its natural capacity, enabling it to receive the divine essence as its intelligible form. Just as natural light enables the eye to see colors, the light of glory enables the intellect to see God.

The distribution of this light isn't uniform. Those who have more charity — more love for God — receive more of the light of glory and therefore see God more perfectly. This is what Scripture means by "in thy light we shall see light": by the supernatural light God gives, we see the divine light itself.
elegant: No created mind can see God by its own natural power. The intellect is naturally proportioned to created things -- material forms for embodied minds, immaterial forms for angels. God's essence infinitely exceeds every created form. The bridge must be built from God's side: the light of glory, a supernatural strengthening that raises the intellect above its native capacity. And this light is not given uniformly. Those with more charity receive more light and see God more perfectly. In thy light we shall see light.

refashioned:
The objection that an infinite addition to a finite power is needed to reach the infinite — and therefore no created intellect can ever bridge the gap — is answered by distinguishing comprehensive from non-comprehensive vision. The created intellect doesn't need infinite power to see God. It needs enough power to see, not to comprehend exhaustively. A finite elevation (the light of glory) enables a finite seeing of the infinite — real but not total.

This is critical to Aquinas's whole theology of happiness: beatitude is a genuine seeing of God's essence, but it always remains the seeing of a finite creature — real contact with the infinite, not mastery of it.
elegant: The gap between finite mind and infinite God does not require infinite power to cross -- only enough power to see, not to comprehend exhaustively. The light of glory is a finite elevation enabling a finite seeing of the infinite. Real contact, not mastery. Genuine vision, not total grasp. This is the structure of beatitude: a creature's real encounter with the infinite, real but never finished.

refashioned:
Can the created intellect comprehend God — grasp God's essence totally and exhaustively?

No. To comprehend something is to know it as fully as it can be known — to match the mode of knowing to the full knowability of the object. God is infinitely knowable. A created intellect, even elevated by the light of glory, remains finite. So it can never know God as fully as God is knowable. It sees God's essence truly but not comprehensively.

The analogy: someone can arrive at a destination without having explored every path to it. The blessed reach God — they see his essence — but they don't exhaust what there is to see. God remains inexhaustible even to those who see him face to face.

This isn't a defect in the vision but a necessary feature of finite creatures encountering the infinite. The joy of the beatific vision is not diminished by its non-comprehensiveness — if anything, the inexhaustibility of God is part of what makes the vision endlessly fulfilling.
elegant: To comprehend something is to know it as fully as it can be known. God is infinitely knowable. No finite mind, however elevated, can exhaust what there is to see. The blessed see God's essence truly but not comprehensively. They arrive at the destination without having explored every path. And this is not a defect in the vision -- it is the structure of a finite creature meeting the inexhaustible. The joy is not diminished by the endlessness. It is sustained by it.

refashioned:
Among those who do see God's essence, do some see more perfectly than others?

Yes. The vision of God is real for all the blessed, but it's not uniform. The light of glory is given in varying degrees, and a greater light enables a more perfect vision. This isn't about seeing a different God but about seeing the same God more deeply, more fully.

The principle: the capacity to see God is determined by the intellectual power as elevated by the light of glory. Since this elevation varies — proportioned to each person's charity and God's gift — the resulting vision varies. One person sees more of God's essence without either person seeing all of it.
elegant: Among the blessed, some see God more perfectly than others. The light of glory is given in varying degrees, proportioned to each soul's charity. One sees deeper than another without either seeing all. It is not a different God they see but a deeper view of the same God -- as two people can both grasp a proof, but one with surer and more penetrating insight.

refashioned:
The objection that God either is seen or isn't — that there can't be degrees — treats seeing as binary. But Aquinas argues it's more like understanding: two people can both understand a proof, but one grasps it more deeply, with a surer and more penetrating insight. Similarly, all the blessed see God's essence, but they don't all see it with equal depth. The more perfectly one participates in the light of glory, the more perfectly one sees God.

This has a practical consequence for Aquinas's moral theology: the degree of charity one achieves in this life determines the degree of glory — and therefore the depth of vision — one receives in the next. The saints see God more fully than others, not because they see a different God but because they bring a greater capacity to the seeing.
elegant: Seeing God is not binary. It admits of depth. The charity achieved in this life determines the capacity for glory in the next. The saints see God more fully not because they see a different God but because they bring a greater capacity to the seeing. Love in time becomes vision in eternity.

refashioned:
When the blessed see God's essence, do they thereby see everything that exists — all of creation, all of history, all possible things?

Not necessarily. Seeing God's essence doesn't automatically mean seeing everything God knows. The blessed see all that God is, but God's knowledge includes infinitely many possible things, and no finite mind can grasp all of them.

What the blessed do see in God's essence is whatever God wills them to see — and they do see at least whatever pertains to their own perfection and happiness. They see how all things relate to God as their cause. But they don't see the entire scope of God's knowledge, because that would require comprehending God, which no created mind can do.
elegant: Seeing God's essence does not automatically mean seeing everything God knows. The blessed see all that pertains to their happiness, and they see how all things relate to God as their cause. But God's knowledge encompasses infinitely many possible things, and no finite mind can take in all of them. To see everything in God would require comprehending God, and no creature can do that.

refashioned: The more perfectly someone sees God, the more things they see in God. The higher angels see more than the lower angels; the blessed who have more glory see more than those with less. But no created intellect — however exalted — sees everything that God can do, because God's power is infinite. What they see is enough: God himself, and in God, all that pertains to the fullness of their created happiness.
elegant: The more perfectly someone sees God, the more things they see in God. Higher angels see more than lower; the more glorified see more than the less. But no created intellect, however exalted, sees everything God can do. What they see is enough: God himself, and in God, all that their created happiness requires.

refashioned:
Is the beatific vision mediated by any "likeness" or mental representation? No — the blessed see God directly, through his essence and not through any created image. But does this mean the things they see in God are also seen without likenesses?

For God himself, the answer is clear: the divine essence itself is the form by which the intellect knows God. No representation stands between the mind and God. But for the created things the blessed see in God, the situation is nuanced. They can know creatures both through God's essence (in which all things pre-exist as in their cause) and through the creatures' own proper likenesses. Seeing creatures in God is more perfect — it means knowing them as God knows them, in their ultimate cause. Seeing them through their own likenesses is a lesser, supplementary mode of knowledge.
elegant: The blessed see God directly, through his essence -- not through any created image. But for the creatures they perceive in God, the situation is layered. They can know created things both through God's essence (seeing them as God sees them, in their ultimate cause) and through the things' own proper likenesses. The first mode is the higher one. To know a thing in its cause is more perfect than to know it only in itself.

refashioned:
Do the blessed see God's essence all at once or progressively — learning more over time?

All at once. Since the vision of God's essence isn't discursive (not reasoning from premises to conclusions) but immediate and intuitive, it doesn't unfold in a temporal sequence. The blessed don't first see one aspect of God and then another. They see whatever they see in a single, simultaneous act of vision.

This follows from the nature of the medium — God's essence is one and simple, so the act of knowing through it is correspondingly unified. Time and sequence belong to change. The beatific vision, being an act of the intellect elevated beyond its natural condition, participates in eternity's simultaneity.
elegant: The beatific vision is not progressive but simultaneous. The blessed do not first see one aspect of God, then another. They see whatever they see in a single, undivided act, because the medium of their vision -- God's essence -- is one and simple. Time and sequence belong to change. The vision of God participates in eternity's all-at-once.

refashioned:
Can anyone in this life — still in the body, not yet in glory — see God's essence?

The standard answer is no: natural human knowledge in this life works through sensory experience, abstracting intelligible forms from material things. God has no material form to abstract. So the natural process of human knowing cannot reach God's essence.

But Aquinas allows for extraordinary exceptions: Moses and Paul were granted a momentary vision of God's essence while still alive, according to Augustine's interpretation. This was a miraculous, temporary elevation — not the normal condition of earthly life. In these exceptional cases, the mind was lifted entirely beyond sensory cognition for a brief moment, receiving the light of glory in a transient way.

For everyone else in this life, knowledge of God is limited to what can be inferred from created effects. We know that God exists and what God is not, and we can form analogical concepts of God's attributes — but we cannot see God's essence.
elegant: In this life, no one sees God's essence by the ordinary course of nature. Human knowledge works through the senses, abstracting intelligible forms from material things, and God has no material form to abstract. The exceptions are extraordinary: Moses and Paul, granted a momentary, miraculous vision while still alive -- a transient taste of the light of glory that properly belongs to the next life. For everyone else, knowledge of God here is limited to what can be inferred from created effects.

refashioned:
The scriptural passages where people "see God" — Jacob, Isaiah, others — are interpreted as seeing a figure or image that represents God, not the divine essence itself. These were imaginative or intellectual visions of high quality but not the beatific vision.

As for the general principle: while we are in the body, our intellect naturally turns to material images (phantasms) to understand anything. Since God has no material image, seeing God's essence requires being lifted above this natural dependency on sensory experience — which happens fully only in the next life, and in this life only through extraordinary divine intervention.
elegant: When Scripture says Jacob or Isaiah "saw God," it means they saw a figure or image representing God -- a vision of great power but not the beatific vision itself. While we inhabit the body, our intellect naturally turns to material images to understand anything. Seeing God's essence requires being lifted above this dependency entirely -- something that happens fully only beyond the body, and in this life only by extraordinary intervention.

refashioned:
Can we know God by natural reason in this life? Yes — but only indirectly, through his effects.

Natural reason works from sensory experience. The senses give us the material world. From the material world, we can reason to its cause. This gives us knowledge that God exists, that God is the first cause, and various negative and relational truths (God is not material, not finite, not caused; God is the cause of all things, the governor of all things). But it doesn't give us knowledge of what God is in himself.

The mode of our knowledge determines its content. Since we know God from material effects, our concepts of God are shaped by material categories — we conceive of God's goodness on the model of the goodness we experience in creatures, God's power on the model of the power we observe. These concepts are true as far as they go, but they don't capture the divine reality adequately. We know God as the cause of things we do experience, and we know that God exceeds everything we can conceive — but we don't positively grasp the divine essence.
elegant: Natural reason can know God -- but only indirectly, through his effects. The senses give us the material world; from the material world, reason infers its cause. This yields knowledge that God exists, that he is not material, not finite, not caused -- and that he is the cause, governor, and source of all things. But it does not yield knowledge of what God is in himself. Our concepts of divine goodness are shaped by the goodness we encounter in creatures. They are true as far as they go. They do not go all the way.

refashioned:
Is knowledge of God through grace (in this life, short of the beatific vision) superior to knowledge of God through natural reason?

Yes. Grace gives us faith, and faith gives us knowledge of God that exceeds what natural reason can reach. Through faith, we know truths about God — the Trinity, the Incarnation — that no amount of philosophical reasoning could discover.

But grace doesn't replace the natural mode of knowing; it works through it. Even faith-based knowledge in this life uses concepts derived from sensory experience. Grace elevates the content of what we know about God (we learn more) but doesn't change the mode of knowing (we still think in created categories, images, and analogies). The knowledge of God through grace is more extensive than natural reason can achieve, but it remains imperfect compared to the direct vision of God's essence.

The hierarchy of knowing God: natural reason (least perfect, available to all thinking beings), grace-assisted faith (more perfect, available to believers), and the beatific vision (most perfect, available to the blessed in the next life). Each represents a real but progressively fuller access to divine truth.
elegant: Grace does not replace the natural mode of knowing; it works through it, elevating the content without changing the method. Through faith, we know truths about God that no philosophy could reach -- Trinity, Incarnation -- yet we still think in created categories, images, and analogies. Three levels of access, ascending: natural reason, grace-assisted faith, and the beatific vision. Each is real. Each is fuller than the last. Only the last is face to face.

refashioned: Question 13 is about language: can we name God at all, and if so, what do our names for God actually mean? Twelve articles investigate whether any name applies to God, whether names like "good" and "wise" are applied substantially or only metaphorically, whether such names are synonymous, whether they're univocal or equivocal, whether they apply first to God or to creatures, and what the most proper name of God is.
elegant: Can we name God? And if so, what do our names mean? Twelve articles on the most fundamental problem in theological language: whether finite words can reach an infinite reality, and what happens to meaning when they try.

refashioned:
Can we name God at all? The objection is that names follow knowledge, and we can't know God's essence — so we can't name it. Others argue that names either signify concrete individuals (but God isn't a concrete particular in the usual sense) or abstract universals (but God isn't an abstraction).

Aquinas answers that we can name God, but only to the extent that we can know God — which in this life means from his effects. Since our knowledge of God is derived from creatures, our names for God are also derived from creatures. We name God "good" not because we directly perceive divine goodness but because we perceive goodness in creatures and recognize God as its cause and source.

This means our names for God are real but imperfect. They genuinely refer to God — they're not just names for our own ideas — but they don't express the divine essence as it is in itself. They express it as it's reflected in created effects, which is the best our language can do in this life.
elegant: We can name God -- but only as far as we can know him, which in this life means from his effects. We call God "good" not because we perceive divine goodness directly but because we see goodness in creatures and recognize God as its source. The names are real but imperfect: they genuinely refer to God, not just to our ideas about God, but they express the divine essence only as refracted through created effects. Our language reaches God. It does not encompass him.

refashioned:
Are names like "good," "wise," and "living" said of God merely metaphorically — as when we call God a "rock" — or do they apply in a more substantial sense?

They apply substantially, not merely metaphorically. Aquinas distinguishes two kinds of divine names. Metaphorical names (rock, lion, shepherd) apply to God only in terms of a resemblance between the behavior of the named thing and some divine action. God isn't literally a rock; the comparison points to stability and reliability. These names belong properly to the physical things and only figuratively to God.
elegant: Not all divine names are metaphors. "God is a rock" compares a physical property to a divine action -- stability likened to geological permanence. That is metaphor. But "God is good" is different. Goodness genuinely exists in God. It exists more truly in God than in anything else, because God is the source of all goodness.

refashioned:
But names like "good," "wise," and "living" are different. The perfections they signify — goodness, wisdom, life — genuinely exist in God. In fact, they exist more truly in God than in creatures, because God is the source of all goodness, wisdom, and life. These names are said of God substantially: they describe what God truly is, not just what God resembles.

The catch is that while the perfections themselves belong more properly to God than to creatures, the mode of signifying — the way the words mean — is shaped by our creaturely experience. When we say "God is good," the goodness we mean is real and genuinely God's, but the concept of goodness we use is abstracted from created goodness. The word carries the right content but in a limited container.

This is the crucial distinction between what a name signifies (the perfection itself, which belongs primarily to God) and how it signifies (the mode of the concept, which is shaped by our experience of creatures). The first is adequate; the second is not. We speak truly about God, but we never speak about God adequately.
elegant: Names like "good" and "wise" describe what God truly is, not merely what God resembles. The perfection they signify belongs to God more properly than to any creature. But the mode of signifying -- the way the word means -- is drawn from creaturely experience. When we say "God is good," the goodness is real and genuinely God's, but the concept of goodness we deploy was learned from finite things. The content is adequate. The container is not. We speak truly about God. We never speak about God adequately.

refashioned: The position that all divine names are merely metaphorical — held by Maimonides — would mean we can't say anything true about what God is, only about what God does or what God isn't. Aquinas rejects this. If "God is good" means only "God is the cause of goodness" (not that God himself is good), then "God is a body" would be equally true (since God is the cause of bodies). We'd lose any basis for affirming some names of God and denying others. The entire project of theology — saying what God is like while denying what God is not like — would collapse.
elegant: If all divine names were merely metaphorical, "God is good" would mean only "God causes goodness" -- and by that logic, "God is a body" would be equally valid, since God causes bodies. We would lose all basis for affirming some names and denying others. The entire enterprise of theology -- saying what God is like while denying what God is not -- would collapse. Some names must signify what God is, not just what God does.

refashioned:
Can any name be applied to God literally — in its proper sense — or is everything we say about God figurative?

Some names apply literally. The principle: names that signify perfections absolutely — without building any creaturely mode of being into the meaning — can be said of God in their proper sense. "Good," "existent," "living" signify perfections without implying any particular material mode. These apply to God properly.

Names that build a creaturely mode into the very meaning of the word can only apply to God metaphorically. "Thinking" (which in human experience implies discursive reasoning from premises to conclusions) can't apply literally to God, who knows everything in a single act. So when applied to God, such terms need qualification or metaphorical interpretation.

The test: strip away the creaturely mode of the perfection. If the perfection itself (goodness, wisdom, being) is something that could belong to God, the name applies properly. If the word inherently implies a creaturely limitation (physical location, temporal sequence, bodily activity), it applies only metaphorically.
elegant: Some names apply to God in their proper sense, others only figuratively. The test: strip away the creaturely mode of the perfection. If what remains -- goodness, wisdom, being -- can belong to God without implying material limitation, the name applies properly. If the word inherently encodes a creaturely constraint -- physical location, temporal sequence, bodily activity -- it applies only as metaphor. "Good" is proper. "Rock" is not.

refashioned:
When we call God "good," "wise," "just," and "living," are these all synonyms? After all, God is absolutely simple — in God, goodness, wisdom, justice, and life are all identical with the divine essence. If they all refer to the same thing, aren't they just different words for the same meaning?

No. The names aren't synonymous because they express different concepts, even though they refer to the same reality. "Good" and "wise" have different definitions — they capture different aspects of perfection that we distinguish intellectually, even though in God these aspects are one. Synonyms have the same meaning; these names have different meanings that converge on a single reality.

If they were synonymous, saying "God is good" and "God is wise" would be redundant — no different from saying the same thing twice. But clearly these predications convey different information, even when applied to God. Our intellect grasps the simple divine perfection from multiple angles, producing multiple concepts that are genuinely distinct in meaning even though their referent is one.
elegant: In God, goodness, wisdom, justice, and life are all identical with the divine essence. But the names are not synonymous, because they express different concepts. "Good" and "wise" have different definitions. They capture different facets of perfection that our mind distinguishes even though in God they are one. If they were synonyms, saying "God is good" and "God is wise" would be mere repetition. It is not. Our intellect grasps the simple divine perfection from multiple angles, producing genuinely distinct meanings that converge on a single, undivided reality.

refashioned:
When we use the word "good" of both God and creatures, do we use it univocally (same meaning), equivocally (totally different meaning), or analogically (related but different)?

Not univocally. Univocal predication means the same definition applies in exactly the same way to both subjects. But no perfection exists in God the same way it exists in creatures — God's goodness infinitely exceeds created goodness and exists in a fundamentally different mode. A word used univocally of God and creatures would misrepresent the gap.

Not purely equivocally either. If "good" meant something completely different when applied to God and to creatures, we could never reason from creatures to God — the word would be a homonym, like "bank" (riverbank vs. financial institution), conveying no real connection.
elegant: When we call both God and creatures "good," the word is neither univocal nor equivocal. Not univocal: divine goodness infinitely exceeds created goodness and exists in a fundamentally different mode. The same definition does not apply in the same way. Not equivocal: there is a real causal connection between the two uses. Creaturely goodness derives from divine goodness and bears a genuine, if inadequate, resemblance to it.

refashioned:
The answer is analogy. Analogical predication means a term is used of two things because they're related — not identical in nature but connected through a real relationship. When we say food is "healthy" and a person is "healthy," we don't mean the same thing in the same way, but the two uses are connected (healthy food causes health in the person). Similarly, when we say a creature is "good" and God is "good," the uses are connected because creaturely goodness derives from and resembles divine goodness.

The key: in analogical predication, the term applies primarily to one thing and derivatively to the other. As for the perfection itself (goodness, wisdom), it belongs primarily to God and derivatively to creatures, since creatures receive their perfections from God. But as for our use of the word, we apply it first to creatures (since that's where we first encounter goodness) and only then, by inference, to God. The order of being and the order of naming run in opposite directions.
elegant: The answer is analogy: a term used of two things because they are related, though not identical. The perfection itself -- goodness, wisdom -- belongs first to God and derivatively to creatures, since creatures receive their perfections from God. But our use of the word runs in the opposite direction: we learn it from creatures first, then apply it to God. The order of being and the order of naming move in opposite directions. In reality, God is the original. In our speech, he is the afterthought.

refashioned: This analogical approach avoids both the presumption of univocity (claiming our language captures God exactly as it captures creatures) and the despair of equivocity (claiming our language tells us nothing real about God). We speak truly but imperfectly. Our names reach God but don't encompass him.
elegant: Analogy avoids both the presumption of univocity -- claiming our language captures God exactly as it captures creatures -- and the despair of equivocity -- claiming our language tells us nothing real about God at all. We speak truly but imperfectly. Our names reach God but do not encompass him.

refashioned:
When a perfection-word like "good" is applied to both God and creatures, does it apply first to God (since God is the source of all goodness) or first to creatures (since that's where we learn the word)?

Aquinas distinguishes two orderings. In the order of the thing signified — the perfection itself — the name belongs first to God. God is good before any creature is good, because creatures receive their goodness from God. In the order of naming — how we come to use the word — the name belongs first to creatures, because we learn what "good" means from created goods before we apply it to God.

This double ordering is crucial. It means our language about God is genuinely about God (not just about ourselves or our concepts) — because the perfections we name really do belong first to God. But it also means our language is always derivative and inadequate — because the way we understand and apply these names is shaped by creaturely experience.

Some names, like "cause of goodness" or "most powerful," do apply first to God even in the order of naming, because they explicitly describe God's causal relation to creatures. But names that signify perfections absolutely — "good," "wise" — are used of creatures first in the order of speech, even though what they signify belongs first to God in the order of reality.
elegant: In the order of reality, perfection-words like "good" belong first to God -- he is good before any creature is, because creatures receive their goodness from him. In the order of naming, they belong first to creatures -- we learn what "good" means from created goods before applying it to God. This double ordering is what makes theology possible and keeps it humble. Our language genuinely refers to God because the perfections really do belong to him first. Our language remains inadequate because the way we understand those perfections is shaped by creaturely experience.

refashioned:
Do names applied to God signify a relation to creatures, or do they signify something in God himself?

The position Aquinas rejects is that divine names are purely relational — that "God is good" means nothing more than "God causes goodness in creatures." If that were true, all divine names would ultimately mean "God is a cause," and they'd all be synonymous with "first cause." There'd be no basis for affirming "God is wise" while denying "God is a body."

Aquinas holds that names like "good" and "wise" signify something in God's own nature, not merely a relation to creatures. When we call God good, we mean God himself is good — that goodness belongs to the divine substance. The name does signify a relation to creatures indirectly (since we derive the concept from creatures), but its primary signification is the perfection itself as it exists in God.
elegant: "God is good" does not merely mean "God causes goodness in creatures." If it did, all divine names would reduce to "first cause," and there would be no basis for affirming "God is wise" while denying "God is a body." Names like "good" and "wise" signify something in God's own nature. The name does carry an indirect relation to creatures -- since that is where we learn the concept -- but its primary signification is the perfection itself as it exists in God.

refashioned:
The complications multiply because some names do signify relations — "Lord," "Creator," "Savior" — while others signify the divine substance — "good," "wise," "infinite." Relational names obviously imply a connection to creatures. But even substantial names carry an implicit reference to creatures in how we understand them, since all our concepts of divine perfections are derived from created instances.

Aquinas threads the needle: substantial divine names signify the divine substance primarily and the relation to creatures only as a consequence. God is good in himself; creatures are good by participation. We learn the word "good" from creatures, but what the word ultimately signifies — goodness itself — belongs first and most fully to God.
elegant: Some divine names signify relations -- Lord, Creator, Savior. Others signify the divine substance -- good, wise, infinite. Even substantial names carry an implicit reference to creatures in how we understand them, since all our concepts of divine perfections are abstracted from created instances. But the substance comes first, the relation second. God is good in himself; the relation to creatures is a consequence.

refashioned:
The application of names to God also involves a kind of temporal reference that doesn't imply change in God. When we say "God is Lord" or "God creates," these names seem to imply temporal relations — lordship requires subjects, creation implies a beginning. But the relation is real on the creature's side (the creature really depends on God) and rational on God's side (God doesn't change by becoming Lord or Creator — the change is in the creatures that come to exist).

This means we can apply new names to God over time without implying that God changes. "God became the Creator" is true from our temporal perspective, but it doesn't mean God underwent a change. It means creatures came into existence and thereby entered into a real relation to God. The new name reflects a new relation on the creature's side, not a new reality on God's side.

The deeper lesson: our language about God is necessarily time-bound and perspective-bound, even when what it refers to is timeless and absolute. The imperfection is in our mode of speech, not in the divine reality.
elegant: Names like "Lord" and "Creator" seem to imply temporal relations, but the change is on the creature's side, not God's. God does not become Lord by undergoing a change. Creatures come into existence and thereby enter into a real relation to him. Our language about God is necessarily time-bound and perspective-bound, even when what it refers to is timeless and absolute. The imperfection lives in our mode of speech, not in the divine reality.

refashioned:
Does the name "God" signify the divine nature, or is it an operational term — meaning something like "the one who oversees all things"?

Aquinas argues it signifies the divine nature, though our use of it is shaped by our experience of divine operations. The name "God" (from the Greek theos) was originally connected with providence, seeing, or burning — all operational descriptions. But what the name has come to mean is the divine nature itself: the reality that is infinite, perfect, and the source of all things.

The name "God" is communicable in a qualified sense — Scripture calls humans and angels "gods" metaphorically or by participation — but it signifies properly only the one being whose nature it is to be God. When Scripture says "the Lord your God is one God," it uses the name to designate the unique divine nature, not just a role or function.

So "God" is not a proper name in the narrow sense (it describes a nature, not an individual), but it functions as a proper name because only one being has that nature.
elegant: "God" signifies the divine nature, not merely a function or office. The word was originally connected to providence, seeing, or burning -- operational descriptions. But what the name has come to mean is the reality itself: infinite, perfect, the source of all things. Only one being has this nature, so a word that describes a nature functions as a proper name. "God" names what God is, not just what God does.

refashioned: Is the name "God" communicable — can it be shared with others? In one sense, yes: Scripture does apply the word "gods" to angels, judges, and others who participate in divine authority or excellence. But these are improper or metaphorical uses.
elegant: Scripture calls angels and judges "gods" -- the name is communicable in metaphor and by participation. But participation in divine power is not possession of the divine nature.

refashioned:
The name "God" is communicable in opinion (people have believed in many gods) and in metaphorical usage (Scripture calls powerful beings "gods") but not in reality. Only one being is God by nature. Others are called "gods" by participation — they share in some aspect of divine power, authority, or excellence — but the name belongs properly to the one being whose very essence is divinity.

Aquinas distinguishes this from names that are absolutely incommunicable. The name "God" signifies a nature — the divine nature — and natures are in principle communicable (many individuals can share human nature, for instance). But the divine nature happens to be such that only one individual can possess it (since God's essence is his existence, and subsistent existence is necessarily unique). So "God" is communicable in its mode of signification (it describes a nature) but incommunicable in the thing signified (the nature it describes belongs to only one being).

By contrast, if we combine the name "God" with a designation of uniqueness — "this God," "the God who alone has immortality" — it becomes absolutely incommunicable, referring uniquely to the one divine being.
elegant: The name "God" is communicable in opinion -- people have believed in many gods -- and in metaphorical usage, but not in reality. Only one being is God by nature. The divine nature is such that only one individual can possess it, since God's essence is his existence, and subsistent existence is necessarily unique. The name describes a nature that admits of no plural, though the grammar of the word does not prevent the attempt.

refashioned: The claim that an actually existing god shared among many beings is conceivable (even if false) distinguishes the name "God" from proper names that are incommunicable by definition. If someone believes in three gods, they're wrong about reality but not making a grammatical error. The name can be extended in thought even if it can't be extended in fact. This makes it different from something like a truly unique proper name, which by definition picks out only one thing.
elegant: Someone who believes in three gods is wrong about reality but not making a grammatical error. The name "God" can be extended in thought even where it cannot be extended in fact. This distinguishes it from a truly unique proper name. The word permits a plurality its referent does not.

refashioned:
When the name "God" is used of the true God and of false gods, is it used univocally (same meaning) or equivocally (different meaning)?

Neither purely. The answer, again, is analogy. When pagans call their deities "gods," they're using a name that bears a real but distorted relation to the true meaning. A false god is called "god" because people think it has what only the true God actually has — supreme power, governance over nature, worthiness of worship. The name is applied because of a supposed likeness to the true God, even though the likeness doesn't hold up.

This means the name isn't purely equivocal (which would mean the word means something totally unrelated in each case) but it isn't univocal either (which would mean the false gods truly share the divine nature). It's used analogically — based on a resemblance that the speaker believes exists, even if it doesn't.
elegant: When pagans call their deities "gods" and we call the true God "God," the word is used neither univocally nor equivocally but analogically. A false god is called "god" because people believe it has what only the true God actually has. The name is applied based on a supposed likeness -- real in the speaker's mind, false in fact. The connection between the uses is genuine but distorted.

refashioned:
The same analysis applies to names like "wise" used of God and creatures. The name isn't univocal because creaturely wisdom and divine wisdom don't share a common definition in the same way. But it isn't equivocal because there's a real causal connection: creaturely wisdom derives from divine wisdom and bears a genuine (if inadequate) resemblance to it.

The practical consequence: all our God-language is analogical. We speak truly but always with the caveat that our words reach toward a reality that exceeds their grasp. This is not a failure of language but a necessary feature of finite minds naming an infinite being.
elegant: All our God-language is analogical. We speak truly but always with the caveat that our words reach toward a reality that exceeds their grasp. This is not a failure of language. It is the necessary condition of finite minds naming an infinite being.

refashioned:
Is the name "He Who Is" (I AM — Exodus 3:14) the most proper name of God? Aquinas argues yes, on three grounds.

First, because of what it signifies. "He Who Is" designates God by his existence — by being itself. Since God's essence is his existence (Q3, Art. 4), no name captures the divine reality more directly. Every other name — "good," "wise," "powerful" — identifies God through some particular perfection. "He Who Is" identifies God through the most fundamental thing about him: that he is.

Second, because of its universality. Every other name narrows the divine reality to one aspect. "He Who Is" imposes no determination — it signifies the infinite ocean of being without channeling it into any particular shape. It's the most open and least restrictive name, and therefore the most appropriate for the most unlimited reality.

Third, because of what it connotes about time. "He Who Is" signifies being in the present tense, without past or future — being that simply is, without succession. This fits God's eternity perfectly.

The name God chose for himself — I AM WHO I AM — is the metaphysically perfect self-designation. It says: I am not this kind of being or that kind. I am being itself.
elegant: The most proper name of God is the one God chose for himself: I AM WHO I AM. Every other name -- "good," "wise," "powerful" -- channels the divine reality through a particular perfection. "He Who Is" names God through the most fundamental thing about him: that he is. It imposes no determination, signifies no limitation, restricts the infinite ocean of being to no particular shape. And it speaks in the eternal present tense -- being that simply is, without past or future. The metaphysically perfect self-designation: not this kind of being or that kind, but being itself.

refashioned:
Can we form affirmative propositions about God — "God is good," "God is wise" — or should all our statements about God be negative ("God is not evil," "God is not ignorant")?

We can and must make affirmative propositions. The reason is that our intellect, though it can't comprehend God, does make true contact with the divine reality. When we say "God is good," the composition of subject and predicate in our sentence doesn't imply that God is composite. The composition is in our mode of understanding, not in the thing understood.

Every human judgment takes the form of composing a subject with a predicate — that's just how finite intellects work. When we predicate "good" of God, we're not claiming that goodness is a separate component added to the divine substance. We're saying that the simple divine reality is truly (though not adequately) expressed by the concept "good."

Dionysius said that affirmations about God are "vague" — not false, but loose-fitting. They're true as far as they go but don't capture the divine reality with precision. Negations are more precise in what they exclude, but affirmations are necessary because they say something positive about what God is, not just what God isn't.
elegant: We can and must make affirmative statements about God. The composition in "God is good" -- subject plus predicate -- is a feature of how finite minds think, not a claim that God is composed of parts. Every human judgment takes this form; it is inescapable. Affirmations about God are, as Dionysius said, "vague" -- true as far as they go, but loose-fitting. Negations are more precise in what they exclude. But affirmations are necessary because they say something positive about what God is, and not just what he is not.

refashioned:
The objection that our affirmative statements must be false because they impose composition on the simple God is answered by distinguishing the mode of signification from the truth of what's signified. Every human statement about anything — even "Socrates is a man" — involves a compositional mode (subject + predicate). This doesn't mean the thing described is literally composed of the subject-thing and the predicate-thing. It's a feature of how we think, not of what we think about.

So our affirmative statements about God are true in what they assert but imperfect in how they assert it. The composition belongs to our grammar, not to God's nature. Aquinas is defending the possibility of genuine theological speech: we can say true things about God, even though our mode of saying them will always be creaturely and therefore inadequate.
elegant: The composition in our sentences belongs to our grammar, not to God's nature. "Socrates is a man" also involves a compositional mode -- subject plus predicate -- without implying that Socrates is assembled from a Socrates-part and a man-part. Our affirmative statements about God are true in what they assert and imperfect in how they assert it. This is the final word on theological language: we can say true things about God, knowing that our way of saying them will always bear the fingerprint of the creature that speaks.

refashioned: Having examined what God is in himself, we turn to what God does -- beginning with what happens inside God (knowing and willing) before addressing what flows outward (creating). Since understanding is a form of life, knowledge leads naturally to questions about divine life, truth, and the ideas in God's mind. Sixteen questions about divine knowledge follow: whether God knows, how he knows himself, whether he grasps other things, whether his knowledge is step-by-step or all-at-once, whether it causes things to exist, and how it relates to evil, individuals, infinity, the future, and change.
elegant: From what God is, we turn to what God does -- and the first thing God does is know. Knowledge, will, and power: the inner life before the outward act. Sixteen articles trace the reach and nature of a mind that never learns, never forgets, and never reasons its way to a conclusion.

refashioned:
God possesses knowledge in the highest possible degree. The key insight is that what separates knowing beings from non-knowing ones is range: a rock can only be a rock, but a mind can become, in a sense, anything it understands. The more immaterial something is, the wider its cognitive reach. Plants, locked in matter, know nothing. Animal senses, partially freed from matter, know something. The human intellect, further freed, knows more. God, who is utterly immaterial and purely actual, therefore knows most of all.

This does not mean knowledge in God works the way it works in us. In us, knowledge is a quality we acquire -- a habit moving from potential to actual. In God, there is no potentiality. His knowledge is not something added to his being; it is his being. And because knowledge adapts to the knower's mode of existence, divine knowledge does not split into universal and particular, or potential and actual, the way ours does. It operates on an entirely different plane.
elegant: What separates a mind from a stone is range. The stone can only be itself. The mind can, in a sense, become everything it knows. The freer a thing is from matter, the wider its cognitive reach -- sense grasps surfaces, intellect grasps essences, and God, entirely immaterial, grasps all. In him, knowledge is not an acquired habit but identical with his very being.

refashioned:
God knows himself perfectly, and he does so through himself -- not through some external representation. In any act of understanding, the object known must be present in the knower. When we understand something, our intellect is informed by an intelligible likeness of the thing. But this means our intellect starts in potentiality and is actualized by what it receives.

God has no potentiality. His intellect and its object are identical. He does not need to receive a likeness of himself because he already is the very intelligible content that would inform such understanding. His mind, the act of his mind, and the object of his mind are one and the same thing. So God knows himself through himself, without mediation.
elegant: God knows himself through himself -- not through any representation, likeness, or mirror. His intellect and its object are one and the same act. Where we must receive something foreign to begin understanding, God already is the very content that understanding would grasp. Knower, known, and knowing collapse into perfect identity.

refashioned:
The objection that a knower must 'return to itself' to know itself -- implying motion God cannot have -- rests on a metaphor. 'Returning to one's essence' simply means subsisting in oneself rather than depending on something external. God supremely subsists in himself, so he supremely 'returns to himself.'

The objection that understanding involves being moved or perfected by something else also fails. Our intellect is perfected by what it receives only because it starts in potentiality. God's intellect, which is never in potentiality, does not need to be perfected by any object. It is its own perfection.

Finally, the claim that we only know ourselves by first knowing other things reflects a limitation of our passive intellect, which must be activated by external objects before it can reflect. God, as pure act in both existence and intelligibility, needs no such activation.
elegant: The mind that must first look outward before it can look inward is a mind still partly in the dark. Our intellect discovers itself only after encountering other things. God, who is pure act and pure intelligibility, needs no such detour. He does not 'return to himself' by movement. He never left.

refashioned:
God comprehends himself fully. To comprehend something means to know it as perfectly as it can be known. A theorem is comprehended when you grasp its demonstration, not when you merely find it plausible. God's power to know is as great as his actuality in being -- both are infinite and identical. So he knows himself as completely as he is knowable, which is perfect comprehension.

This does not make God 'finite to himself.' Comprehension here does not mean containment by something external. It means nothing about him is hidden from his own knowledge. God is not bounded by his intellect as if his intellect were something separate; rather, his intellect and his being are one.
elegant: To comprehend is to know a thing as fully as it can be known -- not to contain it from outside, but to leave nothing in it hidden. God's power to know matches his actuality in being: both infinite, both identical. Nothing about God is opaque to God. He is not bounded by his own intellect; his intellect is his being.

refashioned:
God's act of understanding is identical with his substance. If it were something separate, then something other than God would be the perfection of God's nature -- his substance would stand to his intellect as potentiality to act, which is impossible in a being of pure actuality.

The logic is straightforward: understanding, unlike building or heating, does not pass outward to an external product. It stays in the knower as the knower's own perfection. Just as existence follows on form, understanding follows on the intelligible content present in the mind. In God, there is no form distinct from his existence, no intelligible content distinct from his essence. Therefore his understanding is his essence is his existence.

The result: in God, the knower, the known, the means of knowing, and the act of knowing are all one. When we say God understands, we are not adding multiplicity to his nature.
elegant: In God, the knower, the known, the means of knowing, and the act of knowing are not four things but one. Understanding is not something added to the divine substance -- it is the divine substance. Where we acquire knowledge as an accidental perfection, God's understanding simply is his existence. Say that God knows, and you have said nothing composite.

refashioned:
God necessarily knows things other than himself. He understands himself perfectly, and perfect self-knowledge includes knowing the full extent of one's own power. Since God's power extends to everything that exists or could exist -- he is the first cause of all things -- knowing his own power means knowing everything his power reaches.

Moreover, since God's existence is identical with his understanding, whatever effects pre-exist in him as their cause must exist in him in an intelligible way. Everything that is in another exists according to the mode of that in which it is. So all things exist in God according to the mode of understanding.

God does not know other things by looking outside himself. He sees them in himself, since his essence contains the likeness of everything. He knows himself through his own essence; he knows everything else through that same essence, because it is the template from which all things derive.
elegant: God sees all things, but not by looking outward. He sees them in himself, because to know his own power perfectly is to know everything that power can produce. His essence is the mirror in which all creation appears -- not as a foreign image impressed from outside, but as a consequence of perfect self-knowledge.

refashioned:
When Augustine says God 'sees nothing outside himself,' this does not mean God is ignorant of the external world. It means that what exists outside God is known by God within himself -- in his own essence, which contains the likeness of all things.

Nor does knowing other things make something external the 'perfection' of God's intellect. A stone is not literally in the soul; its image is. The things God knows are in his intellect through his essence, which already contains their likenesses. No external perfection is added.

And God's intellectual act is not specified by anything outside himself. It is specified by the primary object understood -- his own essence -- in which all other things are comprehended. So his act of understanding remains entirely self-determined.
elegant: Nothing external perfects God's intellect or specifies his act of knowing. The things he understands are in him through his essence, which already contains their likenesses. He does not go out to meet reality. Reality was already home.

refashioned:
Does God know things merely in general, or does he know each thing in its specific, individual distinctness?

Some argued that since God knows other things through himself as their universal cause, he can only know them in a general way -- as beings, not as the particular kinds of beings they are. Just as fire, if it knew itself, would know heat but not the specific things that happen to be hot.

But this cannot be right. Merely general knowledge is imperfect knowledge. Our own minds start with vague, general impressions and become more perfect as they grasp specifics.
