If God's knowledge of things beyond himself were only general, his intellect would be imperfect -- and so would his being, since in God these are identical.
elegant: To know things only in general is to know them imperfectly. Fire that knew itself would know heat -- but not the thousand distinct things that happen to be hot. If God's knowledge of creation stopped at 'beings in general,' his intellect would be less perfect than ours. And that cannot be.

refashioned:
God knows all things with proper, specific knowledge -- not just as beings in general, but as distinct from one another. The reason is that God's essence is not related to the essences of creatures the way a generic concept relates to its species (like 'animal' to 'horse' and 'human'). It is related as a perfect act to imperfect acts. Knowing the number six means you implicitly know three; knowing a human means you implicitly know what an animal is.

God's essence contains every perfection found in any creature, and more. Since each creature's proper nature is a particular degree of participation in divine perfection, God could not know his own essence perfectly unless he knew every way it can be participated in. He therefore knows each thing according to its own distinct nature.

Analogies like 'a center knowing all its radii' or 'light knowing all colors' capture part of this -- universal causality -- but miss the crucial point. Light does not cause the differences between colors; those come from the medium. But God causes not just what creatures share in common (being) but also what distinguishes them from one another. Every form, every specification, every individuating principle pre-exists in God's perfection. So he knows each thing specifically, not just generically.
elegant: God knows each thing in its own distinctness, not just as a specimen of 'being.' His essence relates to creatures not as genus to species but as perfect act to imperfect acts -- the way knowing six implicitly contains three. Every creature is a particular degree of participation in divine perfection, and God could not know himself perfectly without knowing every one of those degrees.

refashioned:
The objection that knowing things 'as they are in the knower' limits God to knowing things only in their divine mode misreads the phrase. The eye does not know a stone according to the stone's existence in the eye; through the image in the eye, it knows the stone as it exists in reality. God knows things in himself, but by that very fact knows them in their own natures -- and more perfectly than they know themselves.

The objection that the created essence cannot reveal the divine essence (so neither should the reverse hold) confuses the direction of the relationship. The imperfect cannot reveal the perfect, but the perfect can reveal the imperfect. The divine essence, as the unlimited perfection that all creatures partially imitate, can serve as the proper concept of each thing according to the specific way that thing participates in it.
elegant: The imperfect cannot reveal the perfect, but the perfect can reveal the imperfect. A creature's essence cannot disclose God, but God's essence can disclose every creature. The divine nature, as the unlimited source that all things partially imitate, serves as the proper concept of each thing according to the specific way it participates.

refashioned:
God's knowledge is not discursive -- he does not reason from one thing to another the way we do. Our thinking has two kinds of movement: sequential (first understanding one thing, then turning to another) and inferential (arriving at conclusions through premises).

Neither applies to God. He sees all things simultaneously in one act, because he sees them all in one thing -- himself. There is no sequence. And he sees effects in their cause without needing to derive them step by step, because he does not discover his effects; he already knows them by knowing himself as their source. Discursive reasoning is the movement from the known to the unknown. In God, nothing is unknown.
elegant: God does not reason. Reasoning is the movement from the known to the unknown, and in God nothing is unknown. He sees all things simultaneously in a single act -- effects in their cause, conclusions in their principles -- with no sequence, no inference, no 'therefore.' His knowledge has the structure of a single eternal glance, not a chain of arguments.

refashioned:
God's knowledge is the cause of things, not the other way around. For us, knowledge is caused by the objects we know -- reality shapes our understanding. But God's relationship to the world resembles an architect's relationship to a building: the design in the architect's mind causes the building, not vice versa.

However, knowledge alone does not produce things. An architect can conceive many houses he never builds. What makes knowledge productive is will. God knows all possibilities, but only those he wills to exist actually come into being. This is why divine creative knowledge is called 'knowledge of approbation' -- knowledge joined with will.

Natural things therefore stand midway between God's knowledge and ours. God's knowledge is prior to things and measures them (as the architect's blueprint measures the building). Things are prior to our knowledge and measure it (as the finished building informs the visitor's understanding).
elegant: Our knowledge is shaped by reality; God's knowledge shapes it. The architect's blueprint causes the building, not the other way around. But knowledge alone does not create -- it takes will to move from concept to existence. God knows all possibilities; only those he wills to exist actually come to be. Nature stands midway: measured by God's knowledge from above, measuring ours from below.

refashioned:
God knows things that do not actually exist. Whatever can be made, thought, or said -- by any creature, or by God himself -- is known to God, even if it never becomes actual.

There is, however, a distinction. Some non-actual things were actual in the past or will be in the future. God knows all of these through what is called 'knowledge of vision,' because his eternal present encompasses all of time simultaneously. Eternity does not move through moments; it comprehends them all at once. What is past or future to us is present to God's gaze.

Other things are merely possible -- within God's power or a creature's power, but never actually realized at any point in time. God knows these too, through what is called 'knowledge of simple intelligence.' He knows them as real possibilities, not as actualities.

Since God's knowledge joined with his will causes things, not everything God knows must exist. Only what he wills to be, or permits to be, actually occurs.
elegant: God knows what never was and what never will be. Past and future are present to eternity, which does not move through moments but comprehends them all at once. Things that existed or will exist he knows by vision -- seeing them as actually present. Things merely possible he knows by simple intelligence -- grasping them as real possibilities within his power. His knowledge extends beyond the borders of what he has chosen to create.

refashioned:
God knows evil, though evil has no positive reality of its own. Evil is the privation of good -- it exists only as an absence or deficiency in something that should be otherwise. Darkness is known through light, and evil is known through good. To know any good thing perfectly, you must know what could corrupt or diminish it. Since God knows all good things perfectly, he necessarily knows the evils that oppose them.

This does not mean evil exists in God or that God causes evil. He knows evil not through evil itself (as if evil were a positive content in his mind) but through the good things to which evil is opposed. And knowing something through its opposite is not imperfect knowledge when the thing in question has no independent reality of its own. Evil by its very nature is a privation -- it can only be defined and understood through the good it negates.
elegant: Evil has no face of its own; it is known only through the good it negates. Darkness is understood through light, sickness through health, privation through what ought to be there but is not. God knows all evil -- perfectly -- precisely because he knows all good perfectly. To know what could corrupt a thing, you must first know the thing in its wholeness.

refashioned:
God knows individual, singular things -- not just universals. Some argued that since immateriality is what makes knowledge possible, and since singular things are individuated by matter, an immaterial intellect like God's could only grasp universals. But this misunderstands the relationship between God's intellect and matter.

Our intellect knows only universals because it abstracts from material conditions -- it strips away individuality to reach the general concept. But God's intellect is not immaterial through abstraction. It is immaterial by its very nature, and as the cause of all things including matter, it is the source of both universal forms and individual instances.

Earlier attempts to explain how God knows individuals failed. Saying he knows them 'through universal causes' is insufficient -- universal causes do not determine what makes Socrates this particular man rather than another. Saying he 'applies' universal knowledge to particular cases just pushes the question back, since applying knowledge to a particular already presupposes knowing that particular.

The real answer: God's knowledge extends as far as his causality. Since his creative power reaches not just to forms (the source of universals) but to matter (the principle of individuation), his knowledge reaches to individuals. His essence, as the productive principle of all things, is sufficient to know everything he makes -- not just in general, but in full particularity.
elegant: God knows not just universals but individuals -- not just 'humanity' but this particular human being. His causality reaches to matter itself, the very principle that makes Socrates this man and not another. Where our intellect must abstract away individuality to grasp the general concept, God's intellect is the source of both the general form and its every particular instantiation.

refashioned:
Our intellect cannot grasp singulars because the intelligible content it works with is abstracted from individualizing conditions. But the intelligible content in God's intellect -- his own essence -- is not immaterial through abstraction. It is the source of everything that makes things what they are, including what makes them individual. So God knows both universals and singulars through his simple intellect.

And while matter in its bare potentiality seems utterly unlike God (who is pure act), even matter, insofar as it has being at all, retains some likeness to the divine being.
elegant: Our intellect cannot grasp the singular because it works by abstracting from material conditions. God's intellect is immaterial not through abstraction but by nature -- and as the cause of matter itself, it reaches what abstraction must leave behind. Even bare matter, insofar as it has being at all, retains some likeness to the one who gave it being.

refashioned:
God can know infinite things. Since he knows not only what is actual but what is possible -- for himself and for all creatures -- and since possibilities are infinite, his knowledge must extend to infinity.

The objection that infinity means 'always more to count' and therefore cannot be grasped is based on a model of sequential counting. God does not enumerate things one by one. He knows everything simultaneously, in a single act. The infinity that defeats step-by-step measurement does not defeat all-at-once comprehension.

The key is the mode of the knowing form. A sense image represents only one individual. A human concept represents a species and thus implicitly covers infinite possible individuals -- but only generically, not distinctly. God's essence, as the sufficient likeness of everything that is or can be, represents all things including their distinguishing features. So his knowledge extends to infinite things as individually distinct.
elegant: God knows infinite things -- not by counting them one by one (which would take forever) but by grasping them all at once in a single act. The infinity that defeats sequential enumeration does not defeat simultaneous comprehension. A sense image represents one individual; a human concept covers a species; God's essence represents all things, individually and distinctly, without limit.

refashioned:
The objection that infinity cannot be 'traversed' conflates traversal (sequential passage through parts) with comprehension (complete grasp). God does not traverse the infinite; he comprehends it, because nothing falls outside his knowledge.

The objection that knowledge must 'measure' what it knows, and infinity cannot be measured, confuses quantitative measurement with the knowledge of essence and truth. God's knowledge measures things not by counting them but by knowing their nature. Even an actually infinite number of things (were such to exist) would have determinate, finite natures -- and therefore be fully knowable to God.
elegant: To say the infinite cannot be 'traversed' is to confuse sequential passage with total comprehension. God does not walk through the infinite; he sees it whole. Even an actually infinite number of things would have determinate, finite natures -- and determinate natures are fully knowable to the mind that caused them.

refashioned:
God knows future contingent events -- things that could go either way and have not yet happened. This is one of the most contested points in philosophical theology, and Aquinas's answer turns on a careful distinction about perspective.

A contingent event can be considered in two ways. Considered in its cause, it is genuinely undetermined -- the cause could produce this outcome or that. No certain knowledge of it is possible from this vantage point; only conjecture. But considered as it actually occurs in itself, it is no longer contingent in that moment -- it is determined, and can be known with certainty. (When you see someone sitting, you know with certainty they are sitting, even though their sitting was a free act.)

God's knowledge is measured by eternity, which is not a very long stretch of time but a simultaneous whole that encompasses all of time. Everything that happens at any moment in time is present to God's eternal gaze as actually occurring. He sees contingent events not in their causes (where they would be uncertain) but in their actual occurrence (where they are determinate). He sees the whole timeline the way someone on a hilltop sees every traveler on the road at once, while the traveler on the road can only see what is in front of him.

So contingent events are genuinely contingent relative to their created causes. But they are infallibly known by God, because he sees them as present in their actuality.
elegant: God knows the future that has not yet decided itself. A contingent event, considered in its cause, is genuinely undetermined. But considered as it actually occurs, it is determinate and certain. God sees from eternity -- and eternity is not a long stretch of time but a vantage point outside time, from which the whole road is visible at once. What the traveler discovers around the bend, the one on the hilltop already sees.

refashioned:
The objection that a necessary cause must have a necessary effect misses the role of secondary causes. A seed's growth is contingent (it might fail) even though the sun that powers it is necessary. Likewise, things known by God may be contingent relative to their proximate causes, even though God's knowledge (the first cause) is necessary.

The deeper puzzle concerns the conditional statement: 'If God knew X would happen, then X will happen.' The antecedent is necessary (God's eternal knowledge does not change). Does the consequent therefore become necessary -- destroying all contingency?

The solution: when the antecedent involves an act of knowing, the consequent must be understood as it exists under that knowledge, not as it is in itself. 'If God knew X, then X will happen' is true where X is understood as present to God's eternal vision. As actually present, it necessarily is what it is (everything that is, while it is, necessarily is). But this does not make it absolutely necessary -- it remains contingent in its own nature and causes.

This parallels how something material (a stone) can be known immaterially -- the stone as known is immaterial, though the stone in itself remains material. Likewise, a contingent event as known by God is necessarily known, but as it exists in itself, it remains contingent.
elegant: That God knows a future event necessarily does not make the event itself necessary. A stone known immaterially does not thereby become immaterial. A contingent event known infallibly does not thereby become inevitable. The mode of the knowing does not dictate the mode of the thing known. Freedom is real in the world; certainty is real in God. Both hold simultaneously.

refashioned:
God knows propositions -- statements that predicate one thing of another -- even though his intellect does not compose and divide the way ours does. Our minds know things one feature at a time and must assemble them into judgments ('Socrates is wise'). God knows every feature of every thing simultaneously by grasping its full essence in a single act.

Just as God knows material things immaterially and composite things simply, he knows propositional truths non-propositionally. His one act of understanding his own essence suffices to represent all that can be true of any thing -- including every proposition that could be formed about it.
elegant: God knows every proposition -- every 'this is that' and 'this is not that' -- without composing or dividing in his mind as we must. We assemble truths piecemeal; God grasps the whole essence of each thing in a single act, and in that act every possible predication is already contained. He knows propositional truths non-propositionally.

refashioned:
God's knowledge does not change. Since his knowledge is identical with his substance, and his substance is utterly immutable, his knowledge must be equally unchanging.

The appearance of change -- 'God knew Christ would be born, but now does not know Christ will be born' -- dissolves once we recognize that God does not know propositional truths through sequential propositions the way we do. He knows in a single eternal act that Christ is born at a particular point in time. The proposition 'Christ will be born' was true before that time and false after, but God's knowledge of the event itself never shifts. He knows without change that the same event is future relative to one moment and past relative to another.
elegant: God's knowledge does not change, because his knowledge is his substance, and his substance cannot change. The appearance of change -- 'he knew it would rain; now he knows it has rained' -- dissolves once we see that God does not track events through sequential propositions. He knows in one eternal act that the same event is future to one moment and past to another. The timeline shifts; the gaze does not.

refashioned:
Relational terms like 'Lord' and 'Creator' change as creatures change -- God becomes 'Lord of Israel' when Israel exists. But knowledge and love are different: they denote relations as the objects stand in God, not as they are in themselves. Since created things exist in God invariably (even as they change in themselves), knowledge and love are predicated of God without variation.

The objection that God 'could know more than he knows' (since he could make things he has not made) is misplaced. God already knows everything he could make -- through his knowledge of simple intelligence. The scope of his knowledge does not expand or contract with the scope of his creation.
elegant: God already knows everything he could make, whether he makes it or not. The scope of his knowledge does not expand with creation or contract with destruction. Creatures come and go; the divine knowledge that comprehends their coming and going is as still as eternity itself.

refashioned:
God has both speculative and practical knowledge. Knowledge is speculative when it concerns what cannot be acted upon, when it considers an operable thing only in its general principles, or when it is not directed toward action. It is practical when it is ordered to operation.

God knows himself speculatively, since he is not something to be produced or acted upon. He knows things he has created (or could create) both speculatively -- grasping their natures and principles -- and practically, insofar as his knowledge directed their production. Things he can make but never does make, he knows speculatively but not practically. Even evil falls under his practical knowledge in a derivative sense, since he permits, directs, or counteracts it -- much as diseases fall under a physician's practical knowledge.

Since God knows all other things by knowing himself, his speculative self-knowledge contains within it both speculative and practical knowledge of everything else.
elegant: God's knowledge is both speculative and practical: speculative when he contemplates his own nature (which is not something to be produced), practical when his understanding directs creation into being. In knowing himself, he holds the blueprint of everything else. The theorist and the architect are one.

refashioned: Having examined God's knowledge, we now consider the ideas in God's mind -- the patterns or blueprints by which things are made and known. Three questions: whether such ideas exist, whether there are many of them, and whether there are ideas of everything God knows.
elegant: Before any creature exists, its form already lives in the mind of its maker. Three questions probe the divine ideas: whether they exist, whether they are many, and whether God holds an idea of everything he knows.

refashioned:
There must be ideas in the divine mind. An 'idea' is simply the form of a thing existing apart from the thing itself -- serving either as a blueprint for producing it, or as a principle for knowing it.

Anything produced by intelligence rather than chance requires a pre-existing plan. An architect must have the form of the house in mind before building it. Since the world was made by God acting through intellect (not by accident), there must be a form in the divine mind to whose likeness the world was made. That is what we mean by a divine idea.

This is not Plato's theory of self-subsisting Ideas floating in a separate realm. The ideas exist in God's intellect, not apart from it. And since God's essence is the productive principle of all things, the idea in God is ultimately identical with his essence -- not as God is in himself, but as his essence relates to and can be imitated by creatures.
elegant: The world was not made by accident. Whatever is made by intelligence requires a prior form in the maker's mind -- an idea. The architect must conceive the house before building it. Since God made the world through intellect, there must be in his mind a form to whose likeness creation was fashioned. These are not Platonic Forms floating in a separate realm. They are God's own essence, grasped as imitable by creatures.

refashioned:
Are there many ideas in God, or just one? Several objections push toward unity: God's essence is one, his wisdom is one, and multiplying ideas seems to multiply eternal things (which would make temporal creatures the cause of something eternal).

Against this, Augustine insists that each thing was created according to its own proper idea -- implying distinct ideas for distinct things.
elegant: If God's essence is one, how can there be many ideas? Must simplicity collapse into a single indistinct thought? Augustine insists otherwise: each thing was created according to its own proper idea.

refashioned:
There must be many ideas in God. The ultimate purpose of creation is not a single creature but the order of the whole universe -- and you cannot conceive a whole without conceiving its parts. A builder cannot design a house without ideas of its individual components.

This does not compromise divine simplicity. The many ideas are not many images informing God's intellect (which would introduce real multiplicity). They are many things understood by one intellect through one essence. God knows his own essence perfectly, which means he knows every way it can be participated in by creatures. Each distinct way of participating in the divine perfection corresponds to a distinct idea. The plurality is in what is understood, not in the medium of understanding.

The relations that multiply ideas are not real relations in God (those belong only to the Trinitarian persons). They are relations of reason -- the divine intellect comparing its own essence to the many things that can imitate it.
elegant: There must be many ideas, because the purpose of creation is not one creature but the order of the whole -- and you cannot conceive a whole without conceiving its parts. Yet the many ideas do not fracture divine simplicity. They are many things understood by one intellect through one essence. God knows every way his perfection can be imitated, and each distinct way of imitation is a distinct idea. The plurality is in what is known, not in the knower.

refashioned:
God's essence is called an 'idea' not as it is in itself but as it is the pattern of this or that particular thing. So there are many ideas because many patterns are understood through one essence.

The distinction between wisdom (by which God understands) and idea (what God understands) matters here. God understands many things by one act of wisdom. But he also understands that he understands them -- grasping the distinct patterns. It is this reflexive awareness that constitutes the plurality of ideas.
elegant: God's essence is called an 'idea' not as it is in itself but as it is the pattern of this particular thing. One essence, many patterns. One act of wisdom, many objects understood. The multiplicity of divine ideas is the divine intellect seeing all the ways its own infinite perfection can be finitely reflected.

refashioned:
Are there ideas of everything God knows? Ideas serve two roles: as exemplars (blueprints for making) and as types (principles for knowing). As exemplars, ideas extend to everything God actually makes. As types, they extend more broadly -- to everything God knows in its specific nature, including things that never come to exist.

Some limits apply. Evil has no idea in God, because evil is not a positive nature but a privation. God knows evil through the type of the good it negates. Primary matter has an idea only in conjunction with form, since matter cannot exist or be known by itself. Genera have no separate idea from species (since genera only exist in species). But individual things do fall under divine ideas, because God's providence extends to individuals, not just species -- contrary to what Plato supposed.
elegant: Ideas serve two roles: as blueprints for making and as principles for knowing. As blueprints, they cover everything God creates. As principles of knowledge, they extend further -- to everything God knows specifically, including what never comes to exist. Evil has no idea, since evil is a privation known through the good it negates. Individuals do have ideas, because God's providence reaches to the particular, not merely the species.

refashioned: Since knowledge concerns what is true, we now examine truth itself. Eight questions: where truth resides, how it relates to being and goodness, whether God is truth, whether truth is one or many, and whether it is eternal and immutable.
elegant: Knowledge pursues truth the way appetite pursues good. Eight articles examine where truth lives -- in things or in minds -- how it relates to being and goodness, whether God is truth itself, and whether truth is one, eternal, and unchangeable.

refashioned: Does truth reside in things or in the mind? Augustine offered definitions suggesting truth is in things ('That is true which is'). Aristotle placed it in the intellect ('The true and the false reside not in things, but in the intellect'). Which is right?
elegant: Is truth in things, or in the mind that grasps them? Augustine said 'that is true which is' -- placing truth in the world. Aristotle said 'the true and the false reside in the intellect' -- placing truth in the knower. The answer must hold both together.

refashioned:
Truth resides primarily in the intellect and secondarily in things. Here is the reasoning:

Goodness is the goal of appetite -- it resides in the desirable thing itself. Truth is the goal of intellect -- it resides in the knowing mind. Knowledge works by drawing the known into the knower, while appetite works by moving the desirer toward the desired. So the endpoint of knowledge (truth) is in the knower, just as the endpoint of desire (goodness) is in the thing desired.

But truth in the mind spills over onto things. A thing is called 'true' insofar as it conforms to an intellect on which it depends. Artifacts are 'true' when they match the designer's blueprint. Natural things are 'true' when they match their exemplar in the divine mind. 'A true stone' is one that fully possesses the nature proper to stone, as preconceived by God.

So truth is first and foremost the mind's conformity to reality. But things are called true by extension, insofar as they conform to the mind that designed them. The classic definition -- 'truth is the equation of thought and thing' -- captures both directions.
elegant: Truth lives primarily in the mind and secondarily in things. Good is in the thing desired; truth is in the mind that knows. But truth spills over from mind to world: a house is 'true' when it matches the architect's blueprint, a stone is 'true' when it fully possesses the nature God conceived for it. The classic formula -- truth is the equation of thought and thing -- captures both directions at once.

refashioned:
Augustine's definition ('that is true which is') describes the truth of things, not of intellect. The ancient philosophers, who denied that natural forms came from any designing intelligence, were forced to ground all truth in conformity to the human mind -- leading to relativism. But if we recognize that things are true primarily by conforming to the divine intellect, these problems dissolve.

The objection that 'a thing is true because it exists, so truth must be in things rather than in minds' confuses the cause of truth with truth itself. A thing's existence causes truth in the intellect, but that does not make the thing itself the primary seat of truth -- just as medicine causes health in the animal without health residing primarily in the medicine.
elegant: The ancient philosophers, denying any designing intelligence behind nature, were forced to ground truth in the human mind alone -- and thereby fell into relativism. But if things are true primarily by conforming to the divine intellect, the problem dissolves. A thing's existence causes truth in our minds, but that does not make the thing the primary seat of truth -- just as medicine causes health without health residing in the medicine.

refashioned:
Truth resides properly in the intellect's act of judging -- composing and dividing (affirming and denying) -- not in simple apprehension. Sight grasps a color, and it grasps it truly. The intellect grasps what a thing is, and grasps it truly. But neither the sense nor the intellect at that stage knows that it grasps truly. Truth as known -- truth as a conscious achievement -- requires the intellect to judge that reality matches its apprehension. This happens only when the intellect affirms or denies something about a subject.

So truth can be in the senses and in simple understanding the way truth is in any true thing. But truth as explicitly known and affirmed resides in the intellect's act of judgment.
elegant: Sight grasps a color truly. The intellect grasps an essence truly. But neither yet knows that it grasps truly. Truth as a conscious achievement -- truth recognized and affirmed -- requires judgment: the intellect comparing its apprehension with reality and pronouncing them matched. This happens only in the act of affirming or denying. Truth as possessed is everywhere; truth as known lives in judgment.

refashioned:
True and being are convertible -- they cover the same territory but differ in meaning. Everything that exists is in principle knowable, and everything knowable is in some sense true. Just as 'good' adds to being the notion of desirability, 'true' adds to being the notion of relation to an intellect.

Even non-being falls under truth in a derivative way: the intellect can make non-being an object of thought, and it is true that non-being is not. But truth is grounded in being, since what is not has no truth of its own apart from the mind that apprehends it.
elegant: True and being are convertible -- they cover the same territory but differ in meaning. Everything that exists is in principle knowable, and everything knowable is in some sense true. 'Good' adds to being the notion of desirability; 'true' adds the notion of relation to an intellect. Even non-being falls under truth derivatively: it is true that what is not, is not.

refashioned:
Truth is logically prior to goodness, for two reasons. First, truth relates to being more directly: truth concerns being simply and immediately, while goodness concerns being insofar as it is desirable (which adds a further qualification). Second, knowledge naturally precedes desire -- you must apprehend something before you can want it.

The sequence is: first being, then truth (being as related to intellect), then goodness (being as related to appetite). But in the order of things desired, good is the more universal category, since truth itself is a good of the intellect.
elegant: Truth is logically prior to goodness. Being comes first; then truth (being as related to intellect); then goodness (being as related to appetite). You must know something before you can want it. But in the order of desire, good is the more universal category -- for truth itself is a good of the intellect.

refashioned:
God is truth itself. Truth in its fullest sense is the conformity of an intellect to reality. In God, this conformity is absolute: his being is not merely conformed to his intellect -- it is the very act of his intellect. His understanding is the measure and cause of every other being and every other intellect. He is his own existence and his own act of understanding. So truth is not merely in him; he is truth itself, the sovereign and first truth.

Even the truth of our proposition 'someone sins' is ultimately from God -- insofar as all intellectual apprehension derives from him. But it does not follow that the sin itself is from God. That would be a fallacy of accident: the truth of the statement comes from God, but the act described in the statement does not.
elegant: God does not merely possess truth. He is truth. His being is not conformed to his intellect as if measured by some external standard -- his being is the very act of his intellect, the measure and cause of every other being and every other mind. In him, existence, understanding, and truth are one. He is the sovereign and first truth from which all other truths derive their name.

refashioned:
Is there one truth or many? Both, depending on what we mean.

If we are talking about truth as it exists in created intellects, there are many truths -- as many as there are acts of knowing. Just as one face produces many reflections in a mirror, the one divine truth is reflected in many created minds.

But if we are talking about truth as it exists in things -- the conformity of things to the divine intellect that designed them -- then all things are true by one primary truth. Each thing is true insofar as it conforms to its exemplar in God's mind, and there is only one divine mind.
elegant: One truth or many? Both. In created minds, there are as many truths as there are acts of knowing -- one divine face reflected in a thousand mirrors. But if we ask by what standard things are called true, there is only one: the divine intellect to whose design all things conform. Many reflections, one light.

refashioned:
Created truth is not eternal. Truth resides in intellects. If no intellect is eternal, no truth is eternal. Only the divine intellect is eternal, so only the truth in the divine intellect is eternal -- and that truth is God himself.

The truths of mathematics ('two plus three equals five') or geometry ('the nature of a circle') are eternal only as they exist in God's mind. The objection that 'it was always true that what exists now would exist' is answerable: that proposition was true only insofar as its future being was contained in an eternal cause, namely God.
elegant: No eternal intellect, no eternal truth. Since only God's intellect is eternal, only truth in his mind is eternal -- and that truth is God himself. The truths of mathematics exist forever not because numbers float in some Platonic heaven, but because they exist in the mind that never had a beginning.

refashioned:
Is truth immutable? Truth in the divine intellect is absolutely immutable, because God's intellect never changes and nothing escapes it. Truth in our intellects is mutable -- not because truth itself moves, but because our minds shift from truth to falsity. We change our opinions while reality stays the same, or reality changes while we keep our old opinions.

But the truth by which natural things are called true -- their conformity to the divine intellect -- is wholly immutable, since the divine standard never shifts.
elegant: Truth in the divine intellect is immutable -- God's mind never shifts from truth to falsity, and nothing escapes his knowledge. Truth in our intellects is mutable, not because truth itself moves, but because our minds do: we change our opinions while reality holds still, or reality changes while we cling to old judgments. The divine standard by which natural things are called true never wavers.

refashioned:
The proposition 'Socrates sits' is true with two kinds of truth simultaneously: the truth of the thing (it is a meaningful expression, as designed by the divine intellect) and the truth of signification (it accurately represents a fact). When Socrates stands up, the first truth remains (the proposition is still a well-formed expression) but the second changes (it no longer matches reality).

So truth does change in a sense -- not that truth disappears entirely, but that particular truths come and go as reality shifts. The sitting of Socrates is a different state of affairs before, during, and after -- and the truth of propositions about it varies accordingly.
elegant: 'Socrates sits' carries two truths at once: the truth of signification (it matches reality) and the truth of the expression (it is a well-formed proposition). When Socrates stands, the second truth remains but the first vanishes. Particular truths come and go as reality shifts. Only the truth by which all things are measured -- the divine truth -- stands outside the flux.

refashioned: We now examine falsity. Four questions: whether falsity exists in things, in the senses, in the intellect, and how true and false are opposed.
elegant: Where truth ends, falsity begins. Four articles ask: can things themselves be false, can the senses deceive, where does falsity lodge in the intellect, and how do truth and falsity stand opposed?

refashioned: Does falsity exist in things? Things cannot be false in relation to the divine intellect -- everything that happens proceeds from God's design (except voluntary deviations, which are sins -- and scripture calls these 'untruths'). But things can be called false relative to our intellect in two ways. First, by misrepresentation: we call something false when a word or thought misrepresents it. Second, by natural tendency to mislead: things that naturally produce false impressions are called false -- fool's gold, sugar-coated poison, deceptive appearances. This second kind is the more proper sense. We do not call everything false that resembles something else; only when the resemblance naturally tends to produce a false judgment in most cases.
elegant: Things cannot be false relative to the mind that made them -- everything proceeds from God's design. But relative to our minds, things can mislead: fool's gold, sugar-coated poison, the actor who resembles the hero. A thing is called false not because it lies (things have no voice) but because its appearance naturally begets a false impression. Falsity in things is borrowed from the gap between appearance and reality.

refashioned: Things do not actively deceive -- they only display their own nature. But they can occasion falsity by bearing a misleading likeness to other things. A thing is false not because it exists (insofar as it exists, it is true relative to the divine intellect) but because it falls short of what our intellect expects. An actor playing Hector is a true actor and a false Hector -- in things that are, there is always some degree of 'is not,' and in that gap, falsity finds its foothold.
elegant: Things do not deceive; they simply display their own nature. But where resemblance is misleading enough to produce false judgments in most observers, we call the thing false. An actor playing Hector is a true actor and a false Hector. In every 'is' there hides some 'is not,' and in that gap, falsity finds its foothold.

refashioned:
The senses can be false, but only in limited ways. A sense is never wrong about its proper object: sight does not err about color (except when the organ is impaired -- a sick tongue tastes sweet as bitter). But the senses do err about common sensibles (size, shape, motion) and incidental objects (identifying a figure in the distance as a particular person).

Falsity is more properly attributed to imagination than to the senses, since imagination can represent absent things as present -- producing illusions, dream experiences, and the like. The senses always report accurately what they receive; the error lies in how this information is interpreted and combined.
elegant: The senses, regarding their proper objects, are nearly infallible: sight does not err about color unless the organ is diseased. But about distance, size, and shape -- the common sensibles -- the senses regularly err. And about what a thing is (identifying a distant figure as a friend) they err most of all. Imagination goes further still, representing the absent as present, conjuring dreams and illusions. The senses always report what they receive; the error lies in how the report is read.

refashioned:
The intellect, like the senses, is infallible regarding its proper object: the essence or 'what-it-is' of a thing. You cannot be wrong about what a human being is while you genuinely grasp human nature. But the intellect can err in its judgments -- when it attributes to a thing something that does not belong to it, or denies something that does. This is the domain of composition and division (affirmation and denial), and here falsity properly resides.

Falsity can creep into even simple apprehension, but only accidentally -- when the intellect mingles judgment into its grasp of an essence, such as applying the wrong definition to a thing, or constructing a self-contradictory definition ('a rational four-footed animal'). Regarding genuinely simple essences grasped on their own terms, the intellect is either right or draws a blank.
elegant: The intellect, like the eye, cannot be wrong about its proper object: you cannot fail to know what a human being is while genuinely grasping human nature. But judgment is where the intellect is vulnerable. Affirm of a thing what does not belong to it, deny what does, and falsity enters. The intellect either grasps the simple essence truly, or draws a blank. It is in the business of 'this is that' and 'this is not that' where mistakes take root.

refashioned:
True and false are contraries, not mere contradictories (presence vs. absence). Falsity is not nothing -- it is a positive misapprehension, something asserted that does not match reality. Just as blindness is not merely 'not-seeing' but the absence of sight in a being that should see, falsity is not merely 'not-truth' but a distortion in a faculty that aims at truth.

Since true and good are universal and convertible with being, every falsity is founded in some truth (as every evil is founded in some good). A false opinion about God is still an opinion -- something real and true as an act of thought -- even though its content is false. In God himself there is no falsity, since his intellect cannot err. But in our apprehension of God, the false opinion is genuinely contrary to the true one.
elegant: True and false are not mere presence and absence. They are contraries -- like black and white, not like black and not-black. Falsity is not nothing; it is a positive misapprehension, something asserted that clashes with reality. Every false opinion is still real as an act of thought, just as every evil is founded in some good. In God there is no falsity. But in our apprehension of God, the false opinion stands genuinely contrary to the true one.

refashioned: Since understanding belongs to living beings, we now consider divine life. Four questions: what it means to live, what life is, whether life properly belongs to God, and whether all things are life in God.
elegant: Understanding belongs to living beings. So from divine knowledge we turn to divine life: what it means to live, what life is, whether life belongs to God, and whether all things are life in him.

refashioned:
Life belongs to things that move themselves. We recognize an animal as alive when it moves on its own; when it stops moving of its own accord, we pronounce it dead. Extending this principle: anything that determines itself to movement or operation of any kind -- whether physical motion, growth, sensation, or thought -- is properly called living. Things that cannot initiate their own operations are not alive, except metaphorically.

This includes 'movement' in a broad sense. Understanding and willing are not physical motions, but they are self-initiated activities of a being in act -- and they count as life at the highest level.
elegant: Life is self-movement. We call an animal alive when it moves on its own; when it stops, we call it dead. Extend the principle: whatever initiates its own activity -- growth, sensation, thought -- is alive. What cannot move itself is not. Understanding and willing are not physical motions, but they are self-initiated operations of a being in act, and they count as life at its highest.

refashioned: When Aristotle calls motion 'the life of natural bodies,' he speaks by analogy, not literally. Heavy objects falling and rivers flowing are moved by external principles (gravity, terrain), not by internal self-determination. Plants are the lowest genuinely living things -- they move themselves in growth and decay. 'Living waters' (flowing springs vs. stagnant ponds) merely look alive; the motion comes from an external source.
elegant: Rivers flow, rocks fall -- but neither moves itself. Their motion comes from outside: gravity, terrain, the push of an external force. 'Living waters' merely look alive. The lowest genuinely living things are plants, which grow from within. Everything below that threshold borrows the appearance of life without possessing the reality.

refashioned:
Life is not an operation but a mode of being. When we say something 'lives,' we mean it has a nature that can initiate its own operations -- not that it is currently performing any particular operation. 'Living' is an essential predicate, not an accidental one. A sleeping animal is still alive.

But we often use 'life' loosely for the operations themselves. We speak of the 'active life' versus the 'contemplative life,' the 'life of virtue,' the 'life of self-indulgence.' In this extended sense, whatever a person organizes their whole existence around becomes their 'life.' And this is how 'knowing God' can be called 'eternal life' -- it is the operation around which the ultimate mode of existence is organized.
elegant: Life is not an operation but a mode of being. A sleeping animal is still alive. 'Living' is an essential predicate, not an accidental one -- it names a nature capable of self-directed activity, not any particular act. Yet we loosely extend 'life' to the operations around which existence is organized: the contemplative life, the active life, the life of virtue. And so 'knowing God' can be called eternal life -- the operation around which the ultimate mode of existence is arranged.

refashioned:
Does life properly belong to God? The objection: life requires self-movement, and God is the 'unmoved mover.' So how can God live?

Another objection: life requires a principle of life (a soul), but God has no principle -- he is underived.
elegant: If life is self-movement, and God is the unmoved mover, how can God live? If life requires a principle of life, and God is underived, where is his soul?

refashioned:
Life belongs to God in the highest possible degree. The argument proceeds by degrees of self-determination:

Plants move themselves, but only in executing growth -- the form and goal of their movement are fixed by nature. Animals are more alive: they acquire the forms that guide their movement through sensation, giving them more self-determination. But animals still cannot set their own goals; instinct does that. Rational beings are more alive still: they can know the relationship between ends and means, set their own goals, and direct themselves accordingly. The intellect commands the senses, which command the body -- ascending layers of self-governance.

But even human intellects are not fully self-determining. Our first principles are given to us by nature, and our ultimate end (happiness) is willed necessarily, not chosen. We are partly moved by what we are.

God alone is wholly self-determining. His act of understanding is his very nature. He is not determined by anything external -- not by received principles, not by a nature he did not choose. His intellectual activity simply is his being. Therefore God has life in the most perfect and complete degree: entirely self-originating, entirely in act, entirely without dependence.
elegant: Life belongs to God in the highest possible degree. The argument proceeds by ascending degrees of self-determination. Plants execute movement but cannot choose its form. Animals acquire sensory forms but cannot set their own goals. Rational beings can set goals and direct themselves -- but even they receive their first principles from nature. God alone is entirely self-determining: his understanding is his nature, his activity is his being, nothing external sets his course. In him, the chain of borrowed determination terminates in pure origination.

refashioned:
The objection about movement confuses two kinds of action. Actions that pass outward (heating, cutting) perfect the thing acted upon. Actions that remain within the agent (understanding, willing) perfect the agent itself. When we say 'God moves himself,' we mean the second kind -- his understanding and willing are his own internal perfection, not a transition from potential to actual.

God has no principle of life because he is his own life. He does not need a soul to animate him, because his existence just is understanding, and understanding just is living. Life in our world requires vegetative powers because corruptible bodies need nourishment and reproduction. Incorruptible beings have no such requirement.
elegant: God has no principle of life because he is his own life. He does not need a soul to animate him, because his existence simply is understanding, and understanding simply is living. The 'movement' attributed to God is not the motion of something imperfect striving toward completion. It is the activity of what is already wholly in act -- understanding and willing as the agent's own internal perfection.

refashioned:
Are all things life in God? Since God's living just is his understanding, and since his intellect, its object, and its act are all identical, whatever exists in God as understood is the divine life itself. All things made by God exist in him as things understood. Therefore all things in God are the divine life.

This does not mean all things in God are movement. Things exist in God in two senses: as sustained by his power (in which sense they retain their own natures) and as known by him (in which sense they exist according to the mode of the divine essence, which is life).
elegant: All things made by God exist in him as things understood. And since God's understanding is his life, all things in God are the divine life itself. Not that stones and trees are literally alive in themselves -- but as known by God, they exist according to the mode of the divine essence, which is life. Everything real is, in the mind of its maker, a living thought.

refashioned:
Things as they exist in the divine mind have a nobler mode of being (uncreated, intelligible) than they have in themselves (created, material). But this does not mean the divine idea of a horse is more truly a horse than an actual horse -- because being a horse involves being material, which the idea is not. The idea has a higher being, but the concrete thing more fully realizes the specific nature.

Evil things, though known by God, are not 'life in God.' Evil is known through the types of good, not through its own positive presence in the divine mind. Things that never come to exist can be called life in God only insofar as 'life' means 'understood' -- they are grasped by the divine intellect -- but not insofar as life implies the principle of actual creation.
elegant: The idea of a horse in the divine mind has a higher being than any actual horse -- uncreated, intelligible, eternal. But it is not more truly a horse, because being a horse involves materiality, which the idea lacks. The concrete thing more fully realizes the specific nature; the divine thought more fully realizes being itself. And evil, though known by God, is not life in God -- it is grasped through the types of good, not through its own positive presence.

refashioned: We now turn from God's knowledge to God's will. Twelve questions follow: whether God has a will, what he wills, whether he wills necessarily, whether his will causes things, whether his will has a cause, whether it is always fulfilled, whether it changes, whether it imposes necessity on things, whether God wills evil, whether God has free will, and how the 'signs' of God's will relate to his actual will.
elegant: From what God knows, we turn to what God wills. Twelve questions probe the divine will: whether God has one, what he wills, whether he wills freely, whether his will causes things, whether anything causes his will, and how his will relates to necessity, evil, change, and freedom.

refashioned:
God has a will, because will follows naturally from intellect. Every natural thing tends toward its proper form and rests in it when possessed. In knowing beings, this tendency extends to things apprehended by the intellect: the mind grasps something as good, and the will inclines toward it. Wherever there is intellect, there is will. Since God has intellect, he has will. And since his intellect is identical with his being, so is his will.

God's will is not an appetite striving for what it lacks (which would imply imperfection). Will includes not just desire for what is absent but love and delight in what is possessed. God's will rests in his own goodness, which he already fully possesses. And since the object of God's will is his own goodness (which is his own essence), his will is not moved by anything external. It moves itself -- or rather, it simply is the divine being's orientation toward its own infinite good.
elegant: Will follows intellect as naturally as inclination follows form. Every knowing being, grasping something as good, inclines toward it. God, possessing intellect, possesses will. But his will is not an appetite striving for what it lacks -- it is love and delight in what it already fully possesses. The object of God's will is his own goodness, which is his own essence. Nothing external moves it. It moves itself -- or rather, it simply is the divine being's infinite rest in its own infinite good.

refashioned:
God wills things other than himself. Every being that is in act and possesses some good naturally tends to communicate that good to others, as far as possible. Fire spreads heat; every agent in act produces its likeness. Since God possesses goodness in the most perfect way, his will to communicate that goodness is strongest of all.

God wills himself as the end and other things as ordered to that end. He wills his own goodness for its own sake and wills that other things exist as participants in it. Other things are willed not as if God needs them -- his goodness is entirely self-sufficient -- but because it befits the divine goodness to be shared.

This does not introduce multiplicity into God's will. Just as his intellect is one while understanding many things through one essence, his will is one while willing many things through one goodness.
elegant: Perfection does not hoard; it overflows. Fire spreads heat. Every agent communicates its good. God, possessing goodness in its fullest measure, wills to share it. He wills himself as the end and other things as participants in that end. Not because he needs them -- his goodness is entirely self-sufficient -- but because it befits infinite goodness to be shared. One will, many things willed, through one goodness.

refashioned:
God necessarily wills his own goodness -- it is the proper object of his will, just as happiness is necessarily willed by us. But he does not necessarily will anything else.

The distinction is between absolute necessity and conditional necessity. A thing is absolutely necessary when denying it would be contradictory (like 'a whole is greater than its part'). God's will has a necessary relation to his own goodness: he cannot not will it. But things apart from God are not necessary for his goodness -- his perfection is complete without them. They are like a horse for a journey you could make on foot: useful but not indispensable.

So God wills other things freely, not out of necessity. However, once he does will something, it becomes conditionally necessary -- his will cannot change, so what he wills he cannot then un-will. His freedom lies in the initial willing, not in instability after the fact.
elegant: God necessarily wills his own goodness, just as we necessarily will happiness. But nothing else is necessary for that goodness -- his perfection is complete without creation. He wills other things freely, the way a traveler might choose a horse for a journey he could make on foot. Once he does will something, it becomes conditionally necessary: his will cannot change. But the freedom was in the willing, not in some later instability.

refashioned:
That God wills from eternity does not make his willing necessary -- only conditionally so (given that he wills it, it must be). That he necessarily wills his own goodness does not force him to will other things, because his goodness does not depend on them. That his nature includes whatever he wills does not make that willing necessary, because willing other things is voluntary, not contrary to his nature.

The deepest point: God's not necessarily willing something does not make his will imperfect or contingent. The deficiency, if any, lies in the thing willed (which does not require the divine goodness to depend on it), not in the divine will itself. And the will's self-determination does not require an external cause to tip the balance -- God's will, unlike a naturally indifferent cause, determines itself.
elegant: That God does not necessarily will something does not mean his will is deficient. The deficiency, if any, lies in the thing willed -- which does not compel the infinite goodness to depend on it. God's will determines itself without needing an external cause to tip the balance. This is not indifference but sovereignty: the will of a being whose goodness is already complete.

refashioned:
God's will is the cause of things. He does not create by necessity of nature (the way fire necessarily heats) but by intellect and will.

Three arguments establish this. First, the order of causes: natural agents must have their ends predetermined by some intelligence. The arrow flies toward the target because the archer aimed it. Since God is first in the order of causes, he must act by intellect and will, not by blind nature.

Second, the character of natural agents: a natural agent, being determinate in nature, always produces the same kind of effect. But God, whose being is infinite and undetermined, produces the vast diversity of creation. This requires will choosing among possibilities, not nature mechanically producing one outcome.

Third, the relation of effects to their cause: effects pre-exist in their cause according to the cause's mode of being. Since God's mode of being is intellectual, his effects pre-exist in him intellectually. They proceed from him through the inclination of will to actualize what his intellect conceives.
elegant: God creates by will, not by necessity of nature. Three proofs converge. The arrow needs an archer: natural agents need their ends set by intelligence, so the first cause must be intellectual and voluntary. Nature produces one kind of effect; God produces infinite diversity, which requires choice. And effects pre-exist in their cause according to the cause's mode -- God's mode is intellect, so his effects proceed through the inclination of will.

refashioned:
The objection that God acts 'by his essence, not by choice' (like the sun shining) confuses the issue. God's essence is his intellect and will. So acting from his essence means acting through intellect and will.

Augustine's statement 'Because God is good, we exist' does not mean God creates by nature rather than will. It means his goodness is the reason his will chose to create -- goodness is the motive, will is the mode.

Knowledge and will are not competing causes. In us too, knowledge directs and will commands the same act. In God, these are one.
elegant: God acts 'from his essence' -- but his essence is his intellect and will. To act from essence is therefore to act through intellect and will, not by blind mechanism. 'Because God is good, we exist' names the motive, not the mode. Goodness is the reason; will is the means.

refashioned:
Nothing external causes God's will. This does not mean God acts irrationally. He wills means for the sake of ends -- but since he grasps end and means in a single eternal act (not sequentially), willing the end does not cause willing the means. Instead, he wills the ordering of means to end. He wills this to be for the sake of that, but does not will this because of that.

The analogy is to understanding: if you grasp premises and conclusion simultaneously (seeing the conclusion in the premises), the understanding of the premises does not cause the understanding of the conclusion. You simply see that the premises entail the conclusion.
elegant: Nothing external causes God's will, yet God does not act irrationally. He wills the ordering of means to ends -- but since he grasps end and means in a single eternal act, willing the end does not cause willing the means. He wills this to be for the sake of that, but he does not will this because of that. The order is understood, not sequentially produced.

refashioned:
That God's will has no cause does not destroy natural science. God wills effects to proceed from definite causes, preserving the order of the universe. So it is perfectly rational to investigate secondary causes -- just mistaken to treat them as primary, independent of God's will.

Nor does it mean everything depends solely on divine whim. Only the first, most fundamental features of creation depend directly on God's will alone (why there are rational beings at all). Everything else follows from the ordering of causes that God willed into existence.
elegant: That God's will has no cause does not destroy natural science. God wills effects to flow from definite causes, preserving the order of the universe. Secondary causes are real and worth investigating -- just not ultimate. At the foundation of every causal chain stands the divine will, which chose to arrange the chain in the first place.

refashioned:
God's will is always fulfilled, because it is the universal cause of all things. A particular cause can be blocked by another particular cause -- but nothing can escape the order of the universal cause, under which all particular causes are included. Whatever seems to depart from God's will in one respect falls back into it in another: the sinner who defies God's moral will is caught by God's just will when punished.

The objection from Paul's 'God wills all to be saved' requires careful handling (see the reply below). The objection that God could will more good than exists confuses knowing all good with willing all good. The objection that secondary causes can block God's will only works for a first cause that is not truly universal -- and God's will is universal, encompassing all causes within it.
elegant: God's will is the universal cause, and nothing escapes the universal cause. A particular force can be blocked by another particular force -- but whatever falls outside one order falls back into the wider order that contains it. The sinner who defies God's moral will is caught by God's just will when punished. Nothing ultimately escapes.

refashioned:
'God wills all to be saved' can be understood in three ways: as applying only to those who are in fact saved; as applying to all classes of people (some from every group) but not every individual; or -- the most nuanced reading -- as expressing God's antecedent will versus his consequent will.

The antecedent/consequent distinction works like this: a judge, considered simply, wills all people to live. But when a particular person turns out to be a murderer, the judge wills his execution. The antecedent will considers things in the abstract; the consequent will considers all relevant circumstances. What God wills consequently (all things considered) always happens. What he wills antecedently (abstractly) may not.

This is like saying 'a merchant wants to keep his cargo' (antecedent) but 'throws it overboard in a storm' (consequent). The consequent will is the operative one.
elegant: A judge, considered simply, wills all people to live. When the defendant is a murderer, the judge wills execution. The antecedent will considers things in the abstract; the consequent will considers all circumstances. God antecedently wills all to be saved. Consequently, weighing all things, he does not. What God wills consequently always happens. What he wills antecedently may not. The merchant wants his cargo -- until the storm.

refashioned:
God's will is entirely unchangeable. Changing one's will requires either new information or a change in one's situation -- discovering that something previously thought bad is actually good, or finding that circumstances have shifted. Since God's knowledge is complete and his nature immutable, neither condition can arise.

The key distinction: willing that things change is not the same as changing one's will. You can decide in advance to build something and later tear it down -- one unchanging plan that includes both construction and destruction at their appointed times. God can will a thing for a season and its opposite for another season without his will ever shifting.
elegant: God's will never changes. Change of will requires either new information or a shift in circumstances, and God has complete knowledge and an immutable nature. The crucial distinction: willing that things change is not the same as changing one's will. A single unchanging plan can include building at one time and tearing down at another.

refashioned:
When scripture says 'God repented,' it speaks metaphorically -- by analogy with human behavior. A person who destroys what they made appears to repent. God destroyed humanity in the flood without any change of will, since that destruction was always part of his plan.

When God declares 'I will destroy this nation' and then relents when they repent, this is not a change of will either. God's consequent will always included the conditional: if they repent, they will be spared. He announced what would happen according to the secondary order of causes, but his higher will already encompassed the possibility of reprieve.
elegant: When Scripture says God 'repented,' it speaks as we speak -- by analogy with human behavior. The flood did not surprise God into regret. And when God declares destruction but relents when the people repent, this is not revision but the unfolding of a plan that always included the conditional: if they turn, they will be spared. The sentence changes; the counsel does not.

refashioned:
God's will imposes necessity on some things but not all. Some have tried to explain this through secondary causes: necessary causes produce necessary effects, contingent causes produce contingent effects. But this is insufficient. No secondary cause can ultimately block God's will, and the distribution of necessary versus contingent causes cannot be independent of God's intention.

The real explanation is God's own will. Because his will is perfectly efficacious, it determines not just what happens but how it happens. God wills some things to happen necessarily (the laws of mathematics, the motions of celestial bodies) and other things to happen contingently (free human choices). He achieves the latter by attaching contingent causes to contingent effects -- not because the contingent cause forces his hand, but because he designed the system to include contingency. The world contains both necessity and freedom because God willed it to contain both.
elegant: God's will imposes necessity on some things and not on others -- not because secondary causes force his hand, but because he designed some effects to happen necessarily and others contingently. His will is so perfectly efficacious that it determines not just what happens but how it happens. The universe contains both necessity and freedom because God willed it to contain both.

refashioned:
God does not will evil. Evil, as the privation of good, is never an object of appetite in itself -- it is only encountered as a side effect of pursuing some good. The lion wills food, not the death of the stag as such. The sinner wills pleasure, not the moral disorder as such.

God wills no good more than his own goodness. He therefore never wills the evil of sin, which is directly opposed to right ordering toward the divine good. But the evil of natural defect or punishment he does will, by willing the goods to which these evils are attached: he wills justice, and punishment is attached to justice; he wills the natural order, and natural decay is part of that order.

God neither wills evil to exist nor wills evil not to exist. He wills to permit evil -- and permission is itself a good, because it preserves the freedom and natural order that make the universe what it is.
elegant: God does not will evil. Evil is a privation -- the lion wills food, not the stag's death as such; the sinner wills pleasure, not the moral disorder as such. God never wills the evil of sin, which strikes at the ordering toward his own goodness. The evil of punishment he wills, because justice demands it. The evil of natural decay he wills, because the natural order includes it. And evil he neither wills to exist nor wills not to exist: he permits it, and permission is itself a good.

refashioned:
Some argued that while God does not will evil, he wills 'that evil should exist' because evil serves the greater good. But this is wrong. Evil is not ordered to good by its own nature -- only accidentally. The tyrant did not intend to showcase the martyrs' patience. Good outcomes from evil are beside the sinner's intention. So we cannot say God positively wills the existence of evil; he permits it, and good sometimes results from that permission incidentally.

The logical point: 'God wills evil to exist' and 'God wills evil not to exist' are both affirmative statements -- they are not contradictory opposites. The real situation is that God neither wills evil's existence nor wills its non-existence. He wills to permit it. That is the precise position.
elegant: Some say God wills evil's existence because evil serves the greater good. But evil serves good only accidentally -- beside the sinner's intention, beside the tyrant's design. The martyrs' patience was no gift of the persecutor. God does not will that evil exist. He does not will that evil not exist. He wills to permit it. That is the precise position -- and it is a good.

refashioned:
God has free will. Free will operates in the domain where necessity does not apply. God necessarily wills his own goodness (just as we necessarily will happiness), so there is no freedom there. But regarding everything else -- things he does not will by necessity -- God freely chooses. He can will a thing to exist or not exist, without either choice being compelled.

The objection that free will implies the ability to sin confuses freedom with defect. Freedom is the power to choose among options compatible with one's nature. Since evil contradicts the divine goodness by which God wills all things, willing evil is not a live option for God -- not because his freedom is limited, but because evil is not a genuine alternative for an infinitely good being. We ourselves can will to sit or not sit without sin; God can will a universe to exist or not exist without deficiency.
elegant: God has free will. He necessarily wills his own goodness (as we necessarily will happiness), so there is no freedom there. But regarding everything else, he freely chooses. The objection that freedom implies the ability to sin confuses freedom with defect. Freedom is the power to choose among genuine alternatives. Evil is not a genuine alternative for an infinitely good being -- not because his freedom is curtailed, but because evil is not an option that coherently exists for him. We can will to sit or not sit without sin; God can will a universe to exist or not exist without deficiency.

refashioned:
There is a difference between what God actually wills (the will of good pleasure) and what we call God's will by metaphor (the will of expression or sign). Just as punishment is called 'God's anger' not because God feels angry but because punishment is what anger produces in us, so precepts and prohibitions are called 'God's will' not because the precept itself is what God inwardly wills, but because issuing precepts is how we normally express our will.

This matters because scripture speaks of God 'willing' things that do not always come to pass (commands that are disobeyed). The will of good pleasure always succeeds. The will of expression indicates what God asks of us, but its fulfillment depends partly on creaturely response.
elegant: What God actually wills (the will of good pleasure) always comes to pass. What Scripture calls God's will by metaphor -- precepts, prohibitions, counsels -- may or may not be obeyed. A command expresses what God asks of us, not what he irresistibly decrees. The sign of will and the reality of will must not be confused.

refashioned:
Five signs of divine will are traditionally distinguished: operation (what God directly does), permission (what he allows without directly causing), precept (what he commands), prohibition (what he forbids), and counsel (what he recommends without commanding).

Operation and permission concern the present: God either acts or allows. Precept, prohibition, and counsel concern the future: God directs rational creatures toward good (through commands for what is necessary and counsel for what is beneficial) and away from evil (through prohibition).

A person reveals their will directly by acting or by not preventing action, and indirectly by directing others -- through binding commands, through forbidding, or through persuasive recommendation. The same framework applies, by analogy, to God.
elegant: Five signs reveal the divine will: operation (what God does), permission (what he allows), precept (what he commands), prohibition (what he forbids), and counsel (what he recommends). A person reveals their will by acting, by not preventing, and by directing others -- through binding orders, forbidding, or persuading. The same framework applies, by analogy, to God.

refashioned:
These five signs overlap: the same thing can be the subject of precept, operation, and counsel simultaneously. This is not redundant; it simply reflects different modes of manifesting the same underlying will.

The signs apply differently to different kinds of creatures. Rational creatures, who are masters of their own acts, receive precepts, counsels, and prohibitions. Other creatures, which act only as moved by God, fall under operation and permission alone. And evil is addressed by a single sign (prohibition) while good requires two (precept and counsel), because good admits of degrees -- what is minimally required versus what is optimally pursued.
elegant: The five signs overlap without redundancy. Rational creatures receive precepts, counsels, and prohibitions. Other creatures fall under operation and permission alone. Evil is addressed by a single sign -- prohibition -- while good requires two: what is minimally required (precept) and what is optimally pursued (counsel). Different modes of manifesting a single underlying will.

refashioned: We now consider what flows from God's will: first love, then justice and mercy. Four questions about divine love: whether love exists in God, whether he loves all things, whether he loves some more than others, and whether he loves better things more.
elegant: From will follows love -- the most fundamental movement of the will, the root from which desire, joy, and all other inclinations grow. Four questions: whether God loves, whether he loves all things, whether he loves some more than others, and whether better things are always loved more.

refashioned:
Love exists in God. Love is the most fundamental act of will -- more basic than joy, desire, or hope. You can only desire what you love, rejoice in what you love, or hate what opposes what you love. Love regards good universally, whether present or absent. Every other movement of the will presupposes it as root and origin.

Since God has will, he must have love. And it is love properly speaking, not merely metaphorical love. In us, love in the sensitive appetite comes with bodily changes (passion). Love in the intellective appetite (will) does not. God's love is of this latter kind: real love without passion. 'God rejoices by an operation that is one and simple,' as Aristotle says.
elegant: Love is the first act of will -- more basic than desire, more basic than joy, more basic than hope.
