elegant: 'The essence is common to three persons' does not entail 'the Father is common to three persons.' Real identity permits the statement 'paternity is the essence'; it does not permit switching whatever is true of one for whatever is true of the other. The same reality, viewed under different formalities, yields different truths.

refashioned:
Can the persons be predicated of the essential names? Can we say 'God is three persons' or 'God is the Trinity'?

Yes. The concrete term 'God' stands for the three persons (since each person is God), so 'God is the three persons' and 'God is the Trinity' are both true. What cannot be said is that the essence is three persons, because the abstract term 'essence' signifies the form, and the form is one, not three.
elegant: 'God is the Trinity' -- true, because the concrete term 'God' stands for the three persons. 'The essence is three persons' -- false, because the abstract term 'essence' signifies the one form, and one form is not three. Concrete terms open the door to Trinitarian predication. Abstract terms close it.

refashioned:
Can essential attributes be 'appropriated' to particular persons -- that is, can we assign power to the Father, wisdom to the Son, and goodness to the Holy Spirit, even though all three share all essential attributes equally?

Yes. Appropriation does not mean exclusive possession. It means using common attributes to illuminate personal properties. Power is appropriated to the Father because He is the unoriginate principle; wisdom to the Son because He proceeds as the Word (an intellectual emanation); goodness to the Holy Spirit because He proceeds as Love (and love's object is the good).

Appropriation helps us understand the persons by analogy. It does not divide the essence.
elegant: Appropriation assigns essential attributes to particular persons -- power to the Father, wisdom to the Son, goodness to the Spirit -- not because they possess them exclusively, but because the attributes illuminate the personal properties. Power resonates with unoriginate principle; wisdom with intellectual procession; love with volitional impulse. The bridges are real correspondences, not arbitrary labels. Appropriation does not divide the essence; it illuminates the persons.

refashioned:
Which attributes are appropriated to which person?

Augustine appropriated differently depending on context. To the Father: eternity, unity, and power. To the Son: beauty (or species), truth, and wisdom. To the Holy Spirit: goodness, joy (or use), and love.

The logic: The Father, as unoriginate principle, is fittingly associated with eternity (no beginning), unity (the source from which distinction arises), and power (the principle of action). The Son, proceeding as Word, is associated with beauty (the splendor of intelligible form), truth (the correspondence between concept and reality), and wisdom (knowledge in its fullness). The Holy Spirit, proceeding as Love, is associated with goodness (the object of love), joy (the rest of love in its object), and love itself.
elegant: Eternity, unity, power to the Father. Beauty, truth, wisdom to the Son. Goodness, joy, love to the Holy Spirit. The Father, as unoriginate source, is fittingly paired with eternity and the root of all distinction. The Son, as Word, with the splendor of intelligible form and the correspondence of concept to reality. The Spirit, as Love, with the good that love pursues and the rest love finds in possession.

refashioned:
Hilary offers a slightly different appropriation: eternity in the Father, beauty in the Image (the Son), and enjoyment in the Gift (the Holy Spirit). These map to the same logic from a different angle.

The point of appropriation is pedagogical. We cannot directly see the personal distinctions in God; they are known only by revelation. But by connecting essential attributes we can grasp (power, wisdom, love) to particular persons, we build conceptual bridges to the invisible relations. The bridges are real correspondences, not arbitrary assignments -- power really does resonate with the notion of an unoriginate principle, wisdom with an intellectual procession, love with a volitional one.
elegant: We cannot see the personal distinctions directly -- they are known only by revelation. But by connecting graspable attributes to invisible persons, we build conceptual bridges to the unseen. Power really does resonate with an unoriginate principle. Wisdom really does correspond to an intellectual emanation. Love really does mirror a volitional procession. The correspondences are not pedagogical fictions but ontological echoes.

refashioned: How do the persons relate to their properties? Four questions: (1) Are relation and person the same? (2) Are the persons distinguished by relations? (3) Do the relations remain if we mentally abstract them from the persons? (4) Do relations presuppose the personal acts, or vice versa?
elegant: The persons and their constitutive properties: identity, distinction, abstraction, and the logical order between who a person is and what a person does.

refashioned:
In God, each person is identical with the relation that constitutes it. The Father is paternity; the Son is filiation; the Holy Spirit is procession. This follows from divine simplicity: there can be no real composition in God, so the person cannot be one thing and the relation another.

But we need to distinguish personal relations (which constitute persons) from other notions. Innascibility is a notion of the Father but does not constitute Him as a person -- it merely negates origin. Common spiration belongs to both Father and Son and so cannot constitute either one individually. Only the three 'personal properties' -- paternity, filiation, and procession -- are subsisting relations that are the persons.
elegant: The Father is paternity. The Son is filiation. The Spirit is procession. In God, there can be no composition -- the person cannot be one thing and the relation another. The personal property does not belong to the person the way a coat belongs to a man. It is the person, subsisting. Only three relations constitute persons; the others (innascibility, common spiration) identify without constituting.

refashioned:
Some thinkers held that the persons are constituted not by relations but by their origins (the acts of generating and spirating). On this view, paternity is something the Father 'has' rather than something the Father 'is.' But this creates a problem: if the person exists before the relation, then the person is constituted by something absolute, and the distinction between persons would be absolute rather than relational. That leads either to a plurality of essences (Arianism) or to no real distinction at all.

The better view: the person and the relation are identical in reality, though we conceive them under different aspects. When we say 'the Father,' we think of the person. When we say 'paternity,' we think of the constitutive relation. Same reality, different conceptual angles.
elegant: If the person existed before the relation, the distinction would be absolute rather than relational -- and we would be back to Arius or Sabellius. The person and the relation are one reality, conceived under two aspects: 'Father' names the person; 'paternity' names the constitutive relation. Same reality, different angles of understanding.

refashioned:
Are the persons distinguished by relations of origin?

Yes, and only by relations of origin. In God there is no matter, no accidents, no composition of any kind. The only distinction available is relational -- specifically, the opposition between 'origin' and 'originated.' Paternity and filiation are opposed as principle and what proceeds from the principle. This relative opposition is what makes the Father and Son genuinely two persons sharing one essence.
elegant: The persons are distinguished solely by relations of origin. In God there is no matter, no accident, no composition. The only distinction available is the opposition between origin and originated -- the one from whom and the one who comes from. This minimal, relational distinction is all the Trinity needs and all it permits.

refashioned:
The distinction between persons cannot come from absolute properties (like wisdom or goodness), because these belong to the one essence and are shared equally. Nor can it come from anything extrinsic. It can only come from relations, and specifically from relations that involve opposition. Non-opposed relations (like common spiration, which is shared by Father and Son) do not distinguish the persons who share them.

So the entire Trinitarian structure stands on relational opposition: who originates from whom.
elegant: Absolute properties -- wisdom, goodness, power -- belong to the one shared essence and distinguish nothing. Non-opposed relations (common spiration) do not distinguish the persons who share them. Only opposed relations of origin create distinction. The entire structure of the Trinity rests on the simplest possible relational fact: who is from whom.

refashioned:
If we mentally strip away the personal relations, is there anything left of the persons?

If we abstract the relations entirely, nothing remains but the undifferentiated divine essence. There is no 'bare person' underneath the relation. The relation is what makes the person a distinct person.

But if we abstract the relations only from their role as relations (their reference to another) while retaining their role as constitutive principles, then we can still conceive the persons as distinct hypostases -- we just lose the explicit referential aspect.
elegant: Strip away the personal relations and nothing remains but the undifferentiated essence. There is no 'bare person' hiding beneath the relation. The relation does not clothe the person; it constitutes the person. Remove it, and the person dissolves into the ocean of the one divine nature.

refashioned: The distinction of persons depends entirely on the relations. Remove the relations, and you remove the persons -- what remains is simply the one divine essence. This confirms that persons in God are constituted by subsisting relations and nothing else.
elegant: Without relations, no persons. Without persons, only essence. This confirms what the entire treatise has been building: in God, person equals subsisting relation. There is no deeper substrate, no hidden foundation. Relation goes all the way down.

refashioned: Some objected that if the hypostases depend on relations, then divine hypostases are not 'absolute' the way created hypostases are. The answer is that in creatures, hypostases are distinguished by matter or individual features. In God, they are distinguished by relations. This does not make the divine hypostases less real -- it makes them differently constituted, as befits a nature that is absolutely simple.
elegant: In creatures, individuals are distinguished by matter. In God, by relation. This does not make divine persons less real than human persons -- it makes them differently constituted, as befits a nature that admits no composition. Simplicity does not weaken individuality. In God, it perfects it.

refashioned:
Which comes first in our understanding: the personal acts (generating, spirating) or the personal properties (paternity, spiration)?

Logically, the relation-as-constituting-the-person is prior to the notional act. The Father must be the Father before He can generate, since generation is an act of a person, and the person is constituted by paternity. But paternity as a relation-referring-to-the-Son is logically posterior to generation, since the reference to the Son presupposes the Son's existence, which comes from the act of generation.

So there is a logical order: (1) the Father is constituted as Father by paternity considered as a form, (2) the Father generates, (3) the Son comes to be, (4) and paternity and filiation refer to each other as correlatives. In reality, all of this is one eternal, simple act -- the ordering is purely in how we understand it.
elegant: Which comes first: the person or the act? The Father must be the Father before He generates, since generating is an act of a person. But paternity as a reference to the Son presupposes the Son, who comes from the act of generation. The logical order: (1) paternity constitutes the Father, (2) the Father generates, (3) the Son exists, (4) paternity and filiation face each other as correlatives. In reality, all of this is one eternal act. The ordering lives only in our understanding -- and understanding needs it.

refashioned: We now consider the persons in relation to the notional acts (generating, being generated, spirating, being spirated). Six questions: (1) Should notional acts be attributed to persons? (2) Are these acts necessary or voluntary? (3) Does a person proceed from nothing or from something? (4) Is there a power in God corresponding to these acts? (5) What does that power mean? (6) Can one notional act terminate in more than one person?
elegant: The notional acts -- generating, being generated, spirating, being spirated. Are they necessary or free? From something or from nothing? What power underlies them? And can one act produce more than one person?

refashioned:
Notional acts should be attributed to the persons. Wherever real distinction exists, we can identify what distinguishes. The divine persons are distinguished by relations of origin, and origin implies acts -- the Father generates, the Son is generated, Father and Son together spirate, the Holy Spirit is spirated. These are the notional acts.

The objection that every action in God reduces to the essence misses the point. Notional acts are not actions like creating (which is essential and common to all three persons). They are the internal acts by which the persons originate from one another. They do not add anything to the substance but express the relational distinctions that are the persons themselves.
elegant: Wherever real distinction exists, there must be something that distinguishes. The persons are distinguished by origin; origin implies acts. The Father generates. The Son is generated. Father and Son spirate. The Spirit is spirated. These notional acts are not actions like creating (which is essential and common). They are the internal acts by which persons originate from one another -- the living pulse of the Trinity's inner life.

refashioned:
Are the notional acts necessary or voluntary? In other words, does the Father generate the Son by choice, or by nature?

By nature -- not by an act of will that could have gone otherwise. If generation depended on the Father's free choice, the Son's existence would be contingent, and the Son would be a creature. But the Son shares the Father's nature fully and necessarily.

This does not mean the Father generates unwillingly. The will accompanies generation in the sense that the Father wills what He does by nature. But the will is not the principle of generation. Nature is. Just as God's self-knowledge is natural (not chosen), so the Word's procession from that self-knowledge is natural.
elegant: The Father generates the Son by nature, not by choice. If generation depended on the Father's free will, the Son's existence would be contingent -- and a contingent Son is a creature. The will accompanies the act: the Father wills what He does by nature. But will is not the principle. Nature is. The Son's existence is as necessary as the Father's own being.

refashioned:
What about the Holy Spirit -- does He proceed by will? Yes, in the sense that the Holy Spirit proceeds as Love, and love belongs to the will. But this does not make the procession voluntary in the sense of optional. The will here operates naturally (as nature inclines it), not freely (as deliberation directs it). Just as we naturally will our own happiness without choosing to, the Father and Son naturally spirate the Holy Spirit through their essential love.

The distinction between 'natural will' and 'free will' is critical. The Spirit's procession is from the will acting naturally. It is not contingent and could not have failed to occur.
elegant: The Holy Spirit proceeds from the will -- but not freely. The will here operates naturally, the way we naturally will our own happiness without deliberation. Natural will, not free will, is the principle. The Spirit's procession is as necessary as the Son's generation. There is nothing optional about the inner life of God.

refashioned:
Does a divine person proceed from nothing, or from something?

From something -- specifically, from the divine substance. The Son is 'from the substance of the Father,' meaning He receives the same divine nature by generation. He is not made from nothing (that would make Him a creature) nor from pre-existing matter (there is no matter in God).

The 'from' here indicates consubstantiality, not causation in the creaturely sense. The Son is from the Father's substance the way light is from the sun -- same nature, no diminishment, no separation.
elegant: A divine person does not proceed from nothing -- that would make Him a creature. He proceeds from the divine substance: the Son is begotten of the Father's substance, receiving the identical divine nature. Not from nothing, not from pre-existing matter, but from the fullness of the Father's own being. Light from light. God from God.

refashioned:
When we say the Son is begotten 'of the substance of the Father,' we mean the divine substance is communicated from Father to Son in generation. We do not mean a portion is detached or a new substance is created. The entire divine substance, undivided, is shared.

This is why the creed says 'God from God, Light from Light.' The preposition indicates origin of person, not division of essence.
elegant: 'Begotten of the substance of the Father' means the whole divine substance is communicated in generation -- not a portion detached, not a new substance created, but the entire undivided nature shared. 'God from God, Light from Light': the preposition marks origin of person, not division of essence.

refashioned: The same applies to the Holy Spirit. He proceeds from the substance of the Father and the Son. Though we do not say He is 'begotten from' their substance (since His procession is not generation), He does receive the same divine nature through spiration. Every divine procession is a communication of the one undivided substance.
elegant: The same holds for the Holy Spirit. He is not begotten (His procession is not generation), but He does receive the same divine nature through spiration. Every divine procession is a communication of the one undivided substance. Nothing in the Trinity is divided. Everything is shared.

refashioned:
Is there a power in God with respect to the notional acts? Can we say the Father has the 'power to generate'?

Yes. Power simply means the principle of an act. Since the Father really does generate, there must be in Him a principle of generation -- and that is what we mean by power. This power is not an added attribute but the divine essence itself, as it is the principle of the notional act.

The power to generate is the divine nature as possessed by the Father in the mode of paternity. It is the same essence that is the power of the Son to be generated and the power of Father and Son to spirate.
elegant: Power means the principle of an act. Since the Father really generates, there is in Him a principle of generation -- and this is what we call the power to generate. Not an added attribute but the divine nature itself, possessed by the Father in the mode of paternity. The same essence that is generative power in the Father is receptive power in the Son.

refashioned:
What exactly does the 'power to generate' signify? Does it signify the relation (paternity), the essence, or both?

Power signifies a principle of action. In God, the principle of generation is the divine nature as possessed by the Father. So the power to generate signifies the divine essence directly (as the root of the act) and the relation indirectly (as the mode in which the essence is possessed by the one who generates).

We cannot say the power to generate is paternity itself, because paternity is a relation, and a relation is not a principle of action in the strict sense. But we can say it is the divine nature as exercised by the Father.
elegant: The power to generate signifies the divine nature directly and the relation indirectly. The root of the act is the essence; the mode of its exercise is the relation. Power is not paternity itself -- a relation is not a principle of action. But it is the divine nature as exercised by one who stands in the relation of fatherhood.

refashioned: The Father and the Son have the same power (the same divine nature), but in different relational modes. The Father has the power as 'giving' -- He can generate. The Son has the same power as 'receiving' -- He is generated. This is why the Son cannot Himself generate a further Son: not because He lacks power, but because the same power exists in Him in the mode of filiation, not paternity.
elegant: Father and Son possess the same power -- the same divine nature. But the Father has it as giving; the Son has it as receiving. The Son cannot beget a further Son, not because His power is less, but because the same power exists in Him in the mode of filiation. The direction of the relation determines what the power can do, not the quantity of the power.

refashioned:
Can one notional act terminate in more than one person? Can the Father beget two Sons?

No. There is only one perfect act of divine generation, producing one perfect Word. The reason goes back to the simplicity of God's understanding: God understands all things by one act. Since the Word proceeds from this one act, there can be only one Word. If there were two sons, either the Father's intellectual act would be divided (impossible) or one of the sons would be redundant.

Similarly, there is only one procession of Love, producing one Holy Spirit. The perfection and simplicity of God's willing means there cannot be multiple proceeding Loves.
elegant: One act of understanding, one Word. One act of willing, one Love. The divine simplicity permits no multiplication. If there were two Sons, the Father's intellectual act would be divided -- or one Son would be superfluous. God's perfect fecundity is not expressed in proliferation but in singularity: one Word that says everything, one Love that embraces everything.

refashioned: We now compare the persons to one another in equality and likeness. Six questions: (1) Is there equality among the divine persons? (2) Is the person proceeding co-eternal with the principle? (3) Is there an order of nature? (4) Is the Son equal to the Father in greatness? (5) Is the Son in the Father and the Father in the Son? (6) Is the Son equal to the Father in power?
elegant: The persons compared: equality, co-eternity, order, magnitude, mutual indwelling, and power. Are the Three truly equal, or does origin imply hierarchy?

refashioned:
The divine persons are equal to one another. Equality means the negation of greater or less. If the persons were unequal, they would differ in their 'quantity' -- and quantity in God means nothing but the perfection of the divine nature. Unequal persons would have different natures, and three persons with different natures would not be one God.

Since the three persons share one and the same divine nature in its full perfection, none exceeds or falls short of another. They are equal.
elegant: The divine persons are equal. Inequality would mean they differ in the perfection of their nature -- and different natures would mean different gods. Since the three share one nature in its full perfection, none exceeds or falls short of another. Equal, not by external measurement, but by identity of being.

refashioned:
The relevant kind of equality is not about physical size (God has no body) but about 'virtual quantity' -- the perfection and power of a nature. This is measured by the form itself: in spiritual beings, 'greater' means 'more perfect.' Since the divine nature is infinitely perfect and is entirely shared by each person, none is greater or lesser.

Equality goes beyond mere likeness. Things can be alike while participating in a form to different degrees (a lukewarm and a hot thing are both warm, hence alike, but not equal in warmth). The divine persons do not merely share the divine nature to varying degrees -- they possess it identically and fully. So they are not only like but equal.
elegant: The equality is not approximate but absolute. Things can be alike while differing in degree -- a warm stone and a hot fire are both warm but unequal in warmth. The divine persons do not share divinity to varying degrees. Each possesses the whole of it, identically and completely. Beyond likeness: equality. Beyond equality: identity of nature.

refashioned:
Is the Son co-eternal with the Father? Arius listed twelve models of generation, each of which implied that what is generated is later than, or less than, its source. His conclusion: the Son came after the Father.

But every model Arius cited was drawn from imperfect, creaturely generation -- material change, temporal succession, inequality. None applies to God.
elegant: Arius marshaled twelve models of generation, each showing that what is generated is posterior to its source. Every model was drawn from imperfect, material, temporal generation. Every model fails in God, where generation involves no matter, no time, no transition from potency to act.

refashioned:
The Son is co-eternal with the Father. Three factors could make an effect posterior to its principle: the agent's free choice of timing, the agent's initial imperfection (needing time to mature), or the successive character of the action. None applies to divine generation.

The Father generates by nature, not by free choice, so there is no chosen delay. The Father's nature was always perfectly actual, so there is no growth toward readiness. And the act of generation is not successive -- it does not unfold in stages through time. It is instantaneous and eternal.

Therefore the Son has existed whenever the Father has existed. The Son is co-eternal with the Father, and so is the Holy Spirit with both.
elegant: The Son is co-eternal with the Father. Three things could make an effect posterior: the agent's choice of timing, the agent's immaturity, or the gradual nature of the action. None applies. The Father generates by nature, not by choice. His nature was always fully actual. The act is not successive. Therefore: wherever the Father exists, the Son exists. Co-eternal, without beginning, without delay.

refashioned:
No single creaturely analogy perfectly captures divine generation. We must gather insights from many: the coeternal nature of light and its splendor, the impassibility of a word born from the intellect, the consubstantiality implied by the name 'Son.' Of all analogies, the procession of the word from the intellect comes closest, since an intellectual word is not posterior to its source (except in an intellect that transitions from potentiality to act, which does not apply to God).

The Son is 'ever being born' and 'ever born' -- 'ever' captures eternity's permanence, 'born' captures the perfection of the generation. There was never a time when the Son was not. Arius's 'there was when He was not' is refuted by the eternal completeness of divine generation.
elegant: No single analogy captures divine generation. Light and its splendor show co-eternity. The word born from intellect shows impassibility. The name 'Son' shows consubstantiality. Of all images, the intellectual word comes closest: a concept is not posterior to the mind that forms it. 'Ever being born and ever born' -- eternity's permanence, generation's perfection. There was never a time when the Son was not.

refashioned:
Is there an order of nature among the divine persons?

Yes, but it is an order of origin, not of priority. 'Order of nature' here does not mean one person is prior in nature to another, but that one is from another. The Father is the principle of the Son, and the Father and Son are the principle of the Holy Spirit. This establishes an order: not 'before and after,' but 'from whom and toward whom.'

Augustine captures it precisely: 'Not whereby one is prior to another, but whereby one is from another.' The order is real -- it is not invented by our minds -- but it carries no implication of superiority, inferiority, or temporal sequence.
elegant: There is an order among the persons, but it is an order of origin, not of rank. 'Not whereby one is prior to another, but whereby one is from another.' The Father does not precede the Son as earlier precedes later. The Father is the source of the Son as a fountain is the source of a river -- not first in time, but first in the logic of flowing. An order without hierarchy. A sequence without succession.

refashioned:
Is the Son equal to the Father in greatness -- that is, in the perfection of the divine nature?

Yes. The very nature of filiation requires it. Paternity and filiation are defined by the fact that the offspring attains the same nature as the parent. In creatures, a child may start imperfect and grow toward equality, or a defect in generation may prevent full equality. But in God there is no potentiality, no growth, no defect. The Son receives the Father's nature fully and perfectly from all eternity.

As Hilary says: remove bodily weakness, the beginning of conception, the process of growth -- and every son, by natural generation, is the father's equal, because he possesses the same nature.
elegant: The Son is equal to the Father in greatness. The very logic of filiation demands it: sonship means receiving the parent's nature. In creatures, defects of generation can prevent full equality. In God, there is no potentiality, no growth, no defect. Remove bodily weakness and the process of maturation, and every son is the father's equal by natural right. In God, this right is perfectly fulfilled.

refashioned:
When Scripture says 'the Father is greater than I,' it speaks of Christ's human nature, in which He is less than the Father. In His divine nature, He is equal. Athanasius: 'Equal to the Father in His Godhead; less than the Father in humanity.'

The objection that the Father has paternity and the Son does not -- so the Son lacks something -- confuses substance with relation. Paternity is the Father's dignity, but it is the same essence that, in the Son, is filiation. The same dignity exists in both, under different relational modes. It is like the same road viewed from two directions: the road from Athens to Thebes and from Thebes to Athens is one road, but the journey differs in direction.

And though the Father has three notions while the Son has two, notions are not parts that add up. All the persons together are not greater than one, because the whole perfection of the divine nature exists in each.
elegant: 'The Father is greater than I' refers to Christ's human nature. In His divine nature, the Son is equal. The Father has paternity and the Son does not -- but paternity is not an addition to the essence; it is the same essence in a different relational mode. The same dignity, the same road, viewed from different directions. And though the Father has three notions and the Son two, notions are not parts that accumulate. Each person is the whole.

refashioned:
Are the Son in the Father and the Father in the Son?

Yes, in three ways. By essence: the Father communicates His own essence to the Son without change, so the Father's essence is in the Son, and therefore the Father Himself is in the Son. And since the Son is His own essence, He Himself is in the Father. By relation: each correlative implies the other in its very concept -- paternity implies filiation and vice versa, so each is conceptually 'in' the other. By origin: the Word proceeds from the intellect yet remains within the speaker; the Son goes forth from the Father yet remains within Him.

No creaturely model of 'being in' captures this perfectly. The closest is the way something exists in its originating principle -- except that in creatures, the originated and the origin differ in nature, while in God they do not.
elegant: The Father is in the Son and the Son is in the Father -- by essence (the Father communicates His own nature, so His essence dwells in the Son), by relation (each correlative implies the other in its very concept), and by origin (the Word proceeds from the intellect yet remains within the speaker). Three modes of mutual indwelling, each confirming the same truth: in the Trinity, to go forth is not to leave.

refashioned:
Is the Son equal to the Father in power?

Yes. Power follows from the perfection of nature. Since the Son possesses the same nature as the Father in its full perfection, He possesses the same power. Whatever the Father can do, the Son can do 'in like manner' (John 5:19).

But 'in like manner' does not mean 'in the same relational mode.' The same power that is 'begetting' in the Father is 'being begotten' in the Son. The Son cannot beget a Son -- not because He lacks power, but because the power exists in Him as filiation, not paternity. Generation is a relation, not an absolute capacity. So the Son has the same omnipotence as the Father, but exercised under a different relational aspect.

When Scripture says the Son 'cannot do anything of Himself,' it means the Son's power is derived from the Father by origin -- not that it is lesser. The Son acts of Himself (per se) but not from Himself (a se).
elegant: The Son is equal to the Father in power. Whatever the Father can do, the Son can do in like manner. But 'in like manner' does not mean 'in the same relational mode.' The same power that begets in the Father is received in the Son. The Son cannot beget -- not from weakness, but because the power exists in Him as filiation. Power is one; its relational expression differs. The Son acts of Himself but not from Himself.

refashioned: Finally, the mission of the divine persons -- how the persons are 'sent' into the world. Eight questions: (1) Can a divine person be sent? (2) Is mission eternal or temporal? (3) What is invisible mission? (4) Can the Father be sent? (5) Can the Son be sent invisibly? (6) Is invisible mission to all who have grace? (7) Is the Holy Spirit sent visibly? (8) Can a person send Himself?
elegant: The mission of the divine persons: how the eternal processions reach into time. Sending, indwelling, grace -- the Trinity's inner life overflowing into the world.

refashioned:
Can a divine person be 'sent'? Sending seems to imply inferiority (the sent is less than the sender), separation (what is sent departs from the sender), and a change of location (arriving somewhere new). None of these applies to God in the ordinary sense.

But mission in God means two things: a relation of origin (the one sent proceeds from the sender) and a new mode of presence (the one sent begins to exist somewhere in a new way). The Son is sent by the Father into the world not because He was absent before, but because He begins to be present in a new way -- through the Incarnation, or through the gift of grace.

This mission does not imply inferiority, because divine procession is according to equality of nature. It does not imply separation, because the Son does not cease to exist where He was. It does not imply local movement, because the divine person was already everywhere.
elegant: A divine person can be 'sent' -- not as an inferior dispatched by a superior, not as one who departs from where he was, but as one who begins to be present somewhere in a new way. The Son was always everywhere. In the Incarnation, He begins to exist in human nature. The Spirit was always sustaining all things. In the gift of grace, He begins to dwell in the soul as the beloved in the lover. Mission does not imply inferiority. It implies origin and arrival.

refashioned:
Mission is temporal, not eternal. The terms used for divine origins fall on a spectrum. Some express only the relation to the principle: 'procession,' 'going forth.' Some express the eternal term: 'generation,' 'spiration' -- these are exclusively eternal, because the terminus of the Son's generation is the divine nature, which is eternal. Others express a temporal term: 'mission' and 'giving' -- because to be sent or given implies a new mode of existence in a creature, and that is temporal.

So 'the Son proceeds from the Father' can be eternal. 'The Son is sent by the Father' is always temporal -- it refers to the visible mission of the Incarnation or the invisible mission of grace. Mission includes the eternal procession but adds a temporal effect.
elegant: Mission is temporal, not eternal. 'The Son proceeds from the Father' can be said of eternity. 'The Son is sent by the Father' is said of time -- because being sent implies a new mode of existence in a creature, and creatures live in time. Mission includes the eternal procession but adds a temporal term: a person arriving, a grace beginning, a presence newly felt.

refashioned:
The invisible mission of a divine person occurs through the gift of sanctifying grace.

God is present in all things by essence, power, and presence -- as the cause is present in every effect that participates in its goodness. But beyond this universal mode, there is a special presence in rational creatures who, by knowledge and love, actually attain to God Himself. When a person knows and loves God in the way that sanctifying grace makes possible, God is not merely the sustaining cause of their being but the object of their direct experience.

This special indwelling -- God present as the known is in the knower and the beloved in the lover -- occurs only through sanctifying grace. The divine person is 'sent' in the sense of beginning to dwell in someone in this new, intimate way.
elegant: God is present in all things by essence, power, and presence -- as the cause sustains every effect. But in rational creatures who know and love Him through sanctifying grace, He is present in a new way: as the known is in the knower, as the beloved is in the lover. This intimate indwelling -- not merely sustaining existence but being directly possessed and enjoyed -- is what the invisible mission accomplishes. The divine person is not far off, sending gifts from a distance. The divine person Himself arrives.

refashioned:
The divine person Himself is given, not merely His effects. Sanctifying grace enables the rational creature to possess and enjoy the divine person directly. The Spirit is not a distant cause whose gifts arrive like packages from afar; the Spirit Himself dwells in the soul through the gift of grace.

The gifts of grace (prophecy, miracles, tongues) manifest the Spirit's presence but are not the basis of the invisible mission. Only sanctifying grace constitutes the new mode of divine indwelling that we call 'being sent.'
elegant: What is given in the invisible mission is not merely an effect of the divine person but the divine person Himself. The Spirit does not send grace ahead while remaining behind. He dwells in the soul through grace. The gifts of prophecy and miracles manifest His presence; sanctifying grace constitutes it. The invisible mission is not the dispatch of a package. It is the arrival of a person.

refashioned:
Can the Father be sent? No. Mission requires procession from another, and the Father proceeds from no one. He is the unoriginate source. The Son can be sent (He proceeds from the Father). The Holy Spirit can be sent (He proceeds from both). The Father dwells in us by grace just as the Son and Spirit do, but He is not described as 'sent,' because there is no one from whom He comes.

The Father gives Himself freely to be enjoyed by the creature, but 'giving' in this context does not imply 'being sent,' which requires origin from another.
elegant: The Father cannot be sent, because mission requires origin from another, and the Father proceeds from no one. He dwells in us by grace, just as the Son and Spirit do. But He is not sent. He arrives without being dispatched. The unsent source, present everywhere, given freely but never proceeding -- because there is no one from whom He comes.

refashioned:
Can the Son be sent invisibly -- not just in the visible mission of the Incarnation, but through interior grace?

Yes. Both the Son and the Holy Spirit can be invisibly sent, because both dwell in the soul by grace and both proceed from another. The Father dwells in us too, but since He does not proceed from another, He is not sent.

The Son's invisible mission corresponds to the intellectual illumination that grace brings. The Spirit's invisible mission corresponds to the kindling of love. Scripture says: 'The Son is sent whenever He is known and perceived by anyone' -- that is, when someone receives the intellectual illumination of faith and wisdom through sanctifying grace.
elegant: The Son is sent invisibly, not only in the visible mission of the Incarnation but through the interior gift of grace. When someone receives the intellectual illumination of faith and wisdom through sanctifying grace, the Son -- the Word, the light of the Father's self-knowledge -- arrives in the soul. Both Son and Spirit can be invisibly sent, because both proceed from another and both dwell in the soul by grace. The Father dwells but is not sent.

refashioned:
Though all gifts of grace are attributed to the Holy Spirit (who is Love and the first Gift), some gifts are specifically appropriated to the Son -- those that belong to the intellect, since the Son proceeds as the Word. So the Son is sent according to intellectual illumination, and the Spirit is sent according to the kindling of charity.

But the Son's Word is not bare knowledge; it is 'knowledge with love.' And the Spirit's love is not blind; it flows from knowledge. So neither mission occurs without the other. Where the Son illuminates, the Spirit kindles; where the Spirit kindles, the Son illuminates. The two invisible missions are distinct in their formal character but inseparable in their occurrence.
elegant: The Son is sent according to intellectual illumination; the Spirit according to the kindling of love. But the Son's Word is not bare knowledge -- it is knowledge breathing love. And the Spirit's love is not blind -- it flows from knowledge. Where the Son illuminates, the Spirit kindles. Where the Spirit kindles, the Son illuminates. Two distinct missions, formally different, experientially inseparable.

refashioned:
Is the invisible mission directed to everyone who participates in grace?

Yes. The invisible mission occurs wherever there is both the indwelling of grace and a new beginning of that indwelling -- either the first reception of grace or a significant advance in it. This includes the Old Testament patriarchs and prophets (the Spirit was given to them, even if the visible signs came later at Pentecost).

The invisible mission occurs with every significant growth in grace -- not with every incremental increase (which would make mission continuous and trivial), but with proficiency in new acts or states: the fervor that leads to martyrdom, the grace to work miracles, the impulse to radical generosity. Even the blessed receive the invisible mission at the beginning of their beatitude, and it extends further through ever-deeper revelation of divine mysteries.
elegant: The invisible mission occurs wherever there is a new beginning of grace -- either the first reception or a significant advance. Not every incremental growth, but every threshold: the fervor that leads to martyrdom, the grace for miracles, the impulse toward radical generosity. Even the blessed receive it at the dawn of beatitude, and it extends further through ever-deeper revelations of divine mystery. The mission is not a single event but a pattern of arrivals, each one deeper than the last.

refashioned:
Is the Holy Spirit sent visibly? Yes -- but differently from the Son's visible mission.

The Son was sent visibly by assuming human nature into the unity of His person. The Incarnation was a permanent, personal union. The Holy Spirit's visible missions were different: the dove at Christ's baptism, the tongues of fire at Pentecost, the breath on the apostles. These were visible signs created for the occasion, not personal unions. The Holy Spirit did not become a dove or become fire.

Human nature requires being led to invisible truths through visible things. So God manifested the invisible missions through visible signs. The Son's visible mission showed Him as the author of sanctification; the Spirit's visible mission showed the Spirit as the sign and gift of sanctification.
elegant: The Son's visible mission was the Incarnation: human nature assumed into personal union, permanently. The Spirit's visible missions were different: the dove, the tongues of fire, the breath on the apostles. Created signs, not personal unions. The Spirit did not become a dove. But human nature requires visible things to grasp invisible truths, and so God manifested the invisible missions through outward signs -- the Son as author of sanctification, the Spirit as its gift.

refashioned:
Because the Son assumed human nature permanently, what is said of that nature can be said of the Son -- including being 'less than the Father.' The Holy Spirit, having not assumed any visible creature into personal union, cannot be called less than the Father on that basis.

The visible missions of the Spirit were not prophetic visions (which exist in the imagination) nor sacramental signs (which use pre-existing things symbolically). They were specially created visible phenomena that appeared, served their signifying purpose, and passed away.

The visible mission of the Spirit was not directed to all who received grace. It was directed to specific persons at foundational moments: Christ at His baptism (signifying His authority to give grace), the apostles at Pentecost (signifying their teaching office). The visible mission manifests the invisible one but is not required for every instance of it. The Old Testament fathers received the invisible mission of grace but not the visible mission of the Spirit, because the Son's visible mission had to come first -- the Spirit manifests the Son, as the Son manifests the Father.
elegant: The visible mission of the Spirit was not given to everyone who received grace but to specific persons at foundational moments -- Christ at His baptism, the apostles at Pentecost. The visible manifests the invisible but is not required for every instance of it. The Old Testament patriarchs received the invisible mission without the visible, because the visible mission of the Son had to come first. The Spirit manifests the Son, as the Son manifests the Father. The order of revelation follows the order of procession.

refashioned:
Can a divine person send Himself? In a sense, yes.

If 'sending' means designating the eternal principle from whom the person proceeds, then only the Father sends the Son, and only the Father and Son send the Spirit. The Son does not send Himself in this sense, because He does not proceed from Himself.

But if 'sending' means being the principle of the temporal effect that constitutes the mission -- the grace, the indwelling, the new mode of presence -- then the whole Trinity sends, because the whole Trinity causes the effects of grace. In this sense, the Son sends Himself and the Spirit sends Himself, because each person, together with the others, causes the grace through which the mission is accomplished.

This does not mean a human being can 'send' the Holy Spirit, because humans cannot cause the effect of sanctifying grace. Only God can do that.
elegant: Can a divine person send Himself? In one sense, no: sending names the principle from whom the person eternally proceeds, and no person proceeds from Himself. In another sense, yes: the whole Trinity causes the grace that constitutes the temporal mission, and each person is part of that whole Trinity. The Son sends Himself insofar as He, together with the Father and Spirit, causes the grace through which He comes to dwell. Eternal procession names the sender. Temporal causation includes all three. The missions originate in eternity and arrive in time -- and the arriving person is never absent from the act that sends Him.

refashioned: Having examined the procession of the divine persons, we now turn to how creatures proceed from God. This divides into three topics: the production of creatures, their distinction from one another, and their preservation and governance. On production, we ask about the first cause of beings, how creatures emanate from that cause, and whether creation has a temporal beginning. Four questions open this inquiry: Is God the efficient cause of all beings? Is primary matter created by God or independent of him? Is God the exemplar cause? And is God the final cause of all things?
elegant: Why does anything exist besides God? Here begins the inquiry into creation itself: its first cause, its manner, and its duration. Four opening questions press the point: Is God the maker of all that is? Is raw matter itself his work? Does he furnish the blueprint? And is he the purpose toward which everything strains?

refashioned:
Everything that exists in any way whatsoever is from God. Here is why. Whatever a thing has by participation must be caused by whatever has that trait essentially. Iron becomes hot through fire, which is heat by nature. God is self-subsistent being itself. There can only be one such being, just as there could only be one self-subsisting whiteness. Everything else, then, does not possess existence on its own but participates in it. And whatever participates in being must ultimately be caused by the one who possesses being most perfectly.

Plato made the same point: unity must precede multiplicity. Aristotle agreed: whatever is greatest in being and truth is the cause of all being and truth.

One might object that the relation of effect to cause is not part of a thing's definition, so things could exist uncaused. But the relation follows necessarily from what a thing is. Because a thing has being only by participation, it must be caused. An uncaused being is possible in principle, but only if that being exists through itself rather than by participation.

Another objection: necessary things cannot fail to exist, so they need no cause. But this confuses two issues. A cause is needed not merely because an effect might not exist, but because the effect depends on the cause for its existence. Even necessary truths in mathematics have causes, as Aristotle recognized.

As for mathematics not using efficient causes in its demonstrations: mathematics abstracts from matter and studies formal relations. That its proofs do not reference efficient causes does not mean its objects lack them. It simply means efficient causation is not the lens through which mathematics examines reality.
elegant: Everything that exists borrows its existence. Only one being possesses existence the way fire possesses heat: essentially, not by participation. Everything else is lit from that fire. Trace any chain of borrowed being far enough and you arrive at the one who does not borrow. If you exist at all, you exist because God does.

refashioned:
Primary matter itself is created by God. Earlier thinkers only considered how particular things emerge from particular causes and so assumed matter was a given starting point. But the question here is not about particular transformations. It is about the origin of being as such. Everything that exists in any way participates in being. Matter, as pure potentiality, has a real share in existence. Therefore it too must derive from the universal cause of all being.

The objection that matter has no prior subject from which to be made misses the point. Creation is not a change from one state to another. It is the bringing forth of being where there was nothing at all. The objection that God and matter are two co-equal principles, one active and one passive, also fails. Matter is not on the same level as God. Even matter's potentiality is a participation in being, and so it depends on God as its source.
elegant: Even raw matter, the barest capacity to become something, is not self-explanatory. It participates in being, however minimally. And whatever participates in being owes that participation to the source of being. There is no uncreated substratum waiting for God to shape it. The clay itself is his.

refashioned:
God is the exemplar cause of all things, and no other exemplar cause is needed. An exemplar cause is the pattern or model according to which something is made. Since God produces things through his intellect rather than by natural necessity, and since his intellect contains the ideas of all things, his essence serves as the model for everything created.

Plato was right that forms or patterns are needed to explain why things are produced in determinate ways. But these patterns do not need to exist as independent entities outside the divine mind. God's essence, understood as imitable in various degrees by various creatures, contains all the exemplar ideas. Each creature is modeled on God's understanding of how his perfection can be participated in a particular way.

The objection that creatures are too unlike God to have him as their model confuses the issue. An exemplar does not require exact resemblance. It requires that the effect be produced according to the pattern. Creatures fall short of God, but they are still made in accordance with the ideas in his mind.
elegant: God does not consult an external blueprint. His own essence, understood as imitable in endless degrees, contains the pattern for every possible creature. Plato was right that forms are needed. He was wrong that they exist apart from the divine mind. Every creature is a thought God has about how his perfection might be partially reflected.

refashioned:
God is the final cause, the ultimate purpose, of all things. Every agent acts for an end. God, as the first agent, acts for an end too. His end can only be his own goodness, since he needs nothing outside himself. But this means that everything God creates is directed toward his goodness as its ultimate purpose.

This does not mean God acts out of need. A generous agent can act for an end without lacking anything. God creates not to acquire goodness but to communicate it. Every creature, by its very existence and activity, tends toward some likeness of divine goodness. That is its purpose.

The objection that purpose implies need confuses the agent's end with the agent's deficiency. The objection that the end, form, and agent cannot be identical conflates the end of the process with the end of the agent. God's goodness is both why he creates and what creation ultimately reflects.
elegant: God creates not to acquire something he lacks but to share something he has. His goodness is the purpose of all things, not because he needs the world to complete him, but because generosity is the nature of the good. Everything that exists is an outpouring, not a requisition.

refashioned: We now consider how things emanate from God, which is called creation. Eight questions: What is creation? Can God create? Is creation something real in the creature? What kinds of things are created? Does creation belong to God alone? Is creation proper to any one divine person? Is there a trace of the Trinity in creatures? And is creation mixed into the works of nature and art?
elegant: How does anything come from God? Not by transformation of pre-existing material, but by the sheer production of being from nothing. Eight questions probe the nature, scope, and attribution of this most radical of acts.

refashioned:
To create is to make something from nothing. Not "nothing" as a material cause, as if something were fashioned out of nothingness the way a statue is carved from marble. Rather, creation means producing being absolutely, without any pre-existing subject. In ordinary change, something becomes something else. In creation, there is no prior something at all.

This distinguishes creation from every other kind of production. Every natural process presupposes material to work with. Creation does not. It is the bringing forth of the whole substance of a thing into existence.

The objection that the noblest action should go from being to being rather than from nothing to something reverses the logic. Creation is nobler precisely because it requires no presupposed material. It produces being itself, which is more fundamental than any transformation of what already exists.
elegant: Creation is not renovation. It is not the reshaping of old material into new form. It is the arrival of being where there was nothing to arrive from. Every other kind of making presupposes something to work with. Creation presupposes nothing at all. That is what makes it the most fundamental act conceivable.

refashioned:
God can create, meaning he can bring things into existence from nothing. The ancient axiom that "nothing comes from nothing" applied to particular agents working on pre-existing material. It does not apply to the universal cause of all being. A particular cause reshapes what already exists. The universal cause produces existence itself.

The further one traces the chain of causes back, the wider the scope of the effect and the less presupposed by the cause. A local artisan presupposes raw materials. A higher cause presupposes less. The highest cause presupposes nothing at all. That is what creation means.

Creation is also not a "change" in the strict sense. Change requires a subject that persists through the transition. In creation there is no persisting subject, because the whole being comes into existence at once.
elegant: The ancient dictum 'nothing comes from nothing' holds for every craftsman who must start with raw material. It does not hold for the source of raw material itself. The further back you trace the chain of making, the less the maker presupposes. The first maker presupposes nothing. That is what it means to create.

refashioned:
The ancient philosophers said "nothing comes from nothing" because they only considered particular causes producing particular effects, which always presuppose existing material. That principle does not apply to the universal origin of all things.

Creation is not change in the proper sense, since change requires a before and after in the same subject. But we can speak of creation as a kind of change in a loose sense: we can say the creature, after creation, exists in a way it did not before. This is a conceptual framework, not a claim about a temporal process.

As for the objection that creation requires infinite power because the distance between nothing and something is infinite: the "distance" between nonbeing and being is not a measurable gap. Creation does not cross a distance. It produces being where there was none.
elegant: Creation is not a journey across an infinite gap from non-being to being. There is no gap to cross. There is simply the sovereign bestowal of existence where none was before. The distance between nothing and something is not a measurable space but the sheer difference between absence and presence. Creation does not bridge that difference; it constitutes it.

refashioned:
Creation is something real in the creature, but it is not a change or a movement. It is the very dependence of the creature's being on God. Think of it this way: the creature's existence is a real relation of total dependence on its creator. This relation is not something added on top of the creature's existence. It is the creature's existence itself, understood as received from God.

The objection that creation cannot be in the creature because it would then be something temporal in God confuses active and passive creation. Actively, creation is God's eternal act. Passively, creation is the creature's real relation of dependence, which begins with the creature. The objection that creation must be a medium between Creator and creature is misleading. Creation is not a bridge between two pre-existing things. It is the very act by which the creature comes to be.
elegant: What is creation from the creature's side? Not a change, not a movement, but a relation: the total dependence of the creature's being on its maker. To be created is not to have undergone a process. It is to exist as something received. The creature's very being is, at its root, a gift held in the giver's hand.

refashioned:
Creation properly belongs to composite, subsisting things, not to abstract parts or forms considered in isolation. What is created is the whole being, the complete substance. We do not say that matter alone is created, or form alone, but that the entire composite thing is brought into existence.

This holds even for simple spiritual substances. Although they lack matter, they are still complete subsisting beings, and their whole being is produced by creation. The key criterion is that creation produces subsisting entities, things that exist in themselves rather than merely as aspects of something else.
elegant: What is created is not an abstraction but a whole, subsisting thing. Not matter alone, not form alone, but the complete entity that stands on its own in existence. Creation brings forth things that are, not parts waiting to become.

refashioned:
Creation belongs to God alone. No creature can create, because creation means producing being from nothing, which requires infinite power. Here is why: the greater the distance between a cause's starting point and its effect, the more power is required. Since in creation there is no starting point at all, no pre-existing material, the power required is beyond anything finite.

A creature can produce a likeness of itself through natural generation or through art, but it always works with pre-existing material. It reshapes what already exists. Only God, whose power is infinite and who is existence itself, can bring something into being from nothing.

The idea that God could delegate creative power to a creature, using it as an instrument, also fails. An instrument contributes something of its own to the effect. But in creation, there is no pre-existing material for an instrument to work on. An instrument that contributes nothing is not really an instrument.
elegant: No creature can create. Creation demands infinite power because it presupposes nothing. Every finite agent works with what is already there; it reshapes, it transforms, it combines. Only the infinite can produce being itself. The gap between making something out of something and making something out of nothing is the gap between finite power and infinite. No instrument can bridge it, because an instrument that contributes nothing is no instrument at all.

refashioned: When a creature produces something like itself, it does not produce the nature absolutely. It applies an existing nature to new material. A man does not create human nature. He transmits it to a new individual through generation, presupposing determinate matter. Similarly, every created being participates in existence rather than possessing it essentially. Only God, who is his own existence, can produce existence itself. Every other agent merely communicates a participated likeness within material that already exists.
elegant: When a creature produces something, it does not bestow existence. It transmits a form to matter that already exists. A father does not create his child's humanity; he passes on a nature he himself received. Only the one who is existence itself can give existence itself. Every other agent merely reshapes what has already been given.

refashioned:
Creation is not the exclusive act of any one divine person, but is common to the whole Trinity. Since the divine persons share one essence and one power, they share one creative act. However, the way we attribute creation can reflect the distinct personal properties: to the Father as origin, to the Son as exemplar (through whom all things are made), and to the Holy Spirit as governing love that directs creatures toward their good.

The processions of the divine persons are the cause of creation. The Son proceeds as the Word, containing the intellectual pattern of all creatures. The Spirit proceeds as Love, containing the impulse that gives creatures their relation to their end. But causation belongs to the divine essence, not to the relations alone, so creation is common to all three.
elegant: Creation is the work of one God in three persons. The Father creates as source, the Son as pattern, the Spirit as love directing all things toward their good. Three persons, one power, one act. The processions within God are the hidden root of every procession from God.

refashioned: The divine nature, though common to all three persons, belongs to them in a certain order: the Son receives it from the Father, the Spirit from both. The same holds for creative power. The Father creates as one who has creative power from no one else. The Son creates as one who receives that power from the Father along with the divine nature. The Spirit creates as receiving from both. This is why Scripture attributes creation to the Father as source, through the Son as pattern, and in the Spirit as sustaining love. But the act itself is one and undivided.
elegant: The Father holds creative power from no one. The Son receives it with the divine nature from the Father. The Spirit receives it from both. And so Scripture attributes creation to the Father as origin, through the Son as wisdom, in the Spirit as sustaining love. One act, indivisibly shared, personally inflected.

refashioned:
Every creature bears a trace of the Trinity. A trace is not a full image, the way the rational mind images the Trinity, but a mark left by the cause in its effect. Every creature has three features that point back to the Trinity: it has a determinate existence (pointing to the Father as origin), a determinate form or species (pointing to the Son as the Word and exemplar), and a determinate relation to other things and to its own good (pointing to the Holy Spirit as love).

This is not a proof of the Trinity from creation. The Trinity can only be known through revelation. But once the Trinity is known, we can recognize its vestiges in the structure of every created thing. The threefold character of creatures, their being, their form, and their order, reflects, however faintly, the threefold character of their source.
elegant: Every creature bears a fingerprint of the Trinity. Not a portrait, but a trace: it has being, pointing to the Father; form, pointing to the Son; and purpose, pointing to the Spirit. The doctrine cannot be discovered by reading creation. But once revealed, it can be recognized everywhere.

refashioned:
Creation is not mixed into the ordinary works of nature or art. When nature produces a new form in existing matter, the form is not created from nothing. It is educed from the potentiality of the matter by the action of a natural agent. The substantial form does not come into being on its own. It comes into being as part of the composite, and the composite comes into being through natural transformation, not creation.

The same applies to art. An artist does not create the form of the artwork from nothing. The artist imposes form on pre-existing material through skill and effort. In every case, natural and artificial production presuppose existing matter and work by transforming it. Creation, by contrast, produces the whole substance. The two are fundamentally different kinds of causation.
elegant: Nature does not create. Art does not create. Both work on what is already there, drawing new forms from existing potential. Creation alone produces the whole substance from nothing. When a seed becomes a tree or marble becomes a statue, something changes. When God creates, something simply begins to be.

refashioned: We now consider whether the created world had a beginning. Three questions: Did the universe always exist? Is its temporal beginning an article of faith? And what does it mean that God created heaven and earth "in the beginning"?
elegant: Did the world always exist, or did time itself have a birthday? Three questions probe whether eternity belongs to God alone or extends to his creation.

refashioned:
The universe has not always existed. Only God can be eternal. God's will is the cause of all things, and God is not compelled to will that the world exist from eternity. He freely wills the world into existence at whatever duration he chooses.

Several arguments for an eternal world fail. The claim that what can exist must have always been possible, and that possibility requires pre-existing matter, confuses active and passive potentiality. Before the world existed, it was possible by God's active power, not by some pre-existing matter. The claim that anything with the power to exist forever must have always existed conflates having that power forever with having existed forever. A thing receives its power to endure when it is created, not before.
elegant: Only God can be eternal. The world exists because God wills it, and God was not compelled to will it from eternity. Before creation there was no 'before,' because time itself is a creature. The world began not within a pre-existing timeline but at the threshold where time itself opened its eyes.

refashioned: Nothing except God can be eternal. God's will is the cause of things, and it is not necessary for God to will that the world always exist. The world exists for as long as God wills it. Since God freely chose a finite duration for the world, the world has a beginning. Its existence is entirely contingent on the divine will, which is under no necessity to produce an everlasting cosmos.
elegant: The world lasts as long as God wills it. Its duration is a gift, not an entitlement. Nothing in the nature of things demands eternal existence. The cosmos is contingent through and through: it hangs, moment by moment, on the free decision of its maker.

refashioned:
Before the world existed, its possibility rested on God's active power, not on any pre-existing material. The objection that things capable of perpetual existence must have always existed fails: a thing receives its capacity for endurance at the moment of its creation. It does not follow that it lacked a beginning.

The argument that God's active power is eternal, and therefore its effect should be eternal, also fails. An agent that acts by will, not by natural necessity, can produce an effect whenever it chooses. A voluntary agent is not compelled to produce its effect the instant it has the power to do so. God freely determined both that the world should exist and when it should begin.

As for the objection that God's action must be eternal because God is eternal: God's action is identical with his being, which is indeed eternal. But the effect of that action begins when God wills it to begin. The eternal act produces a temporal result by free choice.
elegant: An eternal cause need not produce an eternal effect. A voluntary agent acts when it chooses, not when its power happens to be ready. God's creative power is eternal, but his creative will is free. He chose a beginning, and that choice is the reason time has one.

refashioned:
That the world had a beginning is an article of faith, not a conclusion that can be demonstrated by reason alone. We can prove that God is the efficient cause of the world. But we cannot prove by reason that God did not will the world to exist from eternity. The world's temporal beginning depends on God's free choice, and free choices cannot be deduced from necessary principles.

The arguments claiming to demonstrate a temporal beginning all have flaws. Some argue that an infinite series of past events is impossible. But this confuses different kinds of infinity. An infinite past series is not simultaneously existing; each moment passes away. Others argue that an actually infinite number of souls would exist if the world were eternal, but this depends on contested premises about the soul's relation to time.

The world's beginning is known by faith, through revelation. Reason can show it is not impossible for the world to have begun, but reason cannot prove it did.
elegant: That the world began is known by faith, not by demonstration. Reason can prove God made the world. It cannot prove he did not make it from eternity. The world's temporal beginning depends on a free divine choice, and free choices cannot be deduced from necessary principles. This is where philosophy reaches its limit and revelation picks up.

refashioned:
Some philosophers held that the world is eternal but still created by God. They meant that the world has a beginning of creation but not a beginning of time, that God always sustained it in existence. Augustine compared this to a foot eternally planted in dust: the footprint would be eternal too, yet still caused by the foot. This view is logically coherent but incorrect according to faith.

The arguments purporting to demonstrate a beginning of time are not airtight. They show that a beginning is possible, even fitting, but not that it is necessary. The strongest ground for affirming a temporal beginning remains revelation, not philosophical demonstration. Those who attempted rational proofs actually weakened the case for faith by offering arguments that opponents could easily rebut.
elegant: A world eternally sustained by God would still be a created world, just as a footprint eternally imprinted in dust would still be caused by the foot. Logical coherence is not the same as truth. The world could have been eternal in principle. That it was not is a fact we know by faith, not a conclusion we can force from reason.

refashioned:
Creation was in the beginning of time. Time itself began with creation. There was no pre-existing time into which the world was placed. Rather, the first moment of the world's existence was the first moment of time.

The objection that creation is not in time because substance as such is not measured by time misses the point. While the substance of things is not directly temporal, the beginning of existence is. The phrase "in the beginning" refers to the first instant in which things came to be, the point at which time itself commenced.
elegant: Time did not precede the world. The world did not enter a pre-existing timeline. Time and the world were born together. The first moment of creation was the first moment of time. There is no 'before' creation, because 'before' is a temporal word, and time is itself a creature.

refashioned: Having considered the production of creatures, we turn to their distinction. Why are things different from one another? Three questions: Does the multitude and variety of things come from God? Does the inequality of things come from God? And is there only one world?
elegant: Why is the world various rather than uniform? Three questions ask why things differ, why they are unequal, and whether there is one world or many.

refashioned:
The multitude and distinction of things comes from God's intention, not from chance, natural necessity, or any secondary cause. God is a voluntary agent who acts through intellect. Unlike a natural agent that can only produce one kind of effect, an intellectual agent can intend many different effects. God created diverse things because no single creature can adequately represent his goodness. The whole universe, with its variety, expresses what no individual creature could.

The Manichean view that distinction arises from opposing good and evil principles fails because distinction is not a defect. It is a perfection of the whole. Other views, that diversity comes from secondary causes or matter, also fail. They reduce the origin of the world's richness to accident rather than design. The variety of creation is not a byproduct. It is the point.
elegant: The variety of creation is not an accident. It is the point. No single creature can express God's goodness. It takes an entire universe of diverse beings, each reflecting a different facet of infinite perfection, to approximate what no finite thing could capture alone. Multiplicity is not a compromise. It is the design.

refashioned:
A natural agent, acting by a single form, produces only one kind of effect. But a voluntary agent acting through intellect can produce many different effects. Since God is a voluntary agent, his unity does not prevent him from making diverse things.

Nor does the fact that God is the exemplar of creation require that creation be uniform. A single exemplar can be imitated in multiple ways, at varying degrees of likeness. The diversity of creatures represents the many ways God's goodness can be participated.
elegant: Unity in the cause does not require uniformity in the effect. A single sun paints a thousand different shadows. God's oneness is the source, not the limit, of creation's diversity. His goodness can be imitated in countless ways, and so it is.

refashioned:
The inequality of things is from God, by intention. A universe of unequal beings is better than one in which everything is equal, because unequal parts form a more complete whole. An organism made entirely of eyes would be no organism at all. Similarly, the universe achieves its perfection through a hierarchy of beings, from the simplest to the most complex, each contributing something the others cannot.

The objection that the best agent should produce the best possible individual things confuses part with whole. God produces the best possible whole. That requires different grades of perfection. The inequality of creatures is not a defect in God's goodness but its fullest expression.

The further objection that inequality must come from merit or demerit rather than from God directly gets the order backward. Inequality of nature precedes any inequality of action. Things must first be different before they can act differently.
elegant: A universe of equals would be an impoverished universe. An organism made entirely of eyes would be no organism at all. Hierarchy is not oppression. It is how a whole becomes greater than any collection of identical parts. Each grade of being contributes what no other grade can, and the whole is richer for the inequality.

refashioned: The best agent produces the best total effect, not the best individual part. In an organism, removing inequality would destroy the whole. Each part has its goodness in proportion to the whole. God made the universe as a graded hierarchy because that is what perfection in a created whole requires. Each creature is good, but the ordered totality is better than any collection of equals.
elegant: God produces the best possible whole, not the best possible parts. Perfecting the whole requires different grades of perfection in its members. Remove the inequality and you destroy the symphony. Every creature is good; the ordered totality of unequal creatures is better than any assembly of equals.

refashioned:
There is only one world. Since God created all things for his goodness, and the order among things is itself part of what expresses that goodness, the unity of the world's order requires that there be one cosmos. If there were multiple worlds, either they would be unrelated (which would introduce purposeless diversity) or they would form a single ordered whole (making them one world after all).
