The objection that God's infinite power means he could and should create many worlds fails. God's power is not measured by the number of worlds but by the perfection of what he produces. One well-ordered world better reflects divine wisdom than multiple uncoordinated ones.
elegant: There is one world because order is singular. If multiple worlds were unrelated, their diversity would be pointless. If they were related, they would form a single ordered cosmos. One well-ordered universe expresses divine wisdom more perfectly than any multiplication of disconnected realms.

refashioned: We turn now to the distinction of good and evil. Six questions about evil: Is evil a nature? Does evil exist in things? Is good the subject of evil? Does evil destroy good entirely? Is evil rightly divided into suffering and fault? And which has more the character of evil, suffering or fault?
elegant: Now the hardest question in creation: evil. What is it? Where does it live? Can it destroy the good entirely? Six inquiries into the shadow that falls across a world made by infinite goodness.

refashioned:
Evil is not a nature or a positive reality. It is the absence of good. Everything that exists, insofar as it exists, is good. Every nature desires its own being and perfection, and whatever is desirable is good. So being and goodness are convertible. Evil, therefore, cannot signify any being or form. It signifies only the absence of a good that should be present.

This does not mean evil is merely nothing. A privation is not just any absence but the absence of something that ought to be there. Blindness is not simply the lack of wings; it is the lack of sight in a being that should have sight. Evil is real in this sense: it is a genuine privation, a real gap in what should be whole.
elegant: Evil is not a thing. It is the absence of a thing that should be there. Every nature, insofar as it exists, is good. Evil adds nothing to reality; it subtracts. Blindness is not a substance. It is the hole where sight should be. To call evil real is not to grant it being but to name a wound in being.

refashioned:
Aristotle's classification of good and evil as genera reflected the Pythagorean view, not his own settled position. In reality, evil is not a genus but a privation. When evil appears to constitute a species of morality, as when we say a habit is "bad," the species is defined not by a positive nature but by a privation of due order. Good and evil are not contraries in the strict sense of two positive natures opposing each other. Evil is opposed to good as privation to possession.

As for the objection that evil acts and therefore must be real: evil does not act on its own power. It acts through the good that remains in the defective subject. A disease destroys health not by its own positive power but through the disordered action of the body's remaining capacities.
elegant: Evil is not the opposite of good the way cold is the opposite of heat, two positive forces in tension. Evil is privation: the absence of due perfection. It has no nature of its own. When it seems to act, it acts only through the good that remains in the damaged subject, the way a disease destroys through the body's own disordered functions.

refashioned:
Evil does exist in things, not as a positive being but as a real privation. Wherever there is a creature capable of falling short of its proper perfection, evil can occur. And since every creature is finite, every creature can fall short in some respect.

Evil is found in things because the perfection of the universe requires corruptible beings alongside incorruptible ones. The interaction of diverse natural agents inevitably produces some corruption. Fire warms but also burns. The good of the whole requires these interactions, even though they sometimes result in the privation of good in particular things.

The objection that evil cannot exist because it is neither being nor non-being misses the point. Evil is a real privation within a being. It is not a substance, but it is genuinely present in substances as a deficiency.
elegant: Evil exists, not as a being but as a wound in beings. Wherever finite things can fall short of their own perfection, evil finds its opening. The universe, in its richness, contains corruptible creatures alongside incorruptible ones. And wherever there is capacity for corruption, there is room for privation. Evil is real. It is just not a reality.

refashioned:
Evil can only exist in good as its subject. This sounds paradoxical but follows necessarily. Evil is a privation, the absence of a good that should be present. A privation can only exist in something that has, or should have, the missing perfection. Blindness can only exist in a being that ought to see. Therefore every subject of evil must itself be something good, since every existing thing is good insofar as it exists.

This means pure evil is impossible. Evil always parasitizes some good. A thoroughly evil thing, one with no goodness whatsoever, could not exist at all, because to exist is itself a good. The worse something becomes, the less it exists in the full sense. But as long as it exists at all, some good remains.
elegant: Evil can only live in good, because evil is a parasite. A privation requires a host. Blindness can exist only in a being that ought to see. Pure evil is impossible, because pure evil would be pure non-existence. The worse a thing becomes, the less it is. But as long as it exists at all, some good remains. Evil feeds on what it cannot finally consume.

refashioned:
Evil cannot completely destroy good. It can eliminate the particular good to which it is opposed: disease can destroy health. But it cannot destroy the underlying subject or its capacity for good entirely. As long as the subject exists, it retains some good, namely its existence and its fundamental capacity for perfection.

Think of it this way: evil diminishes the aptitude for good, the subject's readiness to achieve its proper perfection. But this aptitude can be diminished endlessly without ever being fully extinguished, because it is rooted in the subject's very nature. The subject's existence is the good that cannot be destroyed without destroying the subject altogether, at which point evil itself would have no foothold.
elegant: Evil can wound the good but never kill it outright. It can strip away this or that perfection, but the subject's bare existence and basic capacity for restoration persist as long as the subject endures. Evil diminishes without limit but never reaches zero, because reaching zero would mean destroying the host, and without the host, the parasite dies too.

refashioned: The particular good opposed to a given evil can be wholly removed. Disease wholly removes health. But the underlying goods, the subject's existence and its basic capacity for restoration, remain. Good is not uniform; it has layers. Evil can strip away one layer while leaving others intact. This is why evil can always diminish good further but never finish the job completely.
elegant: Good is layered. Evil can peel away one layer entirely, as disease strips away health. But deeper layers remain: existence, the capacity for healing, the bare fact of being something rather than nothing. Evil never runs out of good to diminish, and good never runs out of resilience to survive.

refashioned:
Evil is rightly divided into two kinds: suffering (or penalty) and fault (or sin). Evil is the privation of good. But "good" means different things in different contexts. The good of an agent's proper action is one thing; the good of the agent's own being is another. Fault is the privation of right action: the agent fails to act as it should. Suffering is the privation of something in the agent's own being or condition: something the agent has or should have is taken away.

These two are connected. Suffering follows from fault as its natural consequence, because a failure of right order in action produces a corresponding disorder in the agent's condition. But not all suffering traces to the individual's own fault. Sometimes it flows from the order of the whole, in which one part suffers for the correction or good of another.
elegant: There are two kinds of evil because there are two ways to lose what should be yours. Fault is the failure of right action; suffering is the loss of something in your own condition. Fault corrupts what you do. Suffering corrupts what you have. And fault, because it disorders the will itself, is the deeper wound.

refashioned:
Fault has more the character of evil than suffering does. Suffering is the privation of some good in the agent's being or condition. Fault is the privation of right order in the agent's action. But right action is a greater good than mere well-being, because action is the purpose for which capacities exist. A power exists for the sake of its proper act, not the reverse.

Moreover, suffering can be involuntary and does not always diminish the agent's moral worth. Fault is voluntary and directly corrupts the will's relation to the good. Suffering is the consequence of disorder; fault is the disorder itself. The disease is worse than the symptom.

The objection that suffering opposes the agent's good while fault only opposes the good of action, and the agent is higher than the action, reverses the real priority. The agent's highest good is right action. Suffering harms what the agent has. Fault harms what the agent is for.
elegant: Fault is worse than suffering, because action is higher than condition. A power exists for the sake of its proper act. A hand exists to grasp; an eye exists to see. When the act goes wrong, the purpose goes wrong. Suffering harms what you possess. Fault harms what you are for. The disease is always worse than the symptom.

refashioned: We now ask about the cause of evil. Three questions: Can good cause evil? Is God the cause of evil? And is there a supreme evil that is the first cause of all evils?
elegant: Where does evil come from? Not from its own first principle, not from God's intention, and not from the darkness itself. Three questions trace evil to its surprising source: the good.

refashioned:
Good is the cause of evil, but only incidentally. Evil has no cause of its own, because evil is not a positive thing but a privation. A privation does not have an efficient cause in the way a positive reality does. What causes evil is a good agent that falls short, either in its power or in its action.

In nature, evil results from the collision of goods. Fire is good, but it destroys wood. The good of one thing causes the privation of another. In voluntary action, evil results from a defective will, which is itself a created good that fails to maintain right order. The will does not aim at evil as evil. It aims at some apparent good while failing to attend to the order it should maintain.

So evil always traces back to a good cause acting deficiently. Evil is a byproduct of finite goods interacting, not a force with its own independent origin.
elegant: Evil has no cause of its own, because evil is not a thing in its own right. It is what happens when a good cause falls short. Fire does not intend to destroy; it intends to burn, and destruction is a side-effect. The will does not choose evil as evil; it reaches for some apparent good while losing grip on the order it should maintain. Evil is always a misfire, never a direct hit.

refashioned: A good will does not produce morally evil acts. But the will as a natural power, which is itself good, can produce a disordered act. Good causes evil not by intending evil but by falling short in execution or in the ordering of its action. Fire does not intend to destroy; it intends to burn, and destruction is a side effect. The human will does not choose evil as evil; it chooses a lesser good while neglecting the proper order of goods.
elegant: Good causes evil the way a marksman causes a miss: not by aiming at it, but by failing to aim well enough. Every disordered act is still an act, and every act comes from a real power, which is itself a good. The evil lies not in the power but in its misdirection. Creation's capacity for failure is the shadow cast by its capacity for freedom.

refashioned:
God is not the cause of moral evil. He is, however, the cause of the natural order in which suffering and physical evil occur. When Scripture says God "creates evil," it refers to the natural evils of punishment, disaster, and correction, not to sin.

God is the cause of every being and every action insofar as it has being. But the deficiency in an action, the part that constitutes its evil, comes from the defective secondary cause, not from God. An analogy: the power of locomotion causes walking, but the limp comes from the crooked leg, not from the motive power itself. God gives every agent its power to act. When that agent acts deficiently, the deficiency is the agent's own, not God's.

God permits evil because the perfection of the universe requires the possibility of failure in finite agents. But permitting evil and causing evil are fundamentally different.
elegant: God is not the author of sin. He is the author of every being and every power, but the defect in a power's exercise belongs to the defective agent, not to the one who gave the power. The limp comes from the crooked leg, not from the capacity to walk. God permits evil because a universe of free agents is worth the risk of their failure. But permitting is not causing.

refashioned: Is there a supreme evil that causes all other evils, as God is the supreme good that causes all goods? Several arguments suggest so: contraries should have contrary causes; if supreme good exists in nature, so should supreme evil; evil seems to grow endlessly, suggesting an infinite source.
elegant: Is there a dark god, a supreme evil counterpart to supreme good? The symmetry is seductive: if all goodness flows from one source, perhaps all evil flows from another.

refashioned:
There is no supreme evil, no first principle of evil standing opposite to God. This follows from everything established so far.

First, the supreme good is essentially good. But nothing can be essentially evil, because every being, insofar as it exists, is good. A being that was pure evil would have no existence at all.

Second, the supreme good contains all goodness perfectly. But a supreme evil containing all evil is impossible, because total evil would destroy itself. Evil can only exist by parasitizing good. The more evil something becomes, the less it exists. Complete evil is complete non-existence.

Third, evil is always accidental and particular, never essential and universal. It arises from the deficiency of particular causes, not from some cosmic anti-principle. Evil has no unified source because it is not a unified phenomenon. It is the many different ways finite things can fall short.
elegant: There is no supreme evil. The argument is deceptively simple: to be supremely evil, a thing would have to be supremely devoid of good. But to be supremely devoid of good is to not exist at all. A dark god would be an impossible god, because absolute evil is absolute nothingness. Evil has no unified source because it is not a unified phenomenon. It is the many different ways finite things can fail.

refashioned:
Contraries within nature do share a common genus and a common cause, but evil is not the true contrary of good. It is a privation. Privation and possession naturally belong to the same subject, but the supreme good has no potentiality and therefore cannot be the subject of any privation. Evil opposes the kind of good that can fail, not the good that is pure act.

As for evil appearing to grow endlessly: evil diminishes good but cannot eliminate it entirely, as established earlier. This endless diminution does not point to an infinite source of evil. It points to the fact that finite good, while inexhaustible at its root, can always be further diminished by deficient causes.
elegant: Evil is not the true opposite of good. It is good's privation, not its rival. A rival would need its own being, its own power, its own territory. Privation needs only a host and a gap. Evil endlessly diminishes without pointing to any infinite source of diminishment. It is parasitic all the way down.

refashioned: We now turn from the general production of creatures to the distinction between spiritual and corporeal beings. We begin with purely spiritual creatures, called angels in Scripture. We consider first their substance (both absolutely and in relation to bodies), then their knowledge, then their will, and finally their creation. On angelic substance considered absolutely, five questions: Are angels entirely incorporeal? Are they composed of matter and form? How many are there? Do they differ in species? And are they incorruptible?
elegant: From the general architecture of creation, we turn to its most exalted citizens: the angels. Five questions open the inquiry into pure spirit: its immateriality, its composition, its number, its diversity, and its permanence.

refashioned:
Angels are entirely incorporeal. The perfection of the universe requires intellectual creatures, beings that know and understand. But intelligence cannot be the function of a body. Every body is confined to a particular place and time, while understanding grasps universal truths that transcend spatial and temporal limits. So the universe must contain beings that are incorporeal.

Earlier thinkers who denied incorporeal beings made the mistake of assuming that only what can be sensed or imagined is real. They collapsed intellect into sensation. But the very fact that our intellect rises above sense perception, grasping abstract truths that no sense organ could register, is proof that there must be incorporeal realities.

The objection that angels are called corporeal compared to God does not mean they have bodies. It means they are limited, unlike God who is unlimited. Every creature has boundaries, but spiritual creatures are bounded by their essence, not by physical dimensions.
elegant: Angels are entirely without bodies. Intelligence grasps universals that no sense organ could register. A mind confined to matter could never rise above the particular. The universe therefore demands beings whose knowing is unconstrained by flesh, whose existence is pure form without physical dimension. Earlier thinkers who denied this simply lacked the imagination to conceive what exceeds the imagination.

refashioned:
Angels are not composed of matter and form. In material beings, matter is what gives individuality within a species, while form gives the species itself. But angels are not individuated by matter. Each angel is a pure form, subsisting on its own.

This does not mean angels have no potentiality at all. They are not pure act, which belongs to God alone. But their composition is not of matter and form. It is of essence and existence. An angel's essence (what it is) is distinct from its existence (that it is). This is the fundamental composition in every creature: only God is his own existence.

The objection that angels must have matter because they are in a genus (substance) confuses material and logical composition. Genus and species in immaterial beings come not from matter and form but from the degree of perfection of the form itself. Different grades of immaterial perfection generate different species without any matter.
elegant: Angels are not made of matter and form. They are pure forms, each subsisting on its own. But 'pure form' does not mean 'pure act.' Only God is pure act. An angel is composed not of matter and form but of essence and existence: what it is remains distinct from that it is. This is the fundamental fissure in every creature, the mark of dependence that separates everything created from the one who creates.

refashioned:
In material things, genus derives from matter and specific difference from form. In immaterial things, this does not apply. Instead, different grades of perfection within the form itself account for both genus and difference. Each species of angel is constituted by a form at a specific degree of perfection. The more perfect the form, the more universal its power of understanding.

The objection that angels must have matter because they receive existence from God also fails. Receiving existence makes an angel composed of essence and existence, but this is not a matter-form composition. Matter is pure potentiality for physical form. An angel's essence is an actual intelligible nature, not potentiality for physical configuration.
elegant: In material things, matter accounts for individuality and form accounts for species. In angels, there is no matter. Instead, different grades of formal perfection generate different species. Each angelic nature is a distinct degree of intellectual power. The hierarchy is not built from identical bricks arranged differently but from fundamentally different kinds of luminosity.

refashioned:
Angels exist in vast numbers, far exceeding the number of material things. Since immaterial beings are more perfect than material ones, the perfection of the universe demands that they be the most abundant class of beings. Material species are multiplied within each species by matter: there are many individual humans, dogs, or stars. But angelic species are multiplied without matter, meaning there are many distinct species of angel, each a unique grade of intellectual perfection.

Scripture confirms this abundance: "Thousands of thousands ministered to him, and ten thousand times ten thousand stood before him." The point is not a literal count but that the angelic host surpasses all material multiplicity.
elegant: The angelic host vastly outnumbers the material world. In the economy of creation, the higher a class of being, the more abundant it is. Material species multiply individuals by matter; angelic species multiply by the sheer diversity of form. Thousands of thousands, Scripture says, and ten thousand times ten thousand. The point is not a census but a gesture toward plenitude beyond material reckoning.

refashioned: Angelic number is not the kind of quantity that comes from dividing a continuous body. It is the multiplicity that comes from distinct forms. Since each angel is its own species, the number of angels is the number of distinct grades of intellectual being. Their closeness to God's unity does not mean they are few. Rather, the simplicity of their composition allows for a greater diversity of species than material things can achieve.
elegant: Angelic number is not the kind you get by dividing a body into pieces. It is the multiplicity of distinct intellectual natures. Their closeness to divine simplicity does not make them few. It makes their diversity richer, because the simpler the composition, the more room for variety at the level of form itself.

refashioned:
Each angel differs in species from every other angel. In material beings, matter allows multiple individuals to share one form: many humans share human nature because matter individuates them. Angels have no matter. So there cannot be two angels of the same species. Each angel is a unique species, a distinct grade of intellectual being.

The objection that angels differ only by degree (more or less perfect) and that degree does not change species fails. In immaterial beings, every different degree of perfection constitutes a different species. It is not like one human being smarter than another. It is like the difference between fundamentally different kinds of intellectual nature.
elegant: Every angel is its own species. Where matter exists, many individuals can share one nature: there are billions of humans but one human nature. Without matter, there is nothing to individuate within a shared form. Each angel is a unique grade of intellectual being, as different from every other angel as one species is from another. No two angels are the same kind of thing.

refashioned:
Angels are incorruptible. Corruption occurs when form separates from matter. Since angels have no matter, they cannot undergo this kind of dissolution. Their being is a pure subsisting form, and such a form cannot lose itself.

Even if we consider that angels are composed of essence and existence (and so are not absolutely simple), this composition does not make them corruptible. Form separates from matter through natural processes. But existence does not separate from essence through any natural process. Only God, who gave existence, could annihilate it. And God does not annihilate what he has created, because his will sustains all things in being permanently.

So angels are naturally incorruptible, and they are preserved in existence by God's will, which does not withdraw what it has given.
elegant: Angels cannot die. Corruption is the separation of form from matter, and angels have no matter to separate from. Their being is a subsisting form that cannot lose itself. Could God annihilate them? In principle, yes. But God does not withdraw what he has given. His will sustains all things permanently. Angels endure not by their own necessity but by the faithfulness of their maker.

refashioned: We now compare angels to bodies. Three questions: Do angels have bodies naturally united to them? Can they assume bodies? And do they perform living functions in assumed bodies?
elegant: If angels have no bodies, what are we to make of their appearances in Scripture? Three questions: Are they naturally embodied? Can they take on temporary bodies? And do they truly live in the flesh they borrow?

refashioned:
Angels do not have bodies naturally united to them. An intellectual substance does not need a body for its proper operation, which is understanding. Bodies would hinder rather than help angelic intelligence, since understanding operates without material organs.

Some early thinkers attributed subtle bodies, made of air or fire, to angels. But this was based on the inability to conceive of incorporeal substance rather than on sound reasoning. Once we grasp that intellect operates without matter, there is no reason to assign bodies to purely intellectual beings.

The fact that angels sometimes appear in bodily form in Scripture does not prove they have natural bodies. These appearances involve assumed bodies, taken temporarily for the purpose of communication with humans.
elegant: Angels have no natural bodies. Intelligence does not need flesh; it is hindered by it. The early thinkers who gave angels subtle bodies of air or fire were confessing their own inability to imagine what transcends imagination. Once you grasp that thought operates without matter, there is no reason to clothe pure intellect in any material garment.

refashioned:
Angels can assume bodies, not by being naturally united to them, but by moving and shaping material elements to produce an appearance of a living body. The assumed body is not animated by the angel the way our bodies are animated by our souls. It is more like a puppet: the angel moves it from the outside, using its power to produce visible and audible effects.

The purpose of these assumed bodies is to make the angel's presence and message perceptible to human senses. Angels operate in the spiritual realm, but when they need to interact with embodied humans, they take on visible form. The body is a tool for communication, not a natural part of the angel's being.
elegant: Angels can take on bodies, but the way a puppeteer takes up a puppet, not the way a soul inhabits a body. The assumed form is shaped from condensed air or another element, moved by angelic power to simulate life. It is a medium of communication, not a natural extension of the angel's being. The body is borrowed for the message and returned when the message is delivered.

refashioned:
Angels do not truly perform biological functions in the bodies they assume. They do not actually eat, breathe, or reproduce. When Scripture describes angels eating or speaking, the actions are apparent rather than real in the biological sense. An angel can move an assumed body so that it appears to eat, using air to form sounds that appear to be speech, but no genuine nutrition or respiration occurs.

This is not deception. Just as figurative language in Scripture is not false because no one takes it literally, so the appearance of life in an assumed body is not a lie. It is a visible sign of spiritual realities. The body serves as a medium for the angel's message, not as evidence that angels have biological life.
elegant: Angels in assumed bodies do not truly eat, breathe, or reproduce. What Scripture describes as angelic dining is appearance, not digestion. The angel moves the body so it seems to live, but no genuine biology occurs. This is not deception any more than a parable is a lie. The assumed body is a visible sign of invisible realities, and signs do their work precisely by not being the thing they signify.

refashioned:
The fact that angels in assumed bodies appear to perform living functions is similar to how Scripture uses sensible imagery to convey spiritual truths. Neither is deception. The assumed body is taken to represent spiritual properties through visible actions. Sensation in the assumed body is not real sensation. Speech is produced by manipulating air, not by biological vocal organs. If Scripture records angels eating, the food is dissolved by angelic power, not digested.

The essential point: assumed bodies are instruments of communication. Their apparent life is a sign, not a fact of angelic biology.
elegant: Speech in an assumed body is air shaped by power, not vocal cords vibrating by biology. Eating is matter dissolved by angelic force, not nutrients absorbed. The body is an instrument for communication, and its apparent life is a sign to be read, not a fact to be analyzed. Angels borrow flesh the way speakers borrow metaphors: to make the invisible audible.

refashioned: Three questions about angels and place: Is an angel in a place? Can an angel be in several places at once? Can several angels be in the same place?
elegant: Can a being without dimensions occupy a place? Three questions about how something without size can be somewhere.

refashioned:
An angel is in a place, but not in the way a body is. A body occupies place by filling it with its dimensions. An angel occupies place by applying its power to that location. Wherever an angel exercises its causal influence, that is where the angel is.

So angelic presence is defined by activity, not by spatial extension. An angel does not have dimensions that match a location's dimensions. Instead, the angel's power touches a place by acting there. This means angelic "location" is fundamentally different from bodily location, even though we use the same word.
elegant: An angel is in a place not by filling it with dimensions but by touching it with power. Wherever an angel acts, there it is. Angelic location is defined by activity, not by extension. The angel does not take up space; it takes up a task. Its presence is not the displacement of volume but the application of influence.

refashioned:
An angel cannot be in multiple places simultaneously. Its power is finite, so when it applies itself to one location, it is definitively there and not elsewhere. The analogy with the soul, which is entirely in every part of the body, does not hold, because the soul is the form of the body and naturally present throughout it. An angel is not the form of any place.

The angel's presence in a place is determined by the scope of its application of power. It can be present to a large or small area depending on how broadly it acts, but it cannot be present in multiple unconnected places at the same time.
elegant: An angel cannot be in two places at once. Its power, though immense, is finite. When applied to one location, it is definitively there and not elsewhere. The angel can be present to a wider or narrower area, depending on the scope of its action. But it cannot split its presence across disconnected sites. Even spirits are somewhere in particular.

refashioned:
Two angels cannot be in the same place at the same time. Although angels do not fill space the way bodies do, each angel's presence in a place constitutes a complete application of spiritual power to that location. A second angel applying its power to the same location would imply that one place has two complete spiritual presences, which is as impossible in the spiritual order as two bodies occupying the same space is in the physical order.

The underlying principle is that a complete cause is sufficient for its effect. Two complete spiritual agents applied to the same place would mean one place with two sufficient spiritual presences, which is redundant and impossible.
elegant: Two angels cannot occupy the same place, any more than two bodies can. Each angel's presence constitutes a complete spiritual application to that location. A second complete presence would be redundant and therefore impossible. One place, one sufficient spiritual agent. The principle of non-overlap holds in the spiritual order as firmly as in the physical.

refashioned: Three questions about angelic movement: Can an angel move from place to place? Must it pass through intermediate space? And is its movement instantaneous or in time?
elegant: Can an angel travel? Three questions about how a being without parts crosses distances it does not physically occupy.

refashioned:
An angel can move from place to place. Since an angel's presence in a place is defined by where it applies its power, it can successively apply its power to different places. This succession of presences constitutes local movement.

Aristotle's argument that nothing without parts can move locally applies to indivisible points within a continuous magnitude, not to immaterial beings. An angel is not a dimensionless point. It is a substance that can be wholly present to different places in succession. Its movement is not the physical displacement of a body but the successive application of spiritual power to different locations.
elegant: An angel can move from place to place because its presence is the application of power, and that application can shift. Where once it acted, it ceases; where now it acts, it arrives. This is not the displacement of a body through space but the successive reapplication of a spiritual presence. The will is the engine. The angel moves because it chooses to be elsewhere.

refashioned:
Aristotle's proof that indivisible things cannot move applies to physical indivisibles, not to spiritual substances. An angel's movement can be either continuous, smoothly transitioning through space, or discontinuous, a succession of discrete presences. In the discontinuous case, the angel simply ceases to be in one place and begins to be in another, without physically traversing the interval.

The will is the principle of angelic movement. An angel moves because it wills to apply its power to a new location. This is fundamentally different from physical motion caused by external forces.
elegant: Angelic movement need not be continuous. An angel can simply cease to be in one place and begin to be in another, without sweeping through every intermediate point. Or it can move smoothly, passing through space like a wave. Either way, the motion is driven by will, not by physical force. The angel is not pushed; it goes.

refashioned: Does an angel have to pass through every intermediate point when moving from one place to another? This depends on whether the movement is continuous or discontinuous.
elegant: Must an angel pass through every point between departure and arrival? It depends on whether the movement is continuous or discrete.

refashioned:
If an angel's movement is continuous, it must pass through intermediate space, just as any continuous motion traverses a path. But if the movement is discontinuous, the angel can go directly from one place to another without traversing the middle. It simply ceases to apply its power at the first location and begins to apply it at the second.

This discontinuous movement is possible because angelic presence is not tied to spatial extension. A body must traverse intermediate space because it physically occupies each point along the way. An angel's presence is a matter of applied power, and power can be applied to any location the angel's will selects, without needing to sweep through every intervening point.
elegant: If the angel moves continuously, it traverses intermediate space as any continuous motion must. But if it moves discretely, it leaps from here to there without visiting the middle. A body must cross every intervening point because it physically occupies each one in turn. An angel, whose presence is a matter of applied power rather than extended body, can apply that power wherever it wills. The middle is optional.

refashioned: The angel's place is not sized to match it physically but to match its application of power. So angelic place can be divisible or indivisible depending on the scope of the angel's action. The soul's ability to think about distant things without traversing space is not a true parallel, because thought does not involve applying the soul's essence to a location. But the angel's movement does involve applying its essence. The key difference from bodily movement is that the application is voluntary and need not be spatially continuous.
elegant: Angelic place is sized to the scope of the angel's action, not to the dimensions of a body. The angel's presence can be as broad or narrow as its application of power. And since that application is voluntary, the angel can resize its 'footprint' at will. Thought travels without crossing space, but angelic movement is not mere thought: it is the actual relocation of a being's operative presence.

refashioned: Is angelic movement instantaneous? If an angel can go from one place to another without passing through intermediate space, it might seem that movement is instantaneous. But the issue is more nuanced.
elegant: Is angelic movement instantaneous? If the angel can skip intermediate space, why should the arrival take any time at all?

refashioned:
Angelic movement is not instantaneous; it occurs in time, though not necessarily in the continuous time that measures physical motion. The time of angelic movement can be a succession of discrete instants rather than a smooth continuum.

The argument for instantaneous movement is that the angel's power infinitely exceeds any bodily resistance, so movement should take zero time. But this misunderstands what determines the speed of angelic movement. It is not a ratio of power to resistance but a matter of the angel's will determining how many successive positions to occupy and how quickly. The angel chooses the tempo of its movement.

Some held that an angel is in the starting place for an entire preceding time and then in the destination at the last instant. But the more careful view is that angelic movement involves a real succession of positions in a real, though possibly non-continuous, time.
elegant: Angelic movement takes time, but not necessarily the continuous time of clocks. The angel determines the tempo of its own travel. Its movement is not a ratio of power to resistance, like a body fighting friction, but a voluntary sequence of positions. The angel chooses how fast to go. Its journey unfolds in a succession of discrete moments, each determined by the will rather than by physics.

refashioned: If angelic time is not continuous but consists of a succession of discrete instants, it has no fixed ratio to continuous physical time. The swiftness of angelic movement is determined not by the magnitude of angelic power but by the angel's will. An angel can move faster or slower as it chooses, because its movement is volitional, not mechanical.
elegant: The time of angelic movement has no fixed ratio to physical time. An angel's journey can be swifter or slower as the angel chooses, because the speed is determined by will, not mechanics. Physics measures bodies. Angelic time measures the volitional rhythm of a spirit in transit.

refashioned: We now turn from angelic substance to angelic knowledge. Four areas of inquiry: the power of angelic knowledge, its medium, its objects, and its mode. On the power of knowledge, five questions: Is understanding the angel's substance? Is understanding the same as existing for an angel? Is the angel's power of intelligence the same as its essence? Does the angel have both active and passive intellect? And is angelic knowledge purely intellectual?
elegant: From angelic substance and motion, we turn to angelic mind. How does a being without senses know anything? Five questions explore the nature and limits of a purely intellectual power.

refashioned:
An angel's act of understanding is not identical with its substance. In God alone are being, essence, and activity one and the same. In every creature, including angels, there is a real distinction between what the thing is and what the thing does. The angel's substance is the foundation of its understanding, but understanding is an operation that proceeds from that substance.

Aristotle's claim that the intellect's action is its substance applies to the divine intellect, not to created intellects. A created intellect has potentiality: it can understand this or that. Its substance is actual in one way; its understanding is actual in another. The two are related as power to act.
elegant: An angel's act of understanding is not identical with its substance. Only in God are being and knowing the same thing. In every creature, what the thing is and what the thing does remain distinct. The angel's substance is the foundation; its understanding is the activity that rises from it. To exist and to think are two acts, even for the highest created intellect.

refashioned:
In an angel, to understand is not the same as to exist. Existence is the angel's most fundamental actuality. Understanding is a further actuality that follows from it. The angel exists first, and then, by virtue of existing as an intellectual nature, it understands.

If understanding were identical with existence, then every degree of existence would be a degree of understanding, which is false. Many things exist without understanding. The angel's existence and its understanding are related but distinct acts. Existence is the ground; understanding is the operation that flows from it.
elegant: For an angel, to exist and to understand are distinct acts. Existence is the most fundamental actuality, the ground on which everything else stands. Understanding is a further flowering from that ground. Many things exist without understanding. The angel's knowledge is not its existence dressed up in different language; it is a real operation proceeding from a real power, rooted in but not identical with the angel's being.

refashioned:
An angel's power of intelligence is distinct from its essence. Only in God is every attribute identical with the divine essence. In creatures, powers and capacities are really distinct from the substance they belong to. The angel's intellect is a power rooted in its essence but not identical with it.

This follows from the fact that the angel's act of understanding is distinct from its existence. If the power of understanding were identical with the essence, and the essence were identical with existence, then understanding would be identical with existing, which we just denied. So the power of understanding is something in the angel that flows from its essence as a capacity, ready to be actualized in particular acts of knowledge.
elegant: An angel's power of intelligence is not the same as its essence. Only God's every attribute is identical with his essence. In creatures, powers flow from substance the way branches grow from a trunk. The angel's intellect is a capacity rooted in its nature, ready to be activated in particular acts of knowing. Essence, power, and act form three distinct levels even in the simplest created mind.

refashioned:
Angels do not have an active and passive intellect in the way humans do. Humans need an active intellect to abstract intelligible forms from sensory data and a passive intellect to receive those forms. Angels do not abstract from sensory data. They have intelligible forms directly, implanted by God at their creation.

However, in a broad sense, there is something analogous to passive intellect in angels, since they receive intelligible forms rather than being those forms. They are not pure act in the order of knowledge. They have a capacity that is fulfilled by the forms they possess. But they lack an active intellect in the strict sense because they do not need to extract intelligibility from material things.
elegant: Angels do not need to extract meaning from matter the way we do. They have no senses from which to abstract. Their minds come pre-furnished by God with intelligible forms representing all that they are meant to know. Where we must build knowledge brick by brick from sensory experience, angels begin with the completed architecture. They have receptive intellect but no need for the laborious machinery of abstraction.

refashioned:
Angels have only intellectual knowledge, not sensation. They lack bodies and therefore lack sense organs. Since sensation requires a bodily organ, angels cannot sense. Memory, imagination, and experience, as they function in us, are tied to our bodily nature and do not exist in angels in the same form.

This does not mean angels are less knowledgeable than we are. Their intellectual knowledge is far more powerful and comprehensive than ours. What we achieve slowly through sensation, abstraction, and reasoning, they grasp immediately and completely through their innate intellectual forms. They do not need the stepping-stones of sense experience because they begin where we hope to end.
elegant: Angels know only by intellect, never by sensation. They have no bodies and therefore no sense organs. What we achieve painfully, through sensation, abstraction, and step-by-step reasoning, they grasp immediately and completely through the forms implanted in them at creation. They do not need the ladder of the senses because they begin at the height where that ladder terminates.

refashioned: Three questions about how angels know: Do they know everything through their own substance? Do they understand through innate species or species drawn from things? And do higher angels know through more universal species than lower angels?
elegant: How does an angel come to know what it knows? Three questions about the medium of angelic cognition: its own essence, innate species from God, and the universality of those species in higher versus lower angels.

refashioned:
Angels do not know all things through their own substance alone. An angel knows itself through its own essence, since it is directly present to itself as an intelligible form. But other things are not identical with the angel's essence, so knowing them requires something additional.

That additional element is intelligible species, intellectual forms implanted by God at the angel's creation, through which the angel knows realities other than itself. These species are likenesses of things, not derived from the things themselves but from God, who contains the ideas of all things. The angel's mind is furnished with these forms from the start, like a library fully stocked at the moment of its construction.
elegant: An angel knows itself through its own essence, since it is directly present to itself as something intelligible. But the world beyond itself is another matter. For that, it needs intelligible species, intellectual forms implanted by God at creation. The angel's mind is a library fully stocked on the day of its construction, its shelves filled not by experience but by the author of all experience.

refashioned:
Angels do not derive their knowledge from things. Human intellect abstracts forms from sensory experience. Angelic intellect does not. Angels know through innate species that God implanted in them at creation. These species are likenesses of things, but they come from above (from God, the cause of things), not from below (from the things themselves).

This follows from the angel's nature as a purely intellectual being without senses. Since angels have no sensory apparatus, they cannot receive impressions from material things. Their knowledge of the material world comes through the innate forms God gave them, forms that represent material things as they exist in the divine creative plan.
elegant: Angelic knowledge flows downward from God, not upward from things. Angels have no senses to receive impressions from the world. They know creation through the innate forms God gave them, forms that represent things as they exist in the divine plan. Their knowledge of the material world is secondhand only in that it comes from above, which is to say, from the most authoritative source possible.

refashioned:
The images of creatures in the angel's mind are not derived from creatures but from God, in whom the ideas of all creatures originally exist. As Augustine puts it, the pattern of a creature exists in God's Word before the creature is made, and the knowledge of that pattern exists in the angelic mind before the creature comes into being.

This means angelic knowledge is a kind of participated divine knowledge. Angels know things as God designed them, through forms that reflect the creative plan rather than through forms extracted from created objects after the fact.
elegant: The patterns in the angelic mind are not photographs of creatures but blueprints from the creator. Angels know things as God designed them, through forms that reflect the creative intention rather than the finished product. This makes angelic knowledge a kind of participated omniscience: seeing creatures through the eyes, so to speak, of the one who conceived them.

refashioned:
Higher angels understand through more universal species than lower angels. A more powerful intellect can grasp more through a single concept. Where a lower angel might need many specific forms to know a range of things, a higher angel can know the same range through fewer, more comprehensive forms.

This is not a deficiency in the higher angel's knowledge. The higher angel's universal species are not vague generalizations. They contain everything the lower angel's particular species contain, and more. A single comprehensive form in a superior intellect captures all the detail that multiple narrower forms capture in an inferior one.
elegant: Higher angels know through fewer, more comprehensive forms. Where a lesser intellect needs many particular concepts, a greater intellect grasps the same range through a single, luminous universal. The higher species is not vaguer but richer, containing everything the lower species contain and more, the way a master's grasp of a field exceeds the student's not by being less detailed but by being more unified.

refashioned: The universality of angelic species is not the result of abstraction from particulars. It is a feature of how God furnishes different grades of intellect. A higher angel receives forms that are more universal by nature, not less detailed. The universal species of a higher angel is like a high-resolution map that covers a vast territory, compared to the lower angel's collection of detailed but narrower maps. Both capture the same reality, but the higher angel does so with greater unity and elegance.
elegant: The universality of a higher angel's knowledge is not the result of abstracting away detail. It is the gift of a more powerful lens. A high-resolution map covering an entire continent contains everything a detailed city map contains, and infinitely more besides. Higher angels know more, not less, through their more universal species. Unity and detail increase together.

refashioned: Three questions about what angels know of immaterial realities: Does an angel know itself? Does one angel know another? And does an angel know God by natural means?
elegant: What do angels know of the immaterial world? Three questions about self-knowledge, knowledge of other angels, and natural knowledge of God.

refashioned:
One angel can know another. Each angel possesses in its mind innate species representing other angels. Since an angel's nature is an intelligible form, and since other angelic natures are of the same general kind (immaterial intelligible forms differing in degree of perfection), each angel has a natural affinity with and knowledge of the others.

The objection that having a form of another nature within you would prevent knowing external things applies to material intellects corrupted by sensory data, not to immaterial intellects furnished with innate species. An angel's possession of another angel's species does not overwhelm its intellect any more than knowing one truth prevents you from knowing another.
elegant: Angels know one another through innate species representing each other's natures. Since every angelic nature is an intelligible form, and since all angels share the general character of immaterial intellect, each angel possesses an innate likeness of every other. They are transparent to each other not because they lack privacy but because they share a common medium of intelligibility.

refashioned: Angels know one another not because one causes the other, but because of the likeness among angelic natures. All angels share the general character of immaterial intellectual substance, differing only in degree of perfection. Each angel has innate species that represent other angelic natures. The species are not derived from the other angels but implanted by God, who created the whole order of angelic being.
elegant: Angels know one another not because one causes the other but because they share a kinship of form. All are immaterial intellectual substances differing in degree. Each carries within itself God-given species that represent the others. Their mutual knowledge is part of the furniture of their minds, installed by the same hand that made the minds themselves.

refashioned:
An angel can know God through its natural powers, but only in a limited way. Since the angel's own nature is an effect of God, and effects bear some likeness to their cause, the angel can know God as its cause and origin. This natural knowledge of God is far superior to our natural knowledge, because the angel is a much closer likeness to God than any material creature.

But this natural knowledge falls infinitely short of knowing God as he is in himself. God's essence infinitely surpasses every created intellect. No angel can naturally comprehend God. To see God as he truly is requires supernatural elevation, the light of glory, which exceeds any creature's natural capacity. Natural angelic knowledge of God is real and profound, but it is still knowledge of God through a created likeness, not face-to-face vision.
elegant: By nature, an angel can know God, but only through a glass darkly. The angel is God's effect, and every effect bears some likeness to its cause. Since the angel is the closest likeness to God in all creation, its natural knowledge of God far surpasses ours. But it is still knowledge through a created likeness, not face-to-face vision. To see God as he truly is requires a light beyond nature, a light no creature can kindle for itself.

refashioned: Five questions about angelic knowledge of the material world: Do angels know material things? Do they know individual particulars? Do they know the future? Do they know secret thoughts? And do they know the mysteries of grace?
elegant: How do purely immaterial minds know the material world? Five questions: Do angels know material things? Do they know individual particulars? Can they foresee the future? Can they read thoughts? And do they know the mysteries of grace?

refashioned:
Angels know material things. Their innate species, received from God, represent not only immaterial realities but also the material world. Since God is the cause of both spiritual and material things, and since he furnished angels with knowledge through species that reflect his creative plan, those species encompass material creation as well.

The objection that material things are beneath angels and therefore cannot perfect them confuses the direction of knowledge. The angel is not perfected by the material thing itself but by the intelligible species that represents it. And that species comes from God, not from the material thing. Angels know the material world from above, through the divine ideas, not from below through sensory contact.
elegant: Angels know material things, but from above, not from below. Their innate species, received from God, represent the entire created order, matter included. The angel is not perfected by the material thing but by the intelligible form that represents it, and that form comes from the author of both spirit and matter. Angels see the material world through the lens of its divine design.

refashioned: Do angels know individual particular things, or only universals? This question matters because if angels cannot know singulars, they cannot govern the world, since all events involve individuals.
elegant: Can angels know individual things, or only abstractions? The question is urgent: an angel that cannot know particulars cannot govern the world, since everything that happens, happens in particular.

refashioned:
Angels know individual things, not just universals. Denying this would undermine both faith and philosophy. Faith holds that angels administer particular affairs in the world, which requires knowledge of particulars. Philosophy holds that angels move the heavenly bodies and direct natural processes, which also requires knowledge of singulars.

Angels know singulars because their innate species, coming from God, represent things as God knows them, and God knows all things in their individuality. Human intellect knows only universals directly because it abstracts from material conditions. But angelic species are not abstracted from matter. They are comprehensive representations that include both the universal nature and its individual instances.
elegant: Angels know individual things, not just universals. Their innate species, coming from God, represent things as God knows them, which is to say, in their full concrete individuality. The human mind knows only universals directly because it must abstract from material conditions. The angelic mind, furnished with comprehensive divine impressions, grasps both the universal nature and every individual instance. Where we generalize by necessity, they particularize by nature.

refashioned: Aristotle's claim that intellect knows only universals while sense knows particulars applies to the human intellect, which works by abstraction from material conditions. Angelic intellect does not abstract from matter. Its species represent things completely, including their individuality. The higher bears a relation to the lower that includes the lower's details, not just its general features. So angelic knowledge of singulars does not reduce the angel to the level of sensation; it operates at a higher level that encompasses what sensation provides and more.
elegant: Aristotle's dictum that intellect grasps universals and sense grasps particulars applies to human cognition, which works by abstraction from matter. Angelic intellect does not abstract. Its forms are comprehensive enough to capture individual detail without descending to the level of sense. The higher power encompasses what the lower power provides, and more. Knowing particulars does not diminish angelic intellect; it demonstrates its scope.

refashioned:
Angels do not know future contingent events as they are in themselves. The future, as contingent, does not yet exist in a determinate state. Only God, who sees all of time in a single eternal present, knows the future with certainty.

However, angels can know some future events through their causes. When a cause necessarily produces a certain effect, the angel can predict it with certainty by knowing the cause. When a cause usually but not always produces an effect, the angel can predict it with probability, the way an experienced physician predicts the likely course of a disease. But events that depend on free will or on the convergence of independent causal chains remain unknowable to angels in advance.

Prophetic knowledge of the future is a different matter. That comes from divine revelation, not from angelic natural knowledge.
elegant: Angels cannot see the future as it is in itself, because the future, as contingent, does not yet exist in determinate form. Only God, for whom all of time is an eternal present, sees tomorrow as clearly as today. Angels can forecast by reading causes: certain effects from necessary causes, probable effects from usual causes. But free choices and the convergence of independent causal chains remain opaque to every created mind. The future belongs to God alone.

refashioned:
Angels cannot know the secret thoughts and free choices of other minds. Thoughts exist in the will and intellect, and their expression depends on the will's free decision to manifest or conceal them. Only God, who is the direct cause and sustainer of every act of will, can see into the mind and know its hidden contents.

Angels can know a person's thoughts indirectly, by observing their external effects: facial expressions, bodily movements, physiological changes. But the inner act of the will itself, in its secret and free dimension, is inaccessible to any creature. This is why only God is called the searcher of hearts.
elegant: No angel can read another mind's secret thoughts. The inner act of the will is visible only to God, who sustains it from within. Angels can observe external signs, the body's betrayal of the soul's intentions. But the thought itself, in its private and free dimension, is locked behind a door that only the creator holds the key to. This is why Scripture reserves the title 'searcher of hearts' for God alone.

refashioned:
In this life, thoughts are hidden from others for two reasons: the opacity of the body and the concealment of the will. In the resurrection, bodily opacity will be removed, so the quality of a person's soul, its grace and glory, will be visible through the brightness of the body. But the will's free concealment remains, even for angels.

One angel can see the intelligible species in another angel's mind, since those species are natural endowments. But the actual use of those species, the free act of attending to this or thinking about that, is hidden until the angel chooses to reveal it.
elegant: In the resurrection, the quality of the soul will shine through the body, making grace and glory visible. But the will's free concealment remains. One angel can see the species in another angel's mind, since these are natural endowments. But what the angel does with those species, the free act of attending to this or choosing that, is hidden until freely revealed. Even in eternity, privacy is a feature of freedom.

refashioned:
Angels do not know the mysteries of grace by their natural powers. Mysteries of grace depend on God's free will, and the free choices of God's will are known only to God unless he reveals them. Since supernatural mysteries (the Incarnation, the sacraments, the divine plan of salvation) flow from God's free decision, no angel can know them naturally.

However, God does reveal mysteries of grace to angels. The mystery of the Incarnation, for example, was revealed to all angels from the beginning of their beatitude, since their ministry is ordered to it. Particular details and applications are revealed to different angels at different times according to God's plan.
elegant: The mysteries of grace are God's free decisions, and free decisions are unknowable to anyone the decider has not told. No angel, however brilliant, can deduce the Incarnation or the sacraments from natural principles. These are known only by revelation. Angelic genius has limits, and the limit is God's sovereign freedom. What God freely chooses, only God can disclose.

refashioned:
The Incarnation was revealed to all angels in general from the start, since their entire ministry is ordered to serving those who inherit salvation. But the particular details, timing, and circumstances were revealed progressively, as events unfolded. Higher angels know more of God's plan and communicate it to lower angels, who receive new insights as their missions require.

The essential point: angelic knowledge of grace is not natural but revealed. It is a gift, not an achievement of angelic intelligence.
elegant: The Incarnation was revealed to all angels in general, since their ministry is ordered to human salvation. But the details unfold progressively. Higher angels receive more of the divine plan and communicate it downward. Angelic knowledge of grace is not an achievement of intellect but a gift of disclosure. The brightest mind in creation still waits to be told what the creator has freely decided to do.

refashioned: Seven questions about how angels know: Is the angelic intellect sometimes potential, sometimes actual? Can an angel understand many things at once? Is angelic knowledge discursive? Do angels compose and divide concepts? Can there be falsehood in angelic intellect? Can angelic knowledge be called "morning" and "evening"? And are morning and evening knowledge the same?
elegant: How does an angelic mind operate? Seven questions probe whether it has potential and actual states, whether it grasps many things at once, whether it reasons step by step, whether it forms judgments, whether it can err, and what it means to speak of 'morning' and 'evening' knowledge.

refashioned:
The angelic intellect is always in act with respect to its natural knowledge. Unlike the human intellect, which moves from potentiality to actuality as it learns, the angel possesses all its natural knowledge from the moment of creation. It does not need to acquire knowledge step by step.

However, this does not mean the angel has no potentiality at all in its knowledge. With respect to supernatural knowledge, what God reveals over time, the angelic intellect can receive what it did not previously possess. And even with respect to natural knowledge, the angel may not always be actively considering everything it knows. It knows everything habitually but attends to particular objects by choice.
elegant: The angelic intellect does not learn. It arrives in existence fully furnished. Unlike the human mind, which moves from ignorance to knowledge step by step, the angel possesses all its natural knowledge from the first instant. But even an angel can attend or not attend. It knows everything habitually; it considers particular objects by choice. The library is complete, but the reader still turns the pages.

refashioned:
An angel can understand many things simultaneously, provided they are grasped through a single intelligible species. Since a single angelic species can represent many things (especially for higher angels, whose species are more universal), the angel can know all those things at once. But if multiple objects require multiple, distinct species, the angel must attend to them successively.

This differs from the human intellect, which can understand only one thing at a time through one species. The angel's greater intellectual power allows simultaneous knowledge of whatever falls under a single comprehensive form. The scope of simultaneous knowledge varies with the angel's rank: higher angels, with more universal species, can grasp more in a single act.
elegant: An angel can know many things at once, provided they fall under a single intelligible species. One comprehensive form can illuminate an entire domain in a single act of understanding. The scope of simultaneous knowledge increases with the angel's rank: the higher the intellect, the more universal the species, and the more that can be grasped in one glance. What we piece together over time, a higher angel sees whole.

refashioned:
Angelic knowledge is not discursive. Discursive reasoning means moving from one known truth to another, as when we move from premises to conclusion in a syllogism. Angels do not reason this way. They see conclusions immediately in the principles, without needing to work through intermediate steps.

This is not because angels are incapable of grasping complex truths. It is because their intellect is powerful enough to see the whole logical structure at once. A human mind must build a chain of reasoning; an angelic mind sees the entire chain in a single glance. They know effects in their causes and conclusions in their principles simultaneously, not sequentially.

Angels do know some things through other things. They know creatures through the divine ideas. But this is not discursive, because they see the cause and the effect together, not one after the other.
elegant: Angels do not reason. They see. Where the human mind builds a chain of inferences, stepping from premise to premise, the angelic mind sees the entire logical structure in a single act. Conclusions are visible in their principles, effects in their causes, all at once. Discursive thought is the footpath of a finite mind that cannot see the whole landscape from one vantage point. The angel stands at the summit.

refashioned:
Angels do not understand by composing and dividing, that is, by forming judgments that combine subject and predicate the way we do. Human intellect first grasps simple concepts and then combines them into propositions. The angelic intellect grasps the complete truth about a thing in a single act, without needing to construct it from parts.

However, angels can form propositions if they choose to. They are not incapable of compositional thought. They simply do not need it as a step toward understanding. They already see the whole truth that compositional thought would laboriously assemble.
elegant: Angels do not need to assemble truths from parts. They grasp the complete truth about a thing in one act, without composing subject and predicate the way we do. They can form propositions if they choose, but they do not need to. The whole truth that compositional thought laboriously constructs is already visible to them in a single, undivided apprehension.

refashioned:
There can be no falsehood in the angel's natural knowledge. With respect to what angels know naturally, through their innate species, they cannot err, because these species come directly from God and represent things as they truly are. Natural knowledge is infallible.

However, angels can err in matters that exceed their natural knowledge. When they make judgments about future contingents, about the hidden thoughts of other minds, or about mysteries of grace, they can be mistaken. Demons, in particular, are subject to a kind of intellectual perversity: not that their natural knowledge is false, but that they misapply it, forming erroneous opinions about matters beyond their natural competence. Their error is not in seeing but in overreaching.
elegant: An angel's natural knowledge cannot be wrong. Its innate species come from God and represent things as they truly are. But beyond the boundaries of natural knowledge, in questions of free future events, hidden thoughts, and divine mysteries, even angels can err. Demons especially overreach, forming false opinions about matters that exceed their competence. Their error is not in seeing but in presuming to see what has not been shown to them.

refashioned:
Augustine distinguishes two kinds of angelic knowledge: "morning knowledge" and "evening knowledge." Morning knowledge is knowledge of things as they exist in the Word of God, in God's eternal creative plan. Evening knowledge is knowledge of things as they exist in their own created natures.

These are two modes of knowing the same objects, not knowledge of two different sets of objects. The angel sees a creature in God's mind (morning knowledge) and also sees that same creature in its own created existence (evening knowledge). The distinction is one of perspective: from above, as God conceives it, or from below, as it exists in itself.
elegant: Augustine names two modes of angelic knowledge: morning and evening. Morning knowledge sees creatures as they exist in God's eternal plan; evening knowledge sees them as they exist in their own created natures. These are two angles on the same reality. The angel looks up and sees the blueprint. It looks down and sees the building. Both views are true. The higher one is called morning because it is the clearer light.

refashioned:
Morning and evening knowledge are distinct but not unrelated. Augustine correlates them with the six days of creation: each "day" represents a class of things known by the angels first in God (morning) and then in themselves (evening). The angel always exercises morning knowledge, since it always sees things in God. But it also simultaneously or subsequently knows things in their own natures.

The key theological point: when evening knowledge is referred back to God's glory, it becomes the "morning" of the next day. That is, natural knowledge of creatures is perfected when it leads back to praise of the Creator. Knowledge of things in themselves is not bad, but it finds its completion in returning to God.
elegant: Morning knowledge and evening knowledge are related as source and reflection. The angel knows creatures in God's Word, then knows them in themselves. But the evening knowledge finds its morning when it returns what it has seen to the praise of the creator. Knowledge of the world is not its own end. It is perfected when it circles back to wonder at the one who made the world.

refashioned: Four questions about angelic will: Do angels have a will? Is the will distinct from the intellect and nature? Do angels have free will? And do they have irascible and concupiscible appetites?
elegant: From angelic intellect we turn to angelic will. Four questions: Do angels have a will? Is it identical with intellect or nature? Do they possess free will? And do they experience anything like desire and passion?

refashioned:
Angels have a will. Will follows from intellect. Any being that understands the good necessarily has an appetite directed toward the good as such, and that rational appetite is will. Since angels are intellectual beings, they must have will.

The objection that will implies unfulfilled desire, and therefore imperfection, confuses will with wanting. Will in its fullest sense is not restless craving but the intellectual appetite's positive orientation toward the good. Even a being that possesses the good still wills it; the will rests in what it has. So the presence of will in angels is not a sign of deficiency but of intellectual nature operating at full capacity.
elegant: Any being that understands the good must want it. Will is not restless craving; it is the intelligent appetite's orientation toward what is truly good. Even a being that possesses the good still wills it: the will rests in what it holds. Angels have will not because they lack something but because they know something, and knowing the good means being drawn toward it.

refashioned:
In angels, the will is really distinct from the intellect, just as the intellect is distinct from the angelic nature. The angel's nature is its essence. Its intellect is the power of knowing that flows from that essence. Its will is the power of desiring the good that flows from knowing it. These are distinct faculties with distinct acts: knowing and willing are not the same operation, even though they are inseparable in any intellectual being.

The natural inclination that comes from the angel's form (nature) is one thing. The inclination that follows from intellectual apprehension of the good (will) is another. Both are present in the angel, but they operate at different levels.
elegant: In the angel, nature, intellect, and will are three real and distinct things. Nature is the essence. Intellect is the power of knowing that flows from it. Will is the power of desiring the good that flows from knowing it. They are inseparable but not identical, the way a spring, its light, and its warmth are one source with different gifts.

refashioned:
Angels have free will. Free will is the capacity to choose between alternatives in light of an intellectual judgment. Since angels have intellect and will, they can consider different goods and choose among them.

The objection that free will requires deliberation, and angels do not deliberate discursively, confuses the process with the capacity. Free will does not require step-by-step reasoning. It requires only that the agent be able to choose between goods that are not absolutely compelling. Since no created good is the infinite good, no created good compels an angelic will. The angel can always choose otherwise with respect to any finite good.

With respect to happiness in general, the will moves necessarily: every intellectual being necessarily wills to be happy. But with respect to particular goods that are not identical with happiness itself, the will is free.
elegant: Angels are free. Free will does not require slow deliberation; it requires only that the agent face alternatives that are not absolutely compelling. Since no created good is the infinite good, no finite option forces an angel's hand. The angel must will happiness in general. But in the realm of particular goods, it chooses, and its choice is its own.

refashioned:
Angels do not have irascible and concupiscible appetites in the proper sense. These are sense appetites, powers tied to bodily organs. The irascible appetite responds to difficulty and threat; the concupiscible appetite responds to pleasure and attraction. Both involve physiological change, which angels, as incorporeal beings, cannot undergo.

When Scripture attributes fury or desire to demons, it speaks metaphorically, describing the effects of their will in terms borrowed from human emotional experience. The demons do not literally feel rage or lust. Their will pursues certain goals with an intensity that resembles these passions, but without any bodily component.
elegant: Angels do not feel rage or lust. These are passions of the body, and angels have no bodies. When Scripture speaks of demonic fury or desire, it borrows the language of human emotion to describe the effects of a purely immaterial will. The demon does not literally seethe. Its will pursues its object with a ferocity that resembles passion but operates without pulse or temperature.

refashioned:
When love, joy, anger, or desire are attributed to angels or demons, these terms refer to acts of the intellectual will, not to bodily passions. Love in an angel is simply willing the good of another. Joy is the will resting in a good possessed. These are not emotions in the human sense. They involve no physiological arousal, no bodily disturbance. They are pure acts of an immaterial will.

The universal appetite of the will, which desires the good as such, encompasses everything that the irascible and concupiscible appetites pursue in embodied creatures, but it does so without the division into separate bodily powers.
elegant: Love in an angel is not an emotion. It is the will's act of embracing a good. Joy is the will's rest in what it holds. These are not softer than human feelings.
