They are purer: the same movements, stripped of every physiological distraction, enacted in the clarity of pure intellect. Angelic love is not less than passion. It is passion purified to its essence.

refashioned: Five questions about angelic love: Is there natural love in angels? Is there elective love? Does an angel love itself naturally and by choice? Does it love other angels naturally as it loves itself? And does it love God more than itself by natural love?
elegant: Five questions about what angels love: Is there natural love in them? Elective love? Do they love themselves? Do they love one another? And do they love God more than themselves?

refashioned:
There is natural love in angels. Every being has a natural inclination toward what is good for it, and this inclination, in an intellectual being, is a form of love. The angel naturally loves its own existence, its own perfection, and the good that fulfills its nature. This natural love is not a choice; it is a built-in orientation of the will toward what is naturally fitting.

The objection that natural love is opposed to intellectual love confuses nature with mere instinct. In intellectual beings, the natural inclination of the will is itself intellectual. The angel's natural love is not a blind impulse but an intelligent orientation toward the good, grounded in its knowledge of its own nature.
elegant: Natural love in an angel is not instinct or blind impulse. It is the intelligent will's built-in orientation toward what fulfills its nature. Every being, by the mere fact of existing, inclines toward its own good. In an intellectual being, this inclination is conscious and luminous. The angel's natural love is not a force acting on it but a direction woven into it by its maker.

refashioned:
Angels also have elective love, love that arises from free choice rather than natural inclination. Natural love is directed toward the end itself: the good as such. Elective love is directed toward means to the end, or toward particular goods that are not necessitated by nature.

Since angels have free will, they can love things not merely because nature inclines them but because they freely judge something to be good and choose it. This is how angels can love supernatural goods, goods that exceed their natural orientation. It is also how fallen angels can love disordered goods, by freely choosing what does not accord with right order.
elegant: Angels also love by choice, not only by nature. Natural love points toward the end: the good as such. Elective love selects among means and particular goods. This is how angels can love what exceeds their natural orientation, including supernatural goods. It is also how fallen angels can love what nature never intended: by freely choosing what defies the order they were built to serve.

refashioned:
An angel loves itself with both natural love and elective love. By natural love, every being is inclined toward its own good and its own preservation. This is not selfishness; it is the basic orientation of any nature toward its own fulfillment. By elective love, the angel can also freely choose goods for itself, willing particular perfections and activities.

These two loves of self are not opposed. Natural self-love provides the foundation; elective self-love builds upon it by freely pursuing specific goods. A sinful angel's self-love becomes disordered not because self-love is inherently wrong, but because the angel chooses itself as its ultimate end rather than ordering itself toward God.
elegant: An angel loves itself both naturally and by choice, and this is not vanity but structure. Natural self-love is the basic orientation of any being toward its own fulfillment. Elective self-love is the free pursuit of particular goods. The two reinforce each other in a rightly ordered will. They war against each other only when the angel makes itself its own ultimate end, which is the precise architecture of the fall.

refashioned:
An angel naturally loves other angels as it loves itself, insofar as they share the same general nature. Just as every part of a whole naturally loves the whole and the other parts, so each angel, as a member of the angelic community and of the universe, naturally loves the other members. This natural love extends to all who share in intellectual nature, including human beings.

The depth of natural love corresponds to the degree of shared nature. An angel loves other angels more naturally than it loves material creatures, because the likeness is greater. But all natural love of others is ultimately rooted in the natural love of the common good, which encompasses everyone.
elegant: An angel naturally loves other angels as parts of a shared whole. Every part loves the whole of which it is a member and, through the whole, loves the other parts. Angels share intellectual nature. They share a universe. They share a creator. Natural love of others is not altruism learned from experience but solidarity built into being itself.

refashioned:
By natural love, an angel loves God more than it loves itself. This is the most important claim in the whole discussion of angelic love. Every part naturally loves the whole more than itself. A hand naturally moves to protect the body even at cost to itself. Similarly, every creature naturally loves God, the universal good and source of all being, more than its own private good.

This is true even in the order of nature, apart from grace. The angel's natural orientation is toward God as the origin and end of all things. Self-love, properly ordered, is subordinate to love of God. The angel that loves itself rightly loves itself as something that exists for God's sake.
elegant: By nature, an angel loves God more than itself. This is the most important claim in the entire discussion of angelic love. A hand moves to protect the body even at cost to itself; a part naturally loves the whole more than its own private existence. God is not one being among many. He is the whole of which every creature is a part. Self-love, properly ordered, is a subset of the love of God.

refashioned:
The objection that things love what is closer over what is further does not apply here, because God is not just another being alongside the angel. God is the cause of the angel's very existence and goodness. When one thing is the total cause of another's being, the caused thing naturally loves the cause more than itself. The part loves the whole more than itself because the part depends entirely on the whole for its existence and meaning.

The objection that natural love is self-interested, that we love the good "for ourselves," does not prove that the angel loves itself more than God. It proves only that the angel desires God as its good. But desiring God as your ultimate good means loving God more than yourself, not less, because your goodness depends entirely on God's.
elegant: The objection that we love what is closer more than what is far away misreads the geometry. God is not far away. God is the ground on which the angel stands. When one thing is the total cause of another's being, the caused thing naturally loves the cause more than itself. Desiring God as your ultimate good does not mean using God for yourself. It means recognizing that your goodness is a fraction of his.

refashioned: Having treated angelic nature, knowledge, and will, we now consider how angels were produced. Three topics: their natural existence, their perfection in grace and glory, and the fall of some into sin. On natural production, four questions: Do angels have a cause? Were they produced from eternity? Were they created before the material world? And were they created in the empyrean heaven?
elegant: How did angels come to be? Four questions: Do they have a cause? Were they made from eternity? Were they created before or with the material world? And where were they placed at creation?

refashioned:
Angels have a cause of their existence: God created them. Although immaterial forms might seem to be self-sufficient (since they have no matter that needs a cause), every being whose essence is distinct from its existence requires a cause. Only God is his own existence. Every angel receives its existence from God, just as every other creature does.

The absence of explicit mention of angels in Genesis does not mean they were not created. Scripture's account of creation is adapted to the understanding of its original audience, and purely spiritual beings may be included implicitly or under symbolic language.
elegant: Angels are creatures. Though immaterial and seemingly self-sufficient, every being whose essence is distinct from its existence owes that existence to another. Only God is his own existence. Every angel, however exalted, receives its being as a gift. The absence of matter does not exempt a thing from needing a maker; it only means the maker works without raw material.

refashioned:
Angels were not produced from eternity. Only God is eternal. Even though God's creative power is eternal, its effect, the angel's existence, begins when God wills it to begin. An eternal cause can produce a temporal effect if the cause acts by free will rather than by natural necessity.

Angels are above time in the sense that they are not measured by the continuous time of physical change. But they are not above every kind of temporal beginning. They began to exist at the first moment of creation, entering a mode of duration called aeviternity, which has a beginning but no end.
elegant: Angels were not made from eternity. An eternal cause can produce a temporal effect when the cause acts by free choice. God's power is eternal, but his decision to create takes effect when he wills it. Angels are above ordinary time, existing in a duration that has a beginning but no end. But they are not above having a beginning. Only God has no starting line.

refashioned:
The question of whether angels were created before the corporeal world was debated. Some held that spiritual creation preceded material creation. Others held that both were created simultaneously.

The more defensible position is that angels were created at the same time as the material world. This fits the view that God created all things together in a single act. It also reflects the principle that parts of a whole are created together. Angels and material creatures are parts of one universe, and the universe, as a whole, was brought into being at once.

However, this remains a question on which authoritative opinions differ, and no definitive doctrinal resolution has been reached.
elegant: Were angels made before the physical world or alongside it? The stronger view is that both came into being together, parts of a single creation. Spirit and matter are not rival kingdoms produced in sequence but complementary dimensions of one universe, brought forth in a single creative act. But honest minds have differed on this, and certainty belongs to revelation more than to argument.

refashioned:
Angels were created in the empyrean heaven, the highest corporeal place. Although angels do not depend on place for their existence, it was fitting that the noblest corporeal place should correspond to the noblest creatures. The empyrean heaven, understood as the place of divine glory, is where the angels first exercised their contemplation of God.

This does not mean angels are confined to the empyrean heaven. As spiritual beings, they can be present wherever they exercise their power. But their creation in the highest place symbolizes their role as the highest creatures, closest to God in the order of being.
elegant: Angels were created in the highest heaven, the place of divine glory. Not because spirits need a location, but because it was fitting that the noblest creatures should correspond to the noblest place. The assignment is symbolic as much as literal: the highest beings begin in the highest light, closest to the source of all splendor.

refashioned: Nine questions about angelic grace and glory: Were angels created in beatitude? Did they need grace to turn to God? Were they created in grace? Did they merit their beatitude? Did they achieve it immediately? Did they receive grace proportional to their natural gifts? Do natural knowledge and love remain after beatification? Can a beatified angel sin? And can beatified angels grow in blessedness?
elegant: Nine questions about how angels were perfected beyond their nature: Were they made blessed from the start? Did they need grace? Were they given it at creation? Did they merit their glory? How quickly? In what proportion? Does nature persist in glory? Can the blessed fall? And can they grow further?

refashioned:
Angels were not created in the full beatitude of seeing God. They were created in a state of natural perfection, with natural happiness, but not yet in the supernatural blessedness that comes from the direct vision of God's essence. That beatitude required a further step: the angel's free turning toward God, aided by grace.

If angels had been created already beatified, the fallen angels could never have fallen, since beatitude, once possessed, cannot be lost. Since some angels did fall, they had not yet attained final beatitude at the moment of creation.
elegant: Angels were not created in full beatitude. They were created perfect in nature but not yet in glory. Beatitude, the direct vision of God, was not their starting condition but their destination. The journey required a free choice, aided by grace. If they had begun already blessed, no angel could ever have fallen, since beatitude, once possessed, cannot be lost.

refashioned: The "beatitude" attributed to angels at creation refers to their natural perfection, not to the supernatural vision of God. Just as corporeal creatures did not instantly achieve their full operational perfection but only the seeds of it, so the angels at creation possessed the perfection of their nature but not yet the perfection of glory. Natural perfection was the starting point; supernatural beatitude was the goal.
elegant: The 'perfection' of creation is the perfection of a seed, not of a harvest. Angels received the fullness of their nature at creation, just as the physical world received its natural principles. But supernatural glory, the final flowering, awaited their free cooperation with grace. Nature is the starting line. Glory is the finish.

refashioned:
Angels needed grace in order to turn to God in a way that merits supernatural beatitude. Natural love of God is real but insufficient. By nature, the angel loves God as the author of its natural being. But supernatural beatitude means seeing God's own essence, which surpasses any natural capacity. To orient itself toward this higher end, the angel needed a supernatural gift, grace, that elevated its will beyond its natural reach.

The fact that there was no obstacle in the angel's nature does not eliminate the need for grace. Grace is needed not because the task is difficult in the sense of requiring effort, but because it exceeds what any natural power can achieve. Flying is not difficult for a bird, but it is impossible for a stone, no matter how smooth the path.
elegant: Grace was needed because the destination exceeds the traveler's natural capacity. An angel can love God naturally as the author of its being. But seeing God face to face is another matter entirely. No created power can bridge the gap between knowing God through a likeness and knowing God as he is. Grace is the supernatural bridge, not a concession to weakness but an elevation beyond what nature, however perfect, can reach on its own.

refashioned:
The angel loves God naturally insofar as God is the source of its natural being. But turning to God as the source of supernatural beatitude requires grace. "Difficulty" here does not mean resistance or struggle. It means exceeding the natural capacity. Without grace, the supernatural end is not merely hard but impossible, as impossible as flight for a creature without wings.

Grace does not override the angel's natural love of God. It elevates it, redirecting the angel's already-existing love toward a higher object: God known not merely as cause but as he is in himself.
elegant: The angel's natural love of God is real but insufficient for beatitude. Grace does not correct nature; it elevates it. Without grace, the supernatural vision of God is not merely difficult but impossible, the way flight is impossible for a creature without wings, no matter how favorable the terrain. Grace gives the wings.

refashioned:
The most probable view is that angels were created in grace. They were not first created in a purely natural state and then later given grace. Rather, God gave them grace at the moment of creation, as a seed that would flower into glory if they freely cooperated.

This fits the general principle that God equips each creature from the start with what it needs for its proper perfection. Just as he gave nature its principles at creation, so he gave the angels grace at creation. The angels did not earn this initial grace; it was a gift accompanying their nature.
elegant: Angels were created already in grace, not in a purely natural state that was later supplemented. God equips each creature from the start with what it needs for its proper end. Grace was given at creation as a seed of glory, a gift awaiting the angel's free cooperation. They did not earn this first grace. They were simply handed it along with their being.

refashioned:
Angels merited their beatitude. Although they received grace freely, they had to use it freely to merit the reward of glory. Merit requires a free act directed toward the end, aided by grace. The angels who remained faithful freely cooperated with grace and thereby earned their eternal blessedness.

The objection that angels faced no difficulty, and therefore no merit, misunderstands merit. Merit does not require suffering or struggle. It requires a free act of the will, empowered by grace, directed toward an end that exceeds natural capacity. The angel's free choice to love God above all, made in the first moment of its moral life, was genuinely meritorious.
elegant: Angels merited their beatitude through a free act of will empowered by grace. Merit does not require suffering. It requires a free choice directed toward an end that exceeds nature, made possible by a power that exceeds nature. The angel's free decision to love God above all else, made with the full clarity of its intellect and the full force of its will, was genuinely meritorious. Freedom and grace together produced the glory.

refashioned:
Angels obtained beatitude immediately after one act of merit. They did not need a long process of repeated meritorious acts the way humans do. The reason is that angels do not learn gradually or reason discursively. In a single act, the angel grasped and chose its ultimate end with the full force of its intellect and will. One complete act was sufficient.

This makes sense given the angelic mode of operation. Humans need time because our knowledge and willing develop incrementally. Angels operate with an intensity and comprehensiveness that allows a single act to accomplish what we achieve over a lifetime. The angel's first free act was also its definitive act.
elegant: One act was enough. Angels do not learn gradually or deliberate step by step. In a single, comprehensive act, the angel grasped its ultimate end and chose it with everything it had. What takes a human lifetime of repeated choices, an angel accomplished in one luminous instant. The angel's first free act was also its definitive act. One choice, and the matter was settled forever.

refashioned:
Angels received grace and glory in proportion to their natural gifts. The more perfect the angel's nature, the more grace it received, and the greater its resulting glory. This is not because nature earns grace, but because God, in his wisdom, proportions his supernatural gifts to the natural capacity of the recipient. A larger vessel receives more, not because the vessel deserves it, but because the giver wisely fills each according to its capacity.

This means the hierarchy of glory among the angels mirrors the hierarchy of nature. The highest angels by nature are also the highest in grace and glory. Natural perfection, grace, and glory form a single ascending order, all flowing from God's unified creative and sanctifying plan.
elegant: God proportioned grace and glory to natural capacity. The higher the angel's nature, the more grace it received, and the greater its resulting beatitude. A larger vessel receives more, not because it earned the filling but because the giver wisely matches the gift to the receiver. The hierarchy of glory mirrors the hierarchy of nature. Nature, grace, and glory form a single ascending scale, tuned by one hand.

refashioned:
Natural knowledge and love remain in the beatified angels. Glory does not destroy nature but perfects it. The angel's natural knowledge of itself, of other angels, and of God as cause continues in the state of beatitude. Supernatural knowledge (seeing God's essence) is added on top of natural knowledge, not substituted for it.

Similarly, natural love, the angel's innate orientation toward its own good and toward God as its source, persists alongside the supernatural love of charity. The two are not in competition. Natural love is taken up into and elevated by supernatural love, like a candle flame that continues to burn even when the sun rises.
elegant: Glory does not destroy nature; it crowns it. The angel's natural knowledge and natural love persist in the beatific state, taken up into and elevated by supernatural vision and supernatural charity. Grace is not a replacement but a completion. The candle keeps burning even after the sun rises. Natural love of God becomes the foundation on which supernatural love builds its cathedral.

refashioned:
A beatified angel cannot sin. Once an angel has attained the direct vision of God, its will is permanently fixed on God as the supreme good. Seeing God as he truly is removes every possibility of choosing something else as an ultimate end. There is no alternative that could appear more attractive, because the angel sees the infinite good itself.

Free will remains, but it operates within the orientation established by the vision of God. The angel is free in the sense that it can choose among various goods. But it cannot choose against God, because no finite good, seen in the light of the infinite good, could ever appear worth preferring. This is not a limitation of freedom but its perfection: freedom fully directed by the truth.
elegant: A beatified angel cannot sin. Once the will sees the infinite good face to face, no finite good can compete. Free will remains, but it operates within an orientation that the truth itself has made unshakable. This is not the loss of freedom but its perfection: freedom fully informed by the truth, fully directed by the good, with nothing left to rebel against because nothing better than what it has could ever be conceived.

refashioned:
Beatified angels do not advance in beatitude. Their blessedness is final and complete. Merit belongs to the state of moving toward an end, not to the state of having arrived. Once the angel possesses God in the beatific vision, there is no further end to move toward, and therefore no further merit to earn.

This does not mean the angel's blessedness is static in the sense of being boring or empty. It means the angel possesses the fullness of what it was created for. There is no lack to be remedied, no further degree of perfection to be attained. The angel rests in God as its complete and final good.
elegant: Beatified angels do not advance in blessedness. Merit belongs to the journey, not to the destination. The angel that has arrived possesses the fullness of what it was created for. There is no further peak to climb, no additional degree to attain. Its rest is not stagnation but completion: the permanent, unimprovable possession of the infinite good.

refashioned: Merit belongs to those who are still on the way to their end. A being that has already arrived at its ultimate end does not merit but simply possesses. The beatified angel has reached its final state and rests there. Its acts of service, such as ministering to human beings, proceed from its already-complete blessedness and are not ways of earning additional reward. The angel's charity in glory is perfect charity, and perfect charity is not a means to an end but the end itself, fully realized.
elegant: A being that has reached its ultimate end does not earn; it possesses. The beatified angel's acts of service flow from completed blessedness, not toward it. Its charity is not a means to glory but glory in action. Perfect love does not strive upward. It radiates outward.

refashioned: Nine questions about how angels became evil: Can angels sin? What kinds of sin are possible for them? What did the devil seek in sinning? Are any demons naturally evil? Was the devil evil from the first instant of his creation? Was there an interval between creation and the fall? Was the highest-ranking sinner the highest of all angels? Did his sin cause the others to sin? And did as many angels sin as remained faithful?
elegant: How did some angels fall? Nine questions trace the mechanics of the most catastrophic free choice ever made: its possibility, its nature, its object, its origin, its timing, and its scope.

refashioned:
Angels can sin. Although their nature is purely intellectual and their natural knowledge is infallible, they have free will, and free will carries the possibility of choosing wrongly. The evil possible in angels is not a corruption of their nature (which remains intact even in demons) but a disorder of their free choice.

The key distinction is between natural operation and free operation. Natural angelic operations cannot go wrong, just as the motions of heavenly bodies cannot err. But free operations, which go beyond what nature determines, can deviate from right order. Sin in angels is the misuse of freedom, not the corruption of nature.
elegant: Angels can sin because they have free will, and free will, in any creature, carries the possibility of choosing wrongly. Their nature is perfect. Their natural knowledge is infallible. But perfection of nature does not guarantee perfection of choice. Sin in angels is not the corruption of substance but the misuse of freedom. It enters not through the back door of ignorance but through the front door of volition.

refashioned: There is no potentiality in angels for natural deficiency, but there is potentiality in their will for disordered choice. That is where evil can enter. Their natural knowledge is infallible, their natural tendencies are good, but their free will can orient itself wrongly. Even a perfect intellect with perfect natural knowledge can choose to prefer its own good over the universal good, and that is precisely what the fallen angels did.
elegant: There is no flaw in the angel's nature that explains the fall. The flaw is in the will's orientation. Natural knowledge is infallible; natural tendencies are good. But the will can orient itself toward its own private good in defiance of the universal order. Even a being with perfect sight can choose to look the wrong way.

refashioned:
Only sins of pride and envy can exist in angels. Sins that involve bodily pleasure, sensory desire, or material attachment are impossible for incorporeal beings. Angels have no bodies and therefore no capacity for lust, gluttony, or sloth in the physical sense.

Pride and envy are spiritual sins. Pride is the disordered desire for one's own excellence. Envy is sorrow at another's good, insofar as it diminishes one's own relative standing. These do not require a body. They require only a will that can orient itself toward its own private good in defiance of the universal order.

When demons are said to delight in carnal sins, this is not because they experience physical pleasure. They delight in the disorder, in the human degradation and the rebellion against God that such sins represent.
elegant: Only pride and envy can corrupt a being without a body. Lust, gluttony, and sloth require flesh. But the desire for one's own excellence and the resentment of another's, these are purely spiritual disorders. They need no nerve endings, no hormones, no appetite. They need only a will that prefers itself to the order it was made to serve.

refashioned: Did the devil desire to be God? This requires careful analysis. There are two ways to desire equality with God: literally, by wanting to be identical with God, and by likeness, by wanting to be like God in some specific way.
elegant: Did the devil want to be God? The question demands precision. There are two ways to seek equality with the divine.

refashioned:
The devil sinned by desiring to be like God, not by desiring literal equality with God. The angel knew, by natural knowledge, that becoming God was impossible. No creature can become infinite, and any angel understood this. Furthermore, even if it were possible, no creature would naturally desire it, because becoming a different nature means ceasing to be what you are. An ass does not desire to be a horse, because if it became one, it would no longer exist.

So the devil's sin was subtler. He desired likeness to God in a specific way: he wanted to achieve his final happiness through his own power rather than through God's grace. He wanted to be the source of his own blessedness, to be self-sufficient in the way only God is self-sufficient. He wanted the goal (likeness to God) by the wrong means (his own power rather than God's gift).

Alternatively, he desired to have dominion and excellence beyond what God's order assigned him, wanting to rule others as God rules, by his own authority rather than under God's. Either way, the sin was not wanting something impossible but wanting something by a means that was not his to claim. He sought to be an end unto himself, which is God's prerogative alone.
elegant: The devil did not seek the impossible. He knew he could not become God. No creature would even want that, since becoming a different nature means ceasing to be yourself. His sin was subtler: he wanted to reach his final happiness by his own power rather than by grace. He wanted to be self-sufficient in the way only God is self-sufficient, to be an end unto himself. He sought the right destination by the wrong road, and the wrong road led to the only place it could.

refashioned:
No demon is naturally evil. Every nature, as created by God, is good. Evil in angels comes entirely from the misuse of free will, not from any defect in their created nature. The demonic nature remains as good as the angelic nature it was created to be. What is corrupted is the will, not the substance.

The claim that demons are "naturally" crafty or deceitful confuses nature with habit. Their deceptiveness is a consequence of their sinful orientation, not a feature of their original design. God does not create evil natures. He creates good natures that freely chose evil.
elegant: No demon is evil by nature. Every nature, as God made it, is good. The demonic substance is as good today as the day it was created. What is corrupted is the will, not the being. God does not manufacture evil. He manufactures freedom, and freedom, in the hands of a creature, can be turned against its maker.

refashioned: Was the devil evil from the very first instant of his creation? Some say yes: that the instant he was made, he sinned. Others say there was at least a brief interval in which he was good before he fell.
elegant: Was the devil evil from the very first instant of existence? Or was there a moment, however brief, of original goodness before the fall?

refashioned:
The devil was not evil in the first instant of his creation. He was created good, with a good nature and in a state of grace. His sin came afterward, though without any measurable delay in our time.

The reasoning: the angel's first act must have been a natural act, and natural acts in angels are always good. The angel's nature inclines it toward the good. Its first movement, arising from its nature before any free choice intervenes, was necessarily good. Sin required a second act, a free choice that departed from the natural orientation toward God.

Scripture supports this: "You were perfect in your ways from the day of your creation, until iniquity was found in you." The devil was created perfect and then fell. His fall was not instantaneous with his creation but followed it, however closely.
elegant: The devil was not evil at creation. His first act was a natural act, and natural acts in angels are necessarily good. The fall came after, in the very next moment of deliberation. Scripture confirms it: 'You were perfect in your ways from the day of your creation, until iniquity was found in you.' He was made whole and then broke himself.

refashioned:
When Scripture says the devil "sins from the beginning," it means from the beginning of his sin, not from the beginning of his existence. He never repented, so his sin has been continuous from the moment it began. But that moment was not the moment of his creation.

The distinction between light and darkness in the creation narrative, as Augustine reads it, refers to God's foreknowledge of which angels would stand and which would fall, not to their being created in two different moral states.
elegant: When Scripture says the devil 'sins from the beginning,' it means from the beginning of his sinning, not from the beginning of his being. He never repented, so his sin has been continuous from its inception. But inception and creation are not the same moment. The fall followed creation, however closely it followed.

refashioned:
There was no extended interval between the angel's creation and his fall. The angel's intellectual and volitional operations are not measured by continuous time the way ours are. The angel was created, performed one good act (its natural first act), and then in the very next instant of its deliberation, fell.

The idea that the angel "walked in perfection" for a long time before falling reads too much into the metaphor. Angelic time is not our time. What might be described as a sequence from one perspective happened with a swiftness that defies temporal measurement. Creation, the first good act, and then the fall, all in rapid succession, with no prolonged period of innocence between.
elegant: There was no prolonged age of angelic innocence. The interval between creation and fall defies temporal measurement. Creation, one good act, then the fall, all in a succession so swift it cannot be mapped onto human time. The angel was not tested over millennia. It was tested in an instant, and in that instant it chose, and the choice was permanent.

refashioned:
The highest angel among those who sinned was indeed the highest of all the angels. His sin was pride, and pride is proportional to excellence. The most gifted angel had the most to be proud of, and his pride was accordingly the greatest. Scripture's references to the fallen angel as the "anointed cherub" and the bearer of the "seal of resemblance" suggest a being of supreme natural endowment.

This does not mean that being the highest angel caused the fall. It means that the occasion for pride was greatest in the most excellent being. The highest angel had the most reason to be satisfied with his own power and the strongest temptation to seek self-sufficiency rather than dependence on God.
elegant: The highest angel fell the hardest. Pride is proportional to excellence. The most gifted being had the most to be proud of, and so his pride was the greatest. He was the anointed cherub, the seal of resemblance, the summit of created perfection. And he looked at what he was and decided it was enough, that he did not need what he had not given himself. The tallest tree catches the most wind.

refashioned:
The sin of the highest angel was the cause of the other angels' sin, not by compulsion but by persuasion and influence. The other fallen angels chose to follow his lead freely. They sinned through the same kind of pride, desiring to be subject to the highest angel in imitation of his self-exaltation rather than remaining subject to God.

This parallels how sin works among humans: one person's bad example and influence leads others into similar choices, without removing their responsibility. The leader's sin did not necessitate the followers' sin. Each fallen angel chose independently, but the leader's rebellion provided the occasion and the model.
elegant: The leader's sin drew others after him, not by compulsion but by influence. The fallen angels chose freely to follow, seduced by the same pride, modeling their rebellion on his. Sin among angels works the way it works among humans: one bad example opens a door, and others walk through it of their own accord. The leader provided the occasion. Each follower provided the consent.

refashioned:
More angels remained faithful than fell. Sin is contrary to the natural inclination of intellectual beings, which is toward the good. Since angels have perfect natural knowledge and a strong natural orientation toward God, the majority followed that orientation. The fallen angels are the exception, not the rule.

Scripture supports this indirectly. When it speaks of angelic hosts and their vast numbers, the implication is that the heavenly armies far outnumber the fallen. Evil, being a privation and a departure from nature, is always in the minority when measured against the created order's fundamental orientation toward the good.
elegant: More angels stood than fell. Sin is a departure from the natural order, and departures are, by nature, the minority. Intellectual beings are built for the good, and most of them followed that built-in compass. The fallen are the exception. Evil, being privation and defiance of nature, can never outnumber the good, because the good is what nature tends toward and evil is what nature recoils from.

refashioned: Four questions about the punishment of demons: Is their intellect darkened? Is their will fixed in evil? Do they experience sorrow? And is our atmosphere their place of punishment?
elegant: What became of the fallen? Four questions about the punishment of demons: the darkening of their minds, the fixation of their wills, the sorrow they endure, and the strange geography of their exile.

refashioned: Are demons deprived of all knowledge of truth? If they retained any knowledge, would that not be a kind of happiness? But total ignorance seems inconsistent with their nature as intellectual beings.
elegant: Are demons stripped of all knowledge? If they retain any truth, does that not preserve some fragment of happiness?

refashioned:
Demons retain their natural knowledge but lose the knowledge that comes from grace. There are three kinds of knowledge of truth: natural (flowing from the angel's nature as an intellect), gratuitous speculative (divine secrets revealed by grace), and the affective wisdom that produces love of God. The first remains in demons. The second is diminished but not entirely removed, since God still reveals certain things to demons through angels. The third is entirely lost.

Natural knowledge remains because it belongs to the angel's nature, which is not destroyed by sin. The angel's intellectual substance is intact. What is lost is the supernatural light and the loving orientation toward divine truth. Demons can still know truths, but they cannot love the truth they know, and they are cut off from the intimate knowledge of God that beatified angels enjoy.
elegant: Demons still know. Their intellectual substance is intact; sin does not destroy nature. What they have lost is the light of grace, the intimate knowledge of God that beatified angels enjoy, and, above all, the love that makes knowledge sweet. They can see truths but cannot rejoice in them. Their knowledge is accurate but loveless, and loveless knowledge of the good is itself a form of torment.

refashioned: Knowing other spiritual substances is natural for angels and does not constitute happiness. Human happiness might consist in knowing separated substances because such knowledge exceeds our natural capacity. But for an angel, knowing other angels is routine, like our knowledge of everyday objects. Demonic knowledge of natural truths, however extensive, does not give them joy, because they cannot refer that knowledge to God or take delight in the order of creation. Their knowledge is accurate but loveless, and loveless knowledge of the good is itself a form of torment.
elegant: Knowledge without love is not happiness. Knowing spiritual truths is as natural for an angel as knowing everyday objects is for us. There is no bliss in it for a being that cannot refer what it knows to the God it has rejected. Demonic intelligence is vast, precise, and entirely joyless. They see the architecture of creation and can only rage at its beauty.

refashioned: Is the demons' will permanently fixed in evil? Free will seems to imply the ability to change. And God's infinite mercy seems capable of restoring any sinner. So why cannot demons repent?
elegant: Can demons repent? Free will implies the ability to change, and divine mercy knows no limit. So why is the door closed forever?

refashioned:
The demons' will is irrevocably fixed in evil. This is not because God withholds mercy from willing penitents, but because demons are constitutionally incapable of repentance. Here is why.

Angelic choice is total and definitive in a way human choice is not. Humans deliberate step by step, change their minds, learn from consequences, and can reverse course. Angels grasp everything relevant to a choice in a single comprehensive act. When an angel chooses, it does so with full intellectual clarity and total volitional commitment. There is nothing left to learn that might change its mind. Its choice exhausts the deliberative process in one act.

This means that the angel's first free choice, for or against God, is permanent. The good angels are permanently fixed in good, and the fallen angels are permanently fixed in evil. Neither can reverse course, because there is no new information or perspective that could prompt a reconsideration. The choice was made with the full resources of an angelic intellect, and nothing can undo it.
elegant: The door is closed because the one who walked through it did so with absolute clarity and total commitment. Angelic choice is not like human choice. We deliberate in stages, learn from consequences, change our minds. An angel grasps everything relevant in a single act and chooses with finality. There is nothing left to learn, no new perspective that could prompt reconsideration. The choice was made with the full resources of an angelic intellect, and nothing in creation can undo what was done with that kind of completeness.

refashioned:
Both good and fallen angels have free will, but in different states. The good angels' free will is permanently directed toward God. The fallen angels' free will is permanently turned from God. Both are free in the sense that their choices were voluntary. Neither is free to reverse course, because angelic choice, once made with full clarity, is by nature irrevocable.

God's mercy extends to those who repent. Demons cannot repent, not because mercy is withheld but because repentance is impossible for a being that chose with total clarity and finality. The devil's original desire remains in force, not because he still thinks he can achieve it, but because his will is set in the posture of that first refusal and cannot reorient itself.
elegant: God's mercy extends to all who repent. Demons cannot repent. Not because mercy is withheld but because repentance is structurally impossible for a being that chose with total clarity. The good angels are fixed in good by the same mechanism: one comprehensive choice, irrevocable because it was complete. Freedom is not the ability to endlessly revise. It is the power to commit, and angelic commitment is absolute.

refashioned:
Demons experience sorrow. They know that their suffering is just, they see the good they have lost, and they are tormented by the success of those they envy. Their will is set on evil, but they do not achieve everything they will. Every thwarting of their desire is a source of grief. They rejoice in the evil they accomplish and grieve over the evil they are prevented from accomplishing.

This coexistence of joy and sorrow is not contradictory. They are directed at different objects. The demon takes a perverse satisfaction in the harm it causes but suffers genuine anguish at its own punishment, at the triumph of the good, and at the gap between what it desires and what it obtains. Their existence is a mixture of malicious glee and inescapable torment.
elegant: Demons suffer. They see what they have lost. They are thwarted in what they desire. They watch the triumph of what they hate. Their existence is a jagged mixture of malicious satisfaction in the harm they cause and inescapable anguish at their own condition. They rejoice and grieve simultaneously, because the objects of their joy and sorrow are different. The demon is a being at war with its own situation, celebrating its victories while drowning in its defeat.

refashioned:
The demons' place of punishment is twofold: hell and the lower atmosphere of the earth. Hell is their primary place of punishment. But some demons are permitted to inhabit the air around the earth until the final judgment, as a trial for human beings. They operate in our world not because they are free but because God permits their presence as a test.

This arrangement is not inconsistent with their punishment. A prisoner can be taken from his cell to labor without ceasing to be punished. The demons carry their punishment with them wherever they go. Their torment is not primarily a matter of location but of the permanent condition of their will, which is turned from God and fixed in misery. They are in hell wherever they are, because hell, for a spiritual being, is ultimately a state of the will rather than a geographical coordinate.

At the final judgment, all demons will be confined to hell permanently. Their current presence in our atmosphere is temporary, permitted by God for the testing and spiritual growth of the human race.
elegant: Hell is not primarily a place. It is the permanent condition of a will turned from God. Demons carry their punishment wherever they go. Some are permitted to roam the lower atmosphere as a trial for human beings, but a prisoner laboring outside his cell is still a prisoner. Their torment is not geographic but existential: the unending experience of having chosen against the infinite good and being unable to unchoose. At the final judgment, even the geographic freedom ends. But the real confinement was never about walls.

refashioned: Having considered spiritual creatures, we turn to the physical world. Scripture describes three stages in its production: creation ("In the beginning God created heaven and earth"), distinction (separating light from darkness, waters above from waters below), and adornment ("Let there be lights in the firmament"). Four questions arise about creation itself: whether physical things come from God, whether they exist for God's goodness, whether God made them through angels, and whether their forms come from angels or directly from God.
elegant: Three acts shape the physical world: creation calls it into being, distinction carves it into kinds, adornment fills it with splendor. We begin with creation itself -- whether bodies come from God, whether they exist for his goodness, and whether their forms descend through angelic intermediaries or arrive directly from the divine hand.

refashioned: Some ancient heresies claimed that a good God could not have made the visible, physical world -- that it must be the product of an evil principle. This is untenable. Everything that exists shares something in common: being. When many different things share a single property, that property must trace back to a single source. Different heated objects all receive their heat from some source of heat. Likewise, all things that exist -- visible or invisible, physical or spiritual -- must receive their existence from one principle of being. The physical world comes from God, just as the spiritual does.
elegant: Every existing thing shares one property: being. When radically different things share a single trait, that trait must flow from a single source. All heat traces to fire; all existence traces to one principle of being. The physical world is no orphan of a lesser god. Whatever is -- visible or invisible, stone or spirit -- draws its existence from the same well.

refashioned: Physical creatures are not evil in themselves. They endure in some respects forever, at least as to their matter, since what God creates is never annihilated. The closer something is to God's immutability, the more stable it is. As for the apparent evil of physical things -- serpents, scorching heat -- these are harmful only relative to particular observers. What injures one creature benefits another. Something harmful in one respect may be beneficial in another. And physical creation does not by itself draw us away from God; rather, "the invisible things of God are clearly seen, being understood by the things that are made." If creatures lead people astray, the fault lies in those who misuse them, not in the creatures themselves.
elegant: Matter endures. Nothing God creates falls into absolute annihilation. And the closer a creature stands to God's own immovability, the more stubbornly it persists. As for the serpent's venom and the sun's blaze -- these are evil only to the creature they happen to harm. What injures one thing nourishes another. Creation does not pull us from God; it points toward him. If anyone stumbles over the world's beauty into idolatry, the fault lies in the stumbler, not the beauty.

refashioned:
Were physical things created because of God's goodness, or for some other reason? Origen argued that God originally created only spiritual beings, all equal, and that the physical world was imposed as punishment when some of them fell. The degree of their fall determined what kind of body they were bound to. This position is wrong for two reasons. First, it contradicts Genesis, which says after each act of creation that "God saw that it was good" -- meaning each thing was made because it was good for it to exist, not as punishment. Second, it would make the physical arrangement of the universe a matter of pure chance.

The correct view: the universe is a whole composed of parts. Each part exists for its own proper activity. Less noble parts serve more noble ones, as the senses serve the intellect. All parts contribute to the perfection of the whole. And the whole universe, with all its parts, is oriented toward God as its ultimate end, imitating and displaying divine goodness. Physical creatures exist for the sake of God's goodness.
elegant:
Origen imagined the material world as a prison built to punish fallen spirits -- each body a sentence fitted to the crime. But Genesis says otherwise: after each act of making, God saw that it was good. The cosmos is not a punishment. It is a portrait.

Every part exists for its own act. Lesser parts serve nobler ones. All parts together compose the whole. And the whole exists to display, however faintly, the goodness of God. The physical universe is not a detour from the divine plan. It is the plan.

refashioned: That God created things so they might have being does not exclude the deeper truth that he created them for his own goodness -- since having being is itself a reflection of divine goodness. That physical creatures serve spiritual ones as their proximate end does not exclude God's goodness as their ultimate end. And the diversity among creatures is not unjust: just as an architect places different stones in different positions not because of any prior difference between stones, but to achieve the perfection of the whole building, God established creatures of various natures according to his wisdom.
elegant: That creatures exist for their own sake does not contradict their existing for God -- since their very being is a reflection of his goodness. That lower things serve higher ones does not exclude God as their ultimate purpose. And the inequality among creatures is no injustice: an architect places different stones in different positions not because the stones deserve it, but because the building demands it.

refashioned:
Did God create physical things through intermediary spiritual beings, with each level of creation producing the next? No. Creation in the strict sense -- bringing something into existence from nothing, with no pre-existing material -- can only be the work of an infinite power. No creature, however powerful, can create from nothing. The more fundamental the level of existence, the higher the cause it requires. Matter itself, which underlies everything physical, belongs to the causality of the supreme cause alone. Secondary causes can shape and transform, but they cannot create. That is why Genesis says directly: "In the beginning God created heaven and earth."

The order we see in creation reflects different grades of perfection established by divine wisdom, not a chain of created intermediaries each producing the next.
elegant: Creation from nothing is the exclusive work of infinite power. No creature, however exalted, can summon being from non-being. Secondary causes can shape, transform, and arrange -- but the raw fact of existence itself belongs to the supreme cause alone. The more fundamental the substrate, the higher the power required to produce it. Matter, the most universal of all substrates, answers to God and God only.

refashioned:
Do the forms of physical things come from angels or spiritual substances? Three philosophical traditions said yes. Plato held that material forms are participations in separate, immaterial Forms -- an immaterial Horse causing all horses, an immaterial Life causing all living things. Avicenna held that forms do not subsist independently in matter but exist in angelic intellects, from which they flow into matter the way a craftsman's design flows into his work. Certain heretics combined these views, claiming God created matter but the devil differentiated it into distinct species.

All these positions share a common error: they treat forms as if they are independently generated, when in reality what comes into being is always the composite -- the formed thing, not the form by itself.
elegant: Plato, Avicenna, and certain heretics all supposed that the forms of physical things rain down from immaterial sources -- separate Ideas, angelic intellects, or a demonic craftsman shaping inert matter. Each theory shares the same root error: treating the form as if it were the thing being made. But what comes into being is always the composite -- the formed thing, never the form alone.

refashioned:
Aristotle showed that what is properly made is always the composite of form and matter, not the form alone. Forms of perishable things come and go, but they are not themselves generated or destroyed -- the composite is. Like produces like: this fire generates that fire. We should not look for the source of physical forms in some immaterial realm, but in composite agents acting on matter.

That said, since physical agents are themselves moved by spiritual substances, physical forms do derive from spiritual substances in a secondary sense -- not by emanation, but as the endpoint of a causal chain of movement. And the ideas in angelic intellects, which are like seeds of physical forms, trace back ultimately to God as first cause. In the original creation, however, no transformation from potentiality to actuality occurred through natural processes. The first physical forms came directly from God, whose command alone matter obeys as its proper cause.
elegant: Like produces like: this fire begets that fire. Physical forms arise not by emanation from on high but by composite agents acting on matter. Yet the chain of causation does not terminate in matter. Physical agents are moved by spiritual substances; the ideas in angelic minds trace back to God. In the original creation, however, no intermediate process occurred. The first forms came directly from the Word whose command matter obeys as its own proper cause.

refashioned: When Boethius says that visible things come from "forms without matter," he means the ideas in the mind of God. When we say forms in matter are referred to the angelic intellect, this happens through the angels' role as movers, not through emanation. The heavenly bodies likewise influence earthly things through movement, not by radiating forms into matter.
elegant: When Boethius says visible things come from "forms without matter," he means the ideas in the mind of God. Angels influence physical form not by radiating essences into matter but by setting things in motion. The heavenly bodies do the same. Influence by movement, not emanation -- that is the true relationship between the spiritual and the corporeal.

refashioned: Next we consider how creation was ordered toward the distinctions we see in the world. Four questions: Did formless matter exist before receiving form? Is the matter of all physical things the same? Was the empyrean heaven created at the same time as formless matter? Was time created simultaneously with it?
elegant: Before the world could be distinguished into kinds, creation had to be ordered toward distinction. Was there a primordial chaos before form? Is the underlying matter of all things the same? Did the luminous heaven of the blessed exist from the very beginning? And did time itself begin with creation?

refashioned: Did formless matter exist in time before it received its form -- was there a stage of primordial chaos? Genesis says "the earth was void and empty," which Augustine takes to describe formless matter. And in nature, the imperfect does precede the perfect. On the other hand, an imperfect creation would imply an imperfect creator, and God's works are described as perfect. Also, if formlessness preceded form in time, the original state of creation would have been the chaos that the ancients described -- and this contradicts the orderliness Scripture attributes to God's work from the beginning.
elegant: Was the original creation chaos -- raw matter awaiting its sculptor? Genesis says the earth was "void and empty." Nature tells us the imperfect precedes the perfect. But to exist without any form at all is a contradiction: existence is already an act, and every act is already a form. The question is not whether chaos preceded order, but what we mean by chaos.

refashioned:
Augustine and the other Church Fathers only appear to contradict each other on this point because they define "formlessness" differently. Augustine means the complete absence of any form. Under that definition, formless matter could not have preceded form in time, because to exist in time already implies actuality, and actuality is itself a form. You cannot coherently say that something existed -- actually, in time -- yet had no form at all. That would be saying a thing was actual without any actuality.

The other Fathers -- Basil, Ambrose, Chrysostom -- mean something different by "formlessness": not the absence of all form, but the absence of beauty and full differentiation. On this reading, creation initially existed but lacked the order and beauty it would later receive. Specifically, three kinds of beauty were missing: the heavens lacked the beauty of light ("darkness was upon the face of the deep"), the earth lacked the beauty of being uncovered by water ("the earth was void"), and it lacked the beauty of vegetation ("empty" or "shapeless"). When these distinctions are kept clear, the apparent disagreement largely dissolves.
elegant: Augustine and the other Fathers only seem to disagree. Augustine defines formlessness as the total absence of form -- which cannot precede form in time, because to exist in time is already to be actual, and actuality is form. The others mean something gentler: not the absence of all form, but the absence of beauty. The heavens lacked light; the earth lay hidden under water; vegetation had not yet clothed the ground. Formlessness in this sense is not metaphysical impossibility but aesthetic incompleteness -- creation before its finishing touches.

refashioned:
Augustine interprets "earth" and "water" in Genesis 1 as symbols for primary matter itself -- since Moses needed to make the concept of formless matter accessible to ordinary people using familiar images. The other Fathers take "earth" literally as the element of earth.

As for how natural processes relate to divine creation: in nature, the imperfect does precede the perfect in time, because nature works by bringing potentiality into actuality. But God produces being out of nothing and can therefore produce perfect things instantly. If some Fathers hold that formlessness preceded form in time, this was not from any limitation in God's power but from his wisdom in establishing an order from imperfect to perfect.

Scripture already implies several kinds of distinction before the formal "work of distinction" begins: heaven is distinguished from earth (a material distinction), earth and water are named (elemental distinction), and different regions are assigned different positions (spatial distinction).
elegant: Augustine reads "earth" and "water" in Genesis as figures for primary matter -- the nearest Moses could come to explaining pure potentiality to an unphilosophical audience. The other Fathers take them literally. Both agree on what matters: God can produce perfection instantly, and if creation unfolded in stages, this reflects not limitation but wisdom -- the orderly progression from the unfinished to the complete.

refashioned: Is the underlying matter of all physical things -- terrestrial and celestial -- fundamentally the same? Augustine and Plato thought so, since the four elements can transform into one another, proving they share common matter. Aristotle disagreed. The heavenly bodies move in circles, while the elements move in straight lines (up or down). Circular motion has no contrary, so celestial bodies are free from the contrariety that makes terrestrial things corruptible. Different natural movements imply different natures, and different natures imply different matter.
elegant: Is the matter of stars the same as the matter of stones? The pre-Socratics and Plato said yes -- the four elements transform into each other, proving a common substrate. Aristotle said no. Celestial bodies move in circles; elements move in straight lines. Circular motion has no opposite, so celestial bodies are free from the contrariety that makes earthly things perishable. Different movement, different nature, different matter.

refashioned:
The deeper argument is this: matter in itself is pure potentiality, directed toward whatever forms it can receive. If celestial matter could receive terrestrial forms, it would have potentiality for forms it does not possess, which means it would have privation -- and privation combined with potentiality means corruptibility. But celestial bodies are by nature incorruptible. Therefore their matter cannot be the same as terrestrial matter.

Nor can we say, as Averroes suggested, that a celestial body is itself its own matter, with a separate substance serving as its mover. If we strip away the mover and the celestial body is not a composite of form and matter, then it would be pure form and pure actuality -- something actually understood rather than sensibly perceived. But celestial bodies are clearly sensible objects.

So celestial and terrestrial matter are the same only by analogy: both are potentiality relative to their respective forms, but not the same kind of potentiality.
elegant: Matter is pure potentiality, reaching toward whatever forms it can receive. If celestial matter could receive earthly forms, it would harbor a privation -- the absence of forms it is capable of but does not possess. Privation plus potentiality equals corruptibility. But celestial bodies are by nature incorruptible. Therefore their matter is not the same as ours. The two share a name only by analogy: both are potentiality, but potentiality for fundamentally different actualities.

refashioned: When Augustine speaks of formless matter as one, he means either that it is one in the order of being (all bodies share the order of corporeal existence) or he is following Plato in not recognizing a fifth element. As for the objection that all bodies belong to one genus and therefore share one matter: logically there is one genus of body, but physically, corruptible and incorruptible things differ in their modes of potentiality and are not in the same physical genus.
elegant: Logically, all bodies belong to one genus. Physically, they do not. Corruptible and incorruptible things differ in their very modes of potentiality. The word "body" spans the divide, but the underlying realities it names are as different as the actuality each kind of matter strains toward.

refashioned: Was the empyrean heaven -- a realm of light understood as the place of the blessed -- created at the very beginning alongside formless matter? Several objections arise: it would have to be a sensible body, yet it does not appear to move; it would need to influence lower bodies, yet without movement it seemingly cannot; and if it serves contemplation rather than natural purposes, why would a physical place be needed for something spiritual? Further, if it were wholly luminous, it should illuminate constantly, leaving no room for night.
elegant: The empyrean heaven -- a realm of light, the dwelling of the blessed -- raises immediate questions. If it is a body, it should move; yet no movement is observed. If it does not move, how can it influence what lies below? If it exists for contemplation rather than natural effect, why does a spiritual purpose require a physical place? And if it blazes with light, why is there ever darkness?

refashioned:
The empyrean heaven rests on theological tradition rather than explicit Scripture. The various authorities agree it is the place of the blessed but differ on why they posit it. The best reason draws from the nature of glory itself. In the final reward, there will be both spiritual and bodily glory -- not only in resurrected human bodies but in the renewed cosmos. Spiritual glory began at the world's origin in the blessedness of the angels. It was fitting, then, that bodily glory should also have some beginning from the start: a corporeal reality free from corruption, wholly luminous -- a physical anticipation of the glorified cosmos to come. This heaven is called "empyrean" (fiery) not because of heat but because of radiance.

Its immobility is not a defect but suits its nature as something already in the state of glory. And it may well influence lower bodies despite not moving itself, just as the highest angels influence lower ones without being sent on missions. Its light is either of a more subtle nature than the sun's condensed rays, or it possesses the distinctive brightness of glory.
elegant: The best argument for the empyrean draws from the nature of glory itself. In the final reward, glory will be both spiritual and bodily -- not only in risen flesh but in a renewed cosmos. If spiritual glory began at the world's origin in angelic blessedness, then bodily glory should have begun there too: a corporeal reality wholly luminous, free from corruption, shining before anything else was adorned. The empyrean is the first installment of the world's eventual transfiguration.

refashioned: The empyrean heaven, being ordered toward glory, may influence lower bodies not through movement but through stable, conserving power -- analogous to how the highest angels influence lower ones while remaining stationary. Physical place is assigned to contemplation not as necessary but as fitting: outward splendor corresponding to inward illumination. As for its light not illuminating the lower world, the firmament's solid but transparent structure contains the empyrean light within, or that light may be of a subtler nature that does not emit rays the way the sun does.
elegant: The empyrean does not need to move in order to influence. The highest angels influence the lowest without being sent on missions. Stability is not impotence; it is the condition of what has already arrived at its end. And its light does not flood the lower world because the firmament contains it -- or because its brightness is of a subtler order than the sun's condensed rays. The splendor of glory does not compete with the splendor of nature.

refashioned:
Was time created simultaneously with formless matter? Augustine says two things were created before time: primary matter and angelic nature. Others say time must have existed from the beginning because formlessness preceded form in duration, and duration requires a measure.

The resolution depends on which framework you adopt. For Augustine, matter and angels precede form not in time but only in origin and nature -- so time is not among the first-created things. For the other Fathers, who hold that formlessness preceded form in duration, time must have existed as the measure of that duration.

Either way, from the very beginning there was movement of some kind -- at minimum, the succession of thoughts and desires in angelic minds -- and movement without time is inconceivable, since time is simply the measure of succession in movement. Time, unlike place, is not permanent; it was created in its beginning, and we can only grasp it in the present moment -- the "now."
elegant: Did time begin with creation, or before it? Augustine says matter and angels precede time, not in duration, but in the order of nature. Others hold that formlessness preceded form in time, requiring a measure for that duration. Either way, from the first instant there was movement -- at minimum, the succession of angelic thought -- and movement without time is inconceivable. Time is simply the measure of before and after. Unlike place, which exists all at once, time exists only in its fleeting present -- the perpetual "now."

refashioned: We now consider the work of distinction itself -- the first day's work. Four questions: Is "light" used literally or metaphorically when applied to spiritual things? Is physical light a body? Is it a quality? Was light fittingly produced on the first day?
elegant: The first day's work is distinction -- the carving of differentiation into creation's uniformity. Four questions turn on light: whether the word belongs literally to spiritual things, whether physical light is a body, whether it is a quality, and whether it was fittingly the first thing produced.

refashioned: Is "light" literal or metaphorical when applied to spiritual realities? The word has two uses: its original meaning (what makes things visible to the eye) and its extended meaning (what makes anything manifest to any kind of knowing). We say "I see what you mean" without implying eyesight. Similarly, "light" in its strict, original sense is metaphorical when applied to spiritual things. But in its broader, extended sense -- as that which makes manifest -- it applies properly to spiritual realities as well. The key is which sense you intend.
elegant: "Light" began as a word for what makes things visible to the eye. By extension, it came to mean whatever makes anything manifest to any kind of knowing. In its original, strict sense, light applied to spiritual realities is metaphor. But in its broader, living sense -- as that which illuminates understanding -- it names something real. The ambiguity is not confusion; it is the natural expansion of a word that found its truest home beyond the eye.

refashioned:
Is light a physical body? No, for three reasons.

First, place. Two bodies cannot occupy the same space simultaneously, but light and air coexist in the same location.

Second, movement. If light were a body, its spreading would be local motion. But local motion cannot be instantaneous -- a body must pass through every intervening point. Yet light's diffusion is instantaneous: the moment the sun reaches the horizon, the entire hemisphere is illuminated. The distance from east to west is too great for the time to be merely too short to perceive.

Third, generation and corruption. If light were a body, it would have to be destroyed every time darkness falls and regenerated every sunrise -- a body large enough to fill half the sky, created and annihilated daily. This contradicts both reason and common sense.
elegant: Light is not a body. Three proofs settle the matter. Two bodies cannot occupy the same place, but light and air coexist without conflict. A body's movement must cross intervening space, but light's diffusion is instantaneous -- the moment the sun breaks the horizon, the entire hemisphere is lit. And if light were a body, sunrise would be the daily generation of a mass large enough to fill half the sky, and sunset its annihilation. Reason and common sense unite against the idea.

refashioned:
Light is a quality -- specifically, an active quality that flows from the substantial form of a luminous body, the way heat flows from the substantial form of fire. Proof: rays from different stars produce different effects according to the diverse natures of those bodies, which would not be the case if light were merely a generic property.

Light is not a substantial form (as some claimed about the sun's light), because substantial forms are not directly perceptible to the senses, while light clearly is. Nor is light merely an "intentional" or apparent quality like a reflection -- it produces real physical effects, such as warming bodies.

The reason light vanishes when its source is removed is that light is not produced by a permanent transformation of matter. It is more like a quality in process of being received, which endures only as long as its active cause is present -- unlike heat absorbed by water, which lingers after the fire is removed.
elegant: Light is a quality -- an active quality flowing from the nature of a luminous body, as heat flows from fire. It is not a body, not a substance, not a mere appearance. It produces real physical effects: warming, growth, the actualization of color. But unlike heat absorbed by water, light vanishes the instant its source withdraws, because it is not produced by a permanent transformation of matter. Light is the ongoing gift of a luminous agent, sustained only as long as the giver shines.

refashioned:
Was it fitting to assign the production of light to the first day? Light is merely an accidental quality -- shouldn't it be subordinate to more fundamental things? And isn't it the sun that divides day from night, which wasn't made until the fourth day?

These objections have force, but there are strong reasons why light comes first. On the other hand, if we follow Augustine's reading that the "light" of the first day is spiritual -- the formation of angelic nature through illumination by the Word of God -- then the production of spiritual creatures is recorded first as befitting their higher dignity.
elegant: Why should light -- a mere quality, an accident -- come first? Because light is the quality of the primary body. Because it is the most universal property shared by heaven and earth alike. Because all other things become manifest through it. And because without light there is no day, and the narrative of creation unfolds in days. Light must come first because everything else depends on it to be seen.

refashioned:
Other commentators hold that the light produced on the first day was corporeal, and they give good reasons for its priority. Light is a quality of the primary body. Just as knowledge proceeds from general principles to specific applications, so creation proceeds from the general to the particular. Light, being a common quality shared by both terrestrial and celestial bodies, fittingly comes first.

As for what this primordial light was, the best explanation is that it was the sun's own light in a formless, undifferentiated state -- the solar substance already possessing general illuminative power, which later received the specific, determinate power to produce the particular effects we observe. The first day's light thus already established the basic distinction between light and darkness, between hemisphere illuminated and hemisphere in shadow, between the period called "day" and the period called "night."

The movement producing this alternation was the common daily revolution of the entire heavens -- distinct from the specific orbital movements of individual celestial bodies, which were established on the fourth day to produce the further distinctions of seasons, months, and years.
elegant: The light of the first day was the sun's own substance in an undifferentiated state -- illuminative power without yet the specific, determinate character it would receive on the fourth day. The general revolution of the heavens already produced the alternation of light and darkness, day and night. The particular movements of individual celestial bodies -- producing seasons, months, and years -- awaited their appointed time. Creation moves from the general to the specific, as knowledge moves from principles to conclusions.

refashioned:
The primordial light was not some temporary luminous cloud that later dissolved back into the sun's matter. Scripture records the institution of the natural order that would endure permanently, not temporary arrangements. Following Dionysius, the light was already the sun's substance, initially formless, later receiving its determinate character.

On Augustine's alternative reading, the production of light signifies the formation of spiritual creatures -- not in the perfection of glory but in the perfection of grace. The division of light from darkness then denotes the distinction between the spiritual creature formed by grace and the other, still-unformed creation. If all things received their form simultaneously, then the "darkness" must represent the spiritual darkness that God foresaw would come -- the fall of those who would choose against the light.
elegant: The primordial light was not a luminous cloud later dissolved into the sun. Scripture records the permanent order of nature, not temporary arrangements. On Augustine's alternative reading, the first day's light is the formation of spiritual creatures through grace -- the illumination of angelic minds turning toward the Word. The division of light from darkness then distinguishes the formed from the still-formless, or the faithful from the fallen. Either reading preserves the same truth: light -- physical or spiritual -- is the first and foundational act of distinction.

refashioned: The work of the second day. Four questions: Was the firmament made on the second day? Are there waters above the firmament? Does the firmament divide waters from waters? Is there more than one heaven?
elegant: The second day separates what was mingled. Four questions: Was the firmament produced on this day? Do waters exist above it? Does it divide waters from waters? And is there one heaven or many?
