The same intellect that once worked through sensory images now works through received species, the way the same person walks differently on flat ground and steep terrain. The shift is real but the walker is unchanged.

refashioned:
The separated soul can know other separate substances (angels) -- something it could not do while embodied. Since the separated soul now receives its intelligible content through species infused by divine illumination (the same basic mode that angels use), it can understand beings of its own general kind.

However, it does not know them with the clarity that they know themselves. The human soul, even separated, remains the lowest in the hierarchy of intellectual substances. Its received species are less perfectly specified, so its knowledge of angels is real but imperfect -- like seeing through a fog rather than in clear light.
elegant: Freed from the body, the soul can know other separated substances -- angels, other departed souls -- something impossible while embodied. But its knowledge of them is imperfect, like seeing through fog. The human soul, even separated, remains the lowest intellectual substance. It knows its peers clearly but its superiors only dimly.

refashioned:
The separated soul does know all natural things -- but in a general and confused way, not with the specific detail it could achieve through bodily senses.

Angels know all material natures through the comprehensive intelligible species they receive. The separated human soul also receives intelligible species from the divine light, but because it is the lowest intellectual substance, these species are less determinate. It can grasp the general natures of things (what fire is, what water is) but may not be able to distinguish every specific variety or individual instance with precision.

This knowledge surpasses what we can achieve through philosophy in this life in scope (covering all natural kinds) but falls short in precision (lacking the detailed sensory data that embodied inquiry provides).
elegant: The separated soul knows all natural things -- but in a general, impressionistic way, not with the sharp detail that embodied experience provides. Angels know all material natures with precision through their comprehensive species. The separated soul receives similar species but less perfectly specified -- a wide view through frosted glass. Greater in scope than embodied philosophy, lesser in precision than embodied science.

refashioned:
The separated soul knows singulars -- specific individual beings -- but through a different route than it used while embodied.

In the body, singulars were known through the senses and imagination. Separated from the body, the soul knows them through its prior connections (things it experienced in life), through divine disposition (God or angels directing its attention), and through natural affinity (its relationship to certain individuals and places).

But this knowledge of singulars is not unlimited. The separated soul does not automatically know every individual thing in the universe. Its knowledge of particulars is determined by the relevance of those particulars to the soul's own situation and by divine providence.
elegant: The separated soul knows some individuals but not all. In the body, it knew individuals through the senses. Separated, it knows them through prior connection, natural affinity, and divine disposition. You do not suddenly know every person who ever lived. You know those to whom your existence was already bound -- by experience, by love, by providence.

refashioned:
The habits of knowledge acquired in this life do remain in the separated soul. Since these habits reside in the intellect itself (not in the body or in a bodily organ), the destruction of the body does not destroy them.

The intelligible species stored in the passive intellect persist -- they do not depend on the body for their retention. What the soul learned in life, it retains after death.

However, the phantasms (sensory images) that were stored in the imagination and memory do not survive, because the imagination is a power of the body-soul composite. The soul retains the intelligible content it abstracted from those phantasms, but not the phantasms themselves.
elegant: What you learned in life, you keep after death. The habits of knowledge reside in the intellect itself, which has no bodily organ to be destroyed. The sensory images are gone -- the imagination dies with the body -- but the intelligible content abstracted from those images persists. The separated soul retains the distillate of all its earthly learning.

refashioned: The body relates to the soul's knowledge as an instrument, not as an object. So when the instrument is destroyed, the knowledge previously acquired through it is not lost -- only the instrument's ongoing contribution ceases. The intellectual habits remain intact.
elegant: The body was the instrument, not the container, of knowledge. Destroy the instrument and the knowledge previously acquired through it remains intact -- only the ongoing contribution ceases. What the intellect has received, it holds.

refashioned:
The separated soul can use the knowledge it acquired in life, but not in the same way. While embodied, it activated stored intelligible species by turning to phantasms. Separated from the body, it lacks phantasms. Instead, it exercises its acquired knowledge through the intelligible species that remain in it, now activated by the more general, angel-like mode of understanding that comes with separation.

The knowledge functions differently: what was once understood through concrete sensory examples is now understood more abstractly. The soul can still deploy what it knows, but the mode of deployment has shifted from image-dependent to purely intellectual.
elegant: The separated soul can use its earthly knowledge, but in a different mode. Without phantasms, it cannot activate stored species the old way -- through concrete sensory examples. Instead, it exercises its knowledge through a purely intellectual mode suited to its separated state. What was once understood through images is now understood more abstractly. The content survives; the mode of access changes.

refashioned:
Spatial distance does not impede the separated soul's knowledge. Distance is an obstacle to sensation (which requires physical contact between object and organ, possibly through a medium), but the separated soul does not know things through sensation. Its knowledge comes through intelligible species that are not transmitted through physical space.

However, the separated soul does not know everything everywhere. Its knowledge is determined not by spatial proximity but by divine disposition and natural affinity. It knows what it is meant to know, regardless of where those things are located.
elegant: Distance does not impede the separated soul's knowledge. Distance is an obstacle to sensation, which requires physical contact through a medium. But the separated soul knows through species that traverse no physical space. What it knows is determined not by proximity but by providence and affinity. The future is unknowable because it does not yet exist. The distant is knowable because it already does.

refashioned:
The separated souls of the dead do not, as a general rule, know what happens among the living on earth. Their condition and mode of knowing is directed toward what pertains to their own state (whether in reward or punishment), not toward the ongoing affairs of the temporal world.

Gregory compares the dead to the living the way the living relate to the unborn: just as we have no direct knowledge of what the unborn will experience, the dead have no direct knowledge of what the living are currently doing.

There are exceptions: divine revelation can inform separated souls of earthly events when God wills it, just as prophets in life received knowledge of distant or future events. And the blessed in heaven, who see God, may know earthly affairs insofar as God reveals them. But naturally speaking, the separated soul's attention is oriented away from the temporal world.
elegant: The dead do not, by nature, know what happens among the living. Their state is oriented away from the temporal world and toward the realm of spiritual substances. Gregory compares them to the living in relation to the unborn: separated by a gulf of mode, not of mere distance. The blessed in heaven may know earthly affairs insofar as God reveals them -- but naturally, the departed look forward, not back.

refashioned: The fact that the dead may sometimes appear to the living (in visions or apparitions) does not prove they know what happens on earth. These appearances may occur through divine dispensation, angelic mediation, or demonic activity -- not through the separated soul's natural knowledge of earthly events. Or, as Augustine suggests, such visions may be like dream images that do not reflect the real awareness of the person who appears in them.
elegant: That the dead sometimes appear to the living proves nothing about their natural knowledge of earthly affairs. Such appearances occur through divine dispensation, angelic mediation, or demonic activity -- not through the departed soul's own awareness. Care for the living can persist without knowledge of their condition, just as we pray for the dead without knowing their state. Love does not require surveillance.

refashioned: Having covered creation in general, we turn to the creation of humanity specifically. Four topics: (1) the production of man himself, (2) the purpose of that production, (3) the first man's condition, and (4) his dwelling place. On the production of man, three sub-topics: the soul, the body, and the woman. On the soul, four questions: Was it made, or part of God's substance? Was it created from nothing? Was it made directly by God or through angels? Was it made before the body?
elegant: Where does the human soul come from? Four inquiries into the origin of that which makes us more than dust: whether the soul is a fragment of God or something made, whether it was created from nothing, whether angels had a hand in fashioning it, and whether it existed before the body it animates.

refashioned:
The human soul is not a piece of God. This claim sounds flattering, but it falls apart immediately. The soul sometimes lacks understanding. It acquires knowledge gradually from external things. It has multiple distinct powers. None of this is compatible with the divine nature, which is pure actuality, receives nothing from outside itself, and admits no internal variety.

The error has deep roots. Early thinkers, unable to conceive of anything beyond the physical, assumed God was a body and the soul was made of the same stuff. The Manichaeans thought God was light and the soul a trapped fragment of it. Later thinkers who recognized incorporeal realities still made the mistake of treating the soul as part of a single cosmic soul, unable to distinguish different levels of spiritual existence.

All these views have already been refuted. The soul is not God's substance. When Genesis says God "breathed" life into man, this is not literal exhalation but the act of making a spirit. And though the soul is a simple form, it is not pure act like God; it has existence only by participation, not by its own essence. God and the human mind are genuinely diverse, not different versions of the same thing.
elegant:
The soul is not a piece of God. Flattering as the idea sounds, it collapses on contact with experience. We hesitate, we learn slowly, we possess faculties that war with one another. None of this is compatible with the divine nature, which is pure act, receives nothing from outside, and admits no internal division.

The error has ancient roots. Those who could conceive of nothing beyond the physical made God a body and the soul a fragment of it. The Manichaeans called God light and the soul a shard of that light trapped in flesh. Later thinkers recognized incorporeal reality but still could not distinguish levels of spiritual existence, so they made each soul a drop in one cosmic mind.

All these mistakes share a single flaw: they cannot account for what the soul actually does. A being that sometimes fails to understand, that acquires knowledge piece by piece, that contains competing powers, is not divine. It is something made.

refashioned:
The rational soul can only come into existence through creation, not through any natural process of generation. Here is why: the way something is made must correspond to the way it exists. Only substances that subsist in their own right are truly "made" in the full sense. Non-subsistent forms (like whiteness or shape) do not come into being on their own; they come into being only when the composite thing they belong to is produced.

The rational soul is different from other forms because it subsists independently. It is not merely the form of a body in the way heat is a quality of fire. Because it subsists, it can properly be said to exist and therefore to be made. And since it cannot be made from pre-existing matter (whether physical, which would make it a body, or spiritual, which would mean one spiritual substance morphing into another), it can only exist through creation from nothing.

This does not apply to other forms. The rational soul is unique in being both a form and a subsistent being.
elegant:
The rational soul can come into existence only through creation from nothing. The logic is clean: how a thing is made must match how it exists. Only what subsists in its own right is truly "made." A quality like whiteness does not come into being independently; it arrives only when the white thing is produced. But the rational soul subsists. It is not merely a property of the body the way heat is a quality of fire.

Because it subsists, it can properly be said to exist and therefore to be made. And because it cannot be fashioned from any pre-existing matter, whether physical (which would make it a body) or spiritual (which would mean one spirit morphing into another), it can only come from the one source that needs no raw material at all. The soul is created, not generated. It enters existence the way only God can bring things into existence: from nothing.

refashioned: God produces rational souls directly, without angelic intermediaries. The reasoning is straightforward: the soul can only come into existence through creation, and only God can create. Every secondary cause works by transforming something that already exists, but creation means producing something from nothing. Since the rational soul cannot be produced by transforming pre-existing matter, no created power, however elevated, can produce it. Angels can rearrange and transform existing material, but they cannot create from nothing. Therefore, every rational soul is the immediate work of God.
elegant: Only God creates souls. The reasoning is as spare as the conclusion is sweeping. Creation means producing something from nothing. Every secondary cause works by transforming what already exists. The rational soul cannot be produced by transforming pre-existing matter. Therefore no created power, however exalted, can produce it. Angels can rearrange the furniture of the world, but they cannot add to it. Every rational soul is the direct and immediate work of God.

refashioned:
Was the soul created before the body? Origen thought all souls were created simultaneously with the angels, before any bodies existed, because he believed all spiritual substances are naturally equal and only differ by merit. Augustine tentatively suggested the soul of the first man might have been created with the angels and then later joined to the body by its own will, but he explicitly declined to assert this.

The answer depends on what the soul is. If the soul were a complete being that merely administers the body from outside, pre-existence would be conceivable. But the soul is the body's form and naturally a part of human nature. A soul without a body is an incomplete human nature. God created the first things in their complete natural state. Therefore, it would have been unfitting for the soul to be created without the body.

The soul was not made before the body. It was created together with it, as its natural completion.
elegant:
Was the soul created before the body it inhabits? Origen thought all souls were made with the angels, hovering in spiritual existence before descending into flesh. Augustine entertained the possibility without committing to it.

But the answer turns on what the soul is. If it were a complete being that merely administers the body from the outside, pre-existence would be conceivable. It is not. The soul is the body's form, naturally a part of human nature. A soul without a body is an incomplete human being. God made the first things in their natural completeness. The soul was not created before the body. It was created with it, as its natural fulfillment.

refashioned: If the soul were a complete species on its own, it could have been created separately. But as the form of the body, it was necessarily created in the body, not apart from it. The fact that the soul survives the body's death does not prove it existed before the body; death is a defect that came later, not part of the original design.
elegant: That the soul outlives the body proves nothing about pre-existence. Death is a defect that came later, not a feature of the original design. The soul survives the body because it is indestructible; it was created with the body because it is incomplete without one.

refashioned: Four questions about the first human body: (1) What was it made from? (2) Who made it? (3) Was it well-designed? (4) How does Scripture describe its production?
elegant: Four inquiries into the first human body: the matter from which it was formed, the hand that formed it, the fitness of its design, and the meaning of Scripture's account of its making.

refashioned:
The human body was fittingly made from earth. God is simply perfect, containing all things within Himself as effects pre-exist in their cause. Angels reflect this perfection by knowing all natural things. Humans reflect it in a lower way: by being composed of all things. The rational soul belongs to the genus of spiritual substances. The body's balanced temperament resembles the stability of celestial bodies. And the body contains all four elements in their very substance.

The higher elements, fire and air, dominate in power (life requires heat and moisture), but the lower elements, earth and water, dominate in quantity, because the less powerful elements must be present in greater proportion to maintain equilibrium. Earth and water mixed together are called "slime" or "clay." This is why man is called a "little world": every level of creation is represented in him.
elegant: Man is called a "little world" because every level of creation meets in him. His rational soul belongs to the order of spiritual substances. His balanced temperament echoes the stability of celestial bodies. And all four elements compose his flesh. The higher elements, fire and air, dominate in power, for life requires heat and moisture. The lower, earth and water, dominate in quantity, because weaker forces must be present in greater proportion to maintain equilibrium. Earth and water mixed are called slime. From slime, God fashioned the body that would house the universe in miniature.

refashioned: Why not make the body from nothing, to show God's power more dramatically? Because God's power was already shown in creating matter from nothing; the body itself was fittingly composed of all four elements so man would have something in common with every level of physical creation. Why not from a celestial substance? Because celestial matter is impassible and cannot form the sense organs the rational soul needs. Why not mainly from the nobler elements, fire and air? Because if they dominated in quantity as well as power, they would overwhelm the mixture and destroy the equilibrium the sense of touch requires. Touch is the foundation of all the other senses, and its organ must be a balanced mean between the qualities it perceives.
elegant: Why earth and not something nobler? Because God's power was already displayed in creating matter from nothing. The body was made of all four elements so that man would share something with every stratum of physical creation. Celestial matter cannot form sense organs. If fire and air dominated in quantity as well as power, they would overwhelm the mixture and destroy the equilibrium that touch, the most fundamental sense, requires. The body is not made of the noblest substance but of the most fitting one. Design follows purpose, not prestige.

refashioned:
The first human body was produced directly by God, not through any created intermediary. Forms in matter are only produced when one composite generates another composite of the same kind. No pre-existing human body existed to generate the first one. Angels cannot create new forms from scratch; they can only work with what already exists, using something like a seed. God alone, being absolutely immaterial, can produce form in matter without needing a preceding material form. Since there was no prior human body to serve as a template for natural generation, God alone could have formed the first one.

This does not mean angels played no role at all. God may have used them as ministers in subsidiary tasks, just as they will assist at the resurrection by gathering dust. But the actual formation of the first human body required divine creative power.
elegant: The first human body was made directly by God. No created power could have produced it. Forms in matter arise only when one composite generates another of the same kind, and no prior human body existed. Angels can rearrange existing material but cannot introduce new forms from nothing. God alone, being absolutely immaterial, can produce form in matter without precedent. The first human body had no template, no ancestor, no natural precursor. It required the one cause that needs none.

refashioned: The claim that celestial bodies could have produced man is too ambitious. Perfect animals cannot arise from celestial influence alone. Even the Philosopher's dictum that "man and the sun beget man" only means celestial power cooperates with natural generation, not that it replaces it. The production of a human body from earth is not a natural change driven by celestial motion; it is a supernatural act of divine power, like raising the dead.
elegant: The claim that celestial bodies could have produced man overreaches. Even Aristotle's dictum that "man and the sun beget man" only means celestial power cooperates with natural generation, not that it replaces it. Forming a human body from earth is not a natural process; it is a supernatural act, like raising the dead. Nature assisted nothing here. The first body was entirely God's work.

refashioned:
The human body is excellently designed, not in an absolute sense, but relative to its purpose: serving as the instrument of a rational soul. Every artist designs for the intended function, not for abstract perfection. A saw is made of iron, not glass, even though glass is more beautiful. God fashioned the human body as the best possible instrument for rational life. Any apparent deficiencies are necessary trade-offs required by the body's material conditions.

The body's immediate purpose is the rational soul and its operations. Matter exists for the sake of form, and instruments exist for the sake of the agent who uses them. The human body is shaped to serve thought, not the other way around.
elegant: The human body is not the most powerful or the most armored, but it is the best designed for what it must do: serve a rational soul. Every artist builds for the intended function. A saw is made of iron, not glass, though glass is more beautiful, because beauty would defeat the purpose. God fashioned the human body as the finest possible instrument of thought. Its apparent deficiencies are not flaws but trade-offs, the necessary costs of optimizing for intelligence.

refashioned:
Why do some animals have sharper senses or faster movement? Because the human body is optimized for a different priority: the most balanced temperament of any animal, which is what touch (the foundation of all senses) requires. Man has the largest brain relative to body size, both for the interior cognitive powers that support intellectual activity and to cool the considerable heart-heat required for upright posture. That large, moist brain weakens smell, which requires dryness. Similar trade-offs explain why some animals see or hear more acutely.

Why no natural armor, claws, or thick hide? These come from an excess of the earthy element, which would upset human temperament. Instead, man has reason and hands, which Aristotle calls "the instrument of instruments." Through these, man can fashion an unlimited variety of tools, weapons, and clothing, something far more powerful than any fixed biological equipment. A rational nature capable of conceiving infinite solutions needs an instrument capable of infinite adaptation.

Why upright posture? Four reasons. First, human senses serve knowledge, not just survival. An upright face can freely survey heaven and earth, gathering the raw material for understanding. Second, the brain, where interior cognitive activity occurs, is elevated above the other organs. Third, if man were prone, the hands would be locked into the role of forefeet, losing their universal utility. Fourth, a prone posture would require a protruding mouth with thick, hard lips and tongue, making speech, reason's proper instrument, impossible.

Man's orientation is also significant: his head faces the heavens, his feet face the earth. Plants are inverted (roots as mouth, pointing down). Animals are horizontal. Man alone is properly aligned between the higher and lower worlds.
elegant:
Why do dogs smell better and hawks see farther? Because the human body is optimized for something else entirely: the most balanced temperament of any animal, which the sense of touch requires, and the largest brain relative to body size, which intellectual life demands. That large, moist brain dulls smell. That balanced temperament costs speed. Every gain in one direction is a calculated concession in another.

Why no claws, no armor, no thick hide? Because man has reason and hands, which Aristotle called "the instrument of instruments." A fixed biological weapon serves one purpose. Hands guided by intelligence serve infinite purposes.

Why upright posture? Because human senses exist for knowledge, not just survival. The upright face surveys heaven and earth, gathering raw material for understanding. The elevated brain houses the interior powers of thought. Free hands, unburdened from locomotion, become universal tools. And the mouth, liberated from the need to grasp food from the ground, becomes the organ of speech, reason's proper instrument.

Man's orientation is itself significant. Head toward heaven, feet toward earth. Plants are inverted. Animals are horizontal. Only man is aligned between the higher and lower worlds.

refashioned:
Does Scripture's account of creating man fit the theology? Yes, on every count. The special language ("Let us make man") signals not that God needed help, but that human creation received special deliberation, reflecting the plurality of divine Persons whose image is most clearly expressed in man. The "breathing" of life into the face is not a separate act from making the body; it explains what makes the body alive. The soul is the form of the body, and the face is mentioned because that is where vital operations are most visible through the senses.

Body and soul were not produced sequentially. It would be unfitting for God to make a body without a soul or a soul without a body, since each is only a part of human nature, and the body depends on the soul rather than the reverse. The mention of "male and female" alongside "the image of God" does not mean the image resides in sex; it means the image (which is in the mind, where there is no sexual distinction) belongs to both sexes equally.
elegant:
Scripture's special language for the creation of man is deliberate. "Let us make" signals not divine need but divine deliberation, reflecting the plurality of Persons whose image is most fully expressed in the human creature. "He breathed into his face the breath of life" is not a separate event from forming the body; it is the explanation of what makes the body alive. The soul is the form of the body. The face is named because that is where life's operations are most visible.

Body and soul were not produced in sequence. Each is only a part of human nature; neither is complete alone, and the body depends on the soul rather than the reverse. To make one without the other would have been unfitting for the Author of complete natures.

refashioned: Four questions about the creation of woman: (1) Should woman have been made in the original creation? (2) Should she have been made from man? (3) From his rib specifically? (4) Was she made directly by God?
elegant: Four questions attend the creation of woman: whether she should have been made at all, whether from man, whether from his rib specifically, and whether directly by God.

refashioned:
Woman was necessary, but specifically as a partner in generation, not as a general helper. For any other kind of work, another man would have been more efficient. The distinction between male and female follows from the logic of living things.

In the simplest organisms, reproductive power is built into every individual. In plants, male and female functions are permanently united. In perfect animals, the active and passive generative powers are separated into male and female because animal life has higher activities than reproduction. The sexes need not be permanently joined; they unite only for the act of generation. In humans, the separation is even more pronounced because human life is ordered to something higher still: intellectual activity. The female was therefore produced separately from the male, though they are united in the flesh for generation.
elegant: Woman was necessary, but as a partner in generation, not as a general assistant. For any other kind of work, another man would have been more efficient. The distinction between male and female follows the logic of living things. In the simplest organisms, reproduction is built into every individual. In plants, both functions are permanently united. In perfect animals, active and passive generative powers separate into distinct sexes because animal life has purposes beyond reproduction. In humans, the separation is most pronounced because human life is ordered to the highest purpose of all: thought. The female was produced separately from the male so that each could devote the fullness of their nature to intellectual life, united in flesh only for the work of generation.

refashioned:
Aristotle's claim that the female is a "defective male" applies only at the level of individual generation, where the male seed aims to produce a male likeness and sometimes fails due to material factors. At the level of nature's universal intention, which depends on God, woman is not defective at all but is deliberately included for the work of generation.

As for subjection: there are two kinds. Servile subjection, where the superior exploits the subject for his own benefit, only began after sin. But ordered governance, where the more capable direct others for the common good, would have existed even in innocence. Any community requires leadership, and natural differences in judgment would have made some kind of governance necessary. Inequality is not inherently a punishment.

The objection that God should not have made woman because He foresaw she would occasion sin proves too much. If God removed everything that would become an occasion of sin, the universe would be imperfect. The common good is not sacrificed to prevent individual evil.
elegant:
Aristotle's claim that woman is a "defective male" applies only at the level of individual generation, where the male seed aims to produce its own likeness and sometimes does not. At the level of nature's universal intention, woman is not defective but deliberately included. God intends both sexes.

Subjection has two forms. Servile subjection, where one exploits another for personal advantage, began only after sin. Ordered governance, where the more capable direct others for the common good, would have existed even in paradise. Any community requires leadership. Inequality is not inherently punishment.

The objection that God should not have made woman because she would occasion sin proves far too much. Remove everything that becomes an occasion of sin, and the universe would be imperfect. The common good is not sacrificed to prevent individual evil.

refashioned:
It was fitting that woman be made from man rather than independently. Four reasons. First, it gave the first man a dignity parallel to God's: as God is the principle of the whole universe, the first man was the principle of the whole human race. Second, it deepened the bond between man and woman. Knowing she came from him, man would love her more closely and cleave to her, as Genesis says. This was especially important for humans, who unlike other animals live together for life. Third, human marriage serves not only reproduction but domestic partnership, where each has a distinct role. The woman coming from man as her principle reflects this ordered partnership. Fourth, there is a sacramental dimension: the Church originates from Christ, as woman originated from man.

The objection that male and female should share the same material origin (both from earth) misses the point. Nature works from determinate matter in determinate ways. Divine power, being infinite, can produce the same species from any matter.
elegant:
It was fitting that woman be made from man. Four reasons converge. First, it gave Adam a dignity analogous to God's: as God is the principle of the universe, man was made the principle of the entire human race. Second, it deepened the bond between them. Knowing she came from him, he would cleave to her more closely. Third, human marriage is not merely reproductive but domestic, a lifelong partnership of complementary roles. Her origin from man reflects this ordered union. Fourth, there is a sacramental dimension: the Church springs from Christ as woman sprang from man.

Divine power is not bound by the determinism of natural generation. God can produce the same species from any matter.

refashioned:
Why from the rib specifically? To symbolize the social union of man and woman. She was not made from his head, because she should not dominate him. She was not made from his feet, because she should not be treated with contempt as a slave. She was made from his side, signifying partnership. There is also a sacramental meaning: from the side of Christ on the cross flowed blood and water, the sacraments on which the Church was established.

As for the physics: the rib was not simply stretched. Additional matter was created or converted by divine power, analogous to how Christ multiplied loaves by transforming nourishment, not by stretching bread. The rib belonged to Adam's perfection not as an individual but as the principle of the human race, much as seed belongs to the perfection of the one who generates. Its removal caused no pain because pain belongs to the fallen state.
elegant:
From the rib, not the head, not the feet. Not from the head, lest she dominate. Not from the feet, lest she be despised. From the side, signifying partnership. And from the side of Christ on the cross flowed blood and water, the sacraments on which the Church was built.

The rib was not stretched to become a woman. Divine power supplied additional matter, as Christ multiplied loaves not by stretching bread but by creating substance. The rib belonged to Adam's perfection not as an individual but as the principle of the race, the way seed belongs to the one who generates. Its removal caused no pain, for pain belongs to the fallen state.

refashioned: Woman was formed directly by God. In natural generation, an individual of a species is produced from determinate matter by the specific power of that species. Human beings are naturally generated from human seed. Producing a human being from anything other than the normal material (whether from earth for the man or from a rib for the woman) exceeds the capacity of any created power. Only the Author of nature can produce effects outside nature's ordinary course. Angels may have assisted in subsidiary ways, but the actual formation was God's direct work.
elegant: Woman was formed directly by God. Natural generation produces individuals from species-specific matter through the power already present in that species. But producing a human body from a rib exceeds every created power, just as producing one from earth does. Only the Author of nature can work outside nature's ordinary course. Angels may have assisted in minor ways. The actual formation was God's alone.

refashioned: The purpose of human creation is that man is made "to the image and likeness of God." Nine questions: (1) Is God's image in man? (2) In irrational creatures? (3) More in angels than in man? (4) In every person? (5) Does the image reflect the Trinity or just the divine essence? (6) Is it in the mind only? (7) In the soul's acts or its powers? (8) Only in relation to God as its object? (9) How do "image" and "likeness" differ?
elegant: The purpose of human creation is that we are made to the image and likeness of God. Nine questions probe what this means: whether the image truly exists in us, whether it extends to animals, whether angels bear it more perfectly, whether every person carries it, whether it reflects the Trinity or only the divine essence, whether it resides in the mind alone, whether it lives in acts or powers, whether it requires God as its object, and how "image" and "likeness" differ.

refashioned:
There is an image of God in man, but an imperfect one. An image requires two things: likeness and derivation. An egg resembles another egg but is not its image, because it was not copied from it. A reflection in a mirror is an image because it is derived from the original. Equality is not required for an image to exist, though a perfect image would lack nothing found in the original.

Man has a genuine likeness to God, derived from God as exemplar. But the likeness is not one of equality; the exemplar infinitely exceeds the copy. This is why Scripture says man was made "to" God's likeness, with the preposition signaling approach from a distance. The image is not in man's bodily shape but in his spiritual nature. Whereas Christ is simply "the Image" of the Father (being of identical nature), man is "to the image" (being of an analogous, not identical, nature).
elegant:
An image requires two things: likeness and derivation. One egg resembles another but is not its image, because it was not copied from it. A reflection is an image because it is derived from the original. Equality is not required, though a perfect image would lack nothing found in what it represents.

Man bears a genuine likeness to God, derived from God as exemplar. But the copy infinitely falls short of the original. This is why Scripture says man was made "to" God's image, with the preposition signaling approach from a distance, not arrival. Christ is the Image of the Father, being of identical nature. Man is "to the image," an analogy sketched in a lesser substance, the way a king's likeness appears on a silver coin.

refashioned:
The image of God is found only in intellectual creatures, not in animals or inanimate things. A generic resemblance or a shared accidental quality is not enough for an image; what is needed is likeness in species, or at least in the defining characteristic. Things resemble God in three ascending degrees: by existing, by living, and by understanding. Only the last approaches close enough to count as an image.

All creatures participate in some likeness to God, but only intellectual creatures have the specific likeness that constitutes an image. A worm may come from a man but is not his image, because the resemblance is too generic. Similarly, the beauty of the whole universe does not make the universe an image of God in the relevant sense. The image is found where God's defining characteristic, intellect, is specifically imitated.
elegant:
The image of God is found only in creatures that think. A generic resemblance or a shared accidental feature is not enough. Things resemble God in three ascending degrees: by existing, by living, and by understanding. Only the last approaches close enough to qualify as an image.

A worm may descend from a man but is not his image, because the likeness is too remote. The whole universe surpasses any individual creature in collective goodness, but the intellectual creature surpasses everything else in the intensity and specificity of its likeness to God. The image is found where God's defining characteristic, intellect, is specifically mirrored.

refashioned:
Angels bear God's image more perfectly than humans do, because the image consists primarily in intellectual nature, and angelic intellect is superior to human intellect.

There are secondary respects in which man resembles God more than angels do: man proceeds from man as God proceeds from God; the whole soul is in the whole body as God is present to the whole world. But these secondary resemblances only count as aspects of the divine image if the primary likeness (intellectual nature) is already present. Otherwise even animals would qualify. Since the primary criterion is intellectual nature, and angels excel in this, angels are more to God's image absolutely speaking, even though man surpasses angels in certain secondary respects.
elegant: Angels bear God's image more perfectly than we do, because the image consists primarily in intellectual nature, and angelic intellect surpasses human intellect. There are secondary respects in which man resembles God more closely: man proceeds from man as God proceeds from God; the soul pervades the body as God pervades the world. But these secondary likenesses only count as aspects of the divine image if the primary one, intellect, is already present. Otherwise even animals would qualify. Since the defining criterion is intellectual nature, and angels excel in it, they are more fully God's image. Man surpasses them only in the margins.

refashioned:
The image of God exists in every human being, because every person has an intellectual nature. But the image operates at three levels. First, every person has the natural capacity to understand and love God; this is the image of creation, shared by all. Second, those who actually know and love God through grace possess the image of re-creation, found only in the just. Third, those who know and love God perfectly possess the image of glory, found only in the blessed.

Both men and women bear the image equally. Genesis says "to the image of God He created him; male and female He created them" precisely to show the image belongs to both sexes, since it resides in the mind, where there is no sexual distinction.
elegant:
Every human being bears the image of God, because every human being has an intellectual nature. But the image operates at three depths. First, the image of creation: every person has the natural capacity to understand and love God. This belongs to all. Second, the image of re-creation: those who actually know and love God through grace. This belongs to the just. Third, the image of glory: those who know and love God perfectly. This belongs to the blessed.

Both men and women bear the image equally. It resides in the mind, where there is no sexual distinction.

refashioned:
Does the image of God in man reflect the Trinity of Persons, or only the divine essence? Some argue it must reflect only the unity of essence, since what the image imitates (eternity, intelligence, free will, goodness) belongs to the one divine nature, not to the distinction of Persons. Others worry that if the image reflected the Trinity, we could know the Trinity by natural reason alone, which is false.

Against this, Hilary argues that the very plurality of Persons is confirmed by the fact that man is made "to the image of God" using plural language.
elegant: Does the image of God in us reflect the Trinity of Persons, or only the single divine essence? Some argue it can only reflect what is common to all three Persons, since the attributes we imitate, intelligence, freedom, goodness, belong to the one nature. Others worry that if the image reflected the Trinity, we could know the Trinity by natural reason alone, which we cannot.

refashioned:
The image reflects both the divine nature and the Trinity. The distinction of divine Persons follows from the divine nature (being distinguished by relations of origin that are suited to that nature). So imitating the divine nature does not exclude representing the Persons; in fact, one follows from the other.

The objection that this would make the Trinity knowable by natural reason fails because the image in us is deeply imperfect. As Augustine says, we see the trinity within ourselves but only believe the Trinity in God. The gap between our interior life of thought and love and the divine processions is vast enough that the image does not deliver demonstrative knowledge of the Trinity.
elegant:
The image reflects both the divine nature and the Trinity, because the two are inseparable. The distinction of Persons arises from the divine nature through relations of origin. To imitate the nature is already, implicitly, to represent the Persons.

The objection that this would make the Trinity knowable by reason alone fails because the image in us is deeply imperfect. As Augustine observed, we see the trinity within ourselves; we only believe the Trinity in God. The gap between our interior life of thought and love and the divine processions is vast enough that the image delivers suggestion, not demonstration.

refashioned:
Is the image of God found in the whole person or only in the mind? Paul says "the man" is God's image. Genesis ties the image to the creation of male and female. Shape seems relevant to images. And there are trinities in sense experience and imagination, not just in intellect.

Against these, Paul also says renewal "according to the image" consists in putting on the new man in the spirit of the mind. The image belongs to the mind specifically.
elegant: Is the image of God spread across the whole person, body and soul alike? Paul calls "the man" God's image. Genesis ties the image to the creation of male and female. Shape seems essential to what an image is. Yet Paul also says renewal "according to the image" consists in putting on the new man in the spirit of the mind. The image belongs to the mind specifically.

refashioned:
The image of God is in the mind alone. In other creatures, and in the non-rational parts of the human being, we find only a "trace" of God, not an image. The difference: an image represents by likeness in species; a trace represents the way an effect points to a cause without reaching specific likeness (footprints trace an animal, ashes trace a fire).

Rational creatures achieve a representation of species because they imitate God not only in being and living but in understanding. Other creatures show only traces of the intellect that created them. Similarly, in rational creatures we find a procession of word from intellect and love from will that mirrors, however imperfectly, the procession of the Word from the Father and Love from both. In non-rational creatures, we find only traces of these processions: their finite nature points to a principle, their species points to a designing word, their ordered purpose points to the maker's love. In the human body and senses, the likeness to God is by trace. In the mind, it is by image.
elegant:
In other creatures, and in the non-rational parts of the human being, we find only a trace of God, not an image. The distinction is precise. An image represents by likeness in species. A trace represents the way an effect points to a cause without reaching specific likeness: footprints trace an animal, ashes trace a fire, desolation traces an army.

Rational creatures achieve a representation of species because they imitate God not only in being and living but in understanding. In the mind's procession of word from knowledge and love from both, we find a distant but real mirror of the Word proceeding from the Father and Love from both. In everything below the mind, even in the human body's upright posture, the likeness to God is by trace only. The image lives in the mind alone.

refashioned:
The image of God is not in every part of man but is impressed on his mind, as a coin bears the image of a king without every part of the coin being the image. The distinction of male and female does not constitute the image; Scripture mentions both sexes immediately after mentioning the image precisely to show the image belongs to both, since it is in the mind where there is no sexual distinction.

The human body's upright shape does not contain the image but represents it by way of trace, pointing toward the image in the soul. And the trinities found in sensory and imaginary vision fall short of the divine image because their components are external or adventitious to the soul, unlike the mind's own acts of understanding and love, which more closely mirror the divine processions.
elegant:
The image is not distributed across every part of man but is impressed on his mind, as a coin bears the king's likeness without every particle of metal being the portrait. The distinction of male and female does not constitute the image; Scripture mentions both sexes immediately after mentioning the image precisely to show it belongs to both, since it resides where there is no sexual distinction.

The trinities found in sense perception and imagination fall short of the divine image because their components are external or borrowed. Only the mind's own acts of understanding and love, arising from within its own nature, approach the internal processions of the divine Persons.

refashioned:
The image of the Trinity is found primarily in the soul's acts, not just in its powers or habits. The divine Persons are distinguished by the procession of Word from Speaker and Love from both. In us, a word cannot exist without actual thought. Therefore the image is most fully present when we actually think and from that thinking produce an interior word, and from both break forth into love.

Powers and habits are the principles from which acts flow. Since acts virtually exist in their principles, the image can be considered secondarily as existing in the powers, and even more in the habits. But the primary locus is the act itself.
elegant:
The image of the Trinity lives most fully in the soul's acts, not merely in its powers or habits. The divine Persons are distinguished by the procession of Word from Speaker and Love from both. In us, a word cannot exist without actual thought. Therefore the image is most present when we think, produce an interior word from that thinking, and break forth from both into love.

Powers and habits are the springs from which acts flow. The image exists in them secondarily, as a fire exists virtually in the fuel. But the primary locus is the act itself: the living moment of knowledge and love.

refashioned:
Augustine's trinities of mind-knowledge-love and memory-understanding-will both point to the image, with the latter being more precise because it distinguishes functions more clearly. The key insight is that the image resides most fully in actual thought and actual love, not merely in the habitual retention of knowledge. Memory, understanding, and will as Augustine uses them are not three separate powers but three aspects of the mind's activity.

The objection that an act does not always remain (while the image should be permanent) is handled by noting that even when not actively thinking, the soul always remembers, understands, and loves itself habitually. The image exists permanently in the habits and powers as principles, and fully when those principles are activated.
elegant: Augustine's trinities of mind-knowledge-love and memory-understanding-will both point toward the image, with the latter being more precise. The key insight: the image resides most fully in actual thought and actual love, not in the habitual retention of knowledge. Even when the mind is not actively thinking, it always remembers, understands, and loves itself habitually. The image exists permanently in the habits as latent fire, and fully when those habits kindle into act.

refashioned:
The image of God in the soul is most properly found when the mind turns toward God, not when it contemplates any random object. Different objects produce specifically different words and loves in the mind. The Word of God is born of God's self-knowledge, and divine Love proceeds from God's self-love. So the truest image in us is the word born of knowing God and the love proceeding from that knowledge.

This does not mean the image disappears when we think about other things. The mind's capacity to turn toward God is itself a permanent natural feature. And when the mind knows itself, it indirectly mirrors God, since self-knowledge is a stepping stone to knowing the God who made it. But the image is most fully realized in direct knowledge and love of God.

Knowledge of temporal things, while it involves trinitarian structure (species, vision, intention), does not constitute the image properly, because such knowledge is adventitious and directed at objects beneath the mind. The image requires the mind to engage with what is above it.
elegant:
The image is most truly present when the mind turns toward God. Different objects produce specifically different words and loves. The Word of God is born of divine self-knowledge; divine Love proceeds from divine self-love. The truest image in us is the word born of knowing God and the love that flows from that knowledge.

When the mind knows itself, it indirectly mirrors God, since self-knowledge is a stepping stone to knowing the one who made it. But knowledge of temporal things, however trinitarian in structure, does not constitute the image. The image requires the mind to engage with what is above it, not below.

refashioned:
For the image to hold, what proceeds must be a word of God proceeding from knowledge of God. A mere trinity of cognitive elements about any object is not enough.

The knowledge that constitutes the image can be either natural (the mind's innate capacity to know God through reason) or gracious. Even when the image is "obsolete" in those without reason's use, "obscured" in sinners, or "clear" in the just, it never fully vanishes because the natural aptitude to know God always remains.

In the beatific vision, even temporal things will be seen in God and through God's unchangeable truth. At that point, the contemplation of all things will belong to the image, because everything will be known through the divine Word.
elegant:
For the image to hold, what proceeds in us must be a word of God proceeding from knowledge of God. A mere trinity of cognitive elements about any object is not sufficient.

Even so, the image never fully vanishes. The natural capacity to know God always remains, whether "obsolete" in those without reason's use, "obscured" in sinners, or "clear and beautiful" in the just. In the beatific vision, even temporal things will be seen in God and through God's unchangeable truth. At that point, the contemplation of all things will belong to the image, because everything will be known through the divine Word.

refashioned:
"Likeness" and "image" are not redundant terms. Likeness is broader than image: wherever there is an image there is likeness, but not vice versa. The two can be distinguished in two ways.

First, likeness as a preamble to image. Here likeness covers the more general features (incorruptibility of the soul, for instance) while the image proper is found specifically in the intellectual nature. In this sense, the mind is to God's image, while the body and lower faculties bear God's likeness as a trace.

Second, likeness as the perfection of image. Here likeness signifies how well the image succeeds. An image is "like" its original when the representation is good and "unlike" when it falls short. In this sense, likeness adds a qualitative dimension: specifically, likeness of power and virtue. The image gives you the structure (intellect, will, self-movement); the likeness of virtue tells you how well that structure is being used.
elegant:
"Image" and "likeness" are not redundant. Likeness is the genus; image is the species. Wherever there is an image there is likeness, but not the reverse.

The two can be distinguished in two ways. First, likeness as preamble to image: likeness covers the more general features (the soul's incorruptibility, its simplicity) while the image proper is found specifically in intellectual nature. In this sense, the mind bears God's image while the body and lower faculties bear only God's likeness.

Second, likeness as the perfection of image: an image is "like" its original when the representation succeeds, "unlike" when it falls short. In this sense, likeness of power and virtue tells you how well the image is being used. The image gives you the structure. The likeness tells you whether the structure is fulfilled.

refashioned: Likeness is not distinct from image in the general sense (since every image involves likeness) but in the specific sense: either as falling short of the image (covering features too general to constitute the image proper) or as perfecting it (indicating the quality of the image's realization). The soul's essence belongs to the image insofar as it represents the divine essence in intellectual nature, not in generic features like simplicity or indissolubility. Natural seeds of virtue in the soul can ground a natural "likeness," and the two terms can apply to different aspects of the same reality. Love of the word (knowledge loved) belongs to image; love of virtue belongs to likeness.
elegant: Likeness is not distinct from image in the general sense, since every image involves likeness. It is distinct in the specific sense: either as falling short of the image (covering features too broad to constitute it) or as perfecting it (measuring how well the image is realized). The soul's essence belongs to the image insofar as it represents the divine essence in intellectual nature. Love of the word, knowledge beloved, belongs to image. Love of virtue belongs to likeness. The two terms map the same reality from different angles.

refashioned: We now consider the first man's condition, starting with his intellect. Four questions: (1) Did he see God's essence? (2) Could he see angels directly? (3) Did he possess all knowledge? (4) Could he be deceived?
elegant: The first man's mind: four questions. Did Adam see God as God sees Himself? Could he perceive angels directly? Did he possess all knowledge? And was he capable of error?

refashioned:
The first man did not see God in His essence. The proof is simple: anyone who sees God's essence possesses beatitude, and no one possessing beatitude can willingly turn away from God, because beatitude is what everyone necessarily desires. Since Adam sinned, he could not have been seeing God's essence.

But Adam knew God more perfectly than we do now, in a way midway between our current knowledge and the beatific vision. We know God through sensible creatures, and our engagement with the sensible world distracts us from purely intelligible contemplation. In Adam, the lower powers were perfectly subject to reason, and reason was not dragged down by sensory preoccupation. He could contemplate God's intelligible effects clearly and steadily, receiving a kind of direct illumination from the First Truth. His knowledge was not the beatific vision, but it was far clearer than anything we achieve in our present distracted condition.
elegant:
Adam did not see God in His essence. The proof is devastatingly simple: anyone who sees God's essence possesses beatitude, and no one possessing beatitude can willingly turn from God. Adam sinned. Therefore he did not see God's essence.

But he knew God far more clearly than we do, in a way midway between our clouded present knowledge and the unveiled vision of heaven. We know God through sensible things, and our engagement with the sensible world constantly distracts us from higher contemplation. In Adam, the lower powers were perfectly subject to reason. Nothing in his nature dragged him downward. He could contemplate God's intelligible effects with a clarity and steadiness we cannot achieve, receiving illumination from the First Truth as directly as a mind still bound to images can receive it.

refashioned: Adam was happy in paradise, but not with the final happiness of seeing God's essence. He had a natural perfection and integrity that constituted a genuine, if incomplete, blessedness. His good will was well-ordered precisely because it did not desire the final reward before the time of merit had been completed. He knew God without the obscurity caused by sin (where sensory preoccupation clouds intellectual vision) but not without the inherent limitation of knowing God through creatures rather than directly.
elegant: Adam was happy in paradise, but not with the final happiness of seeing God face to face. He possessed a genuine, if incomplete, blessedness: the natural integrity and perfection of an unfallen creature. His will was well-ordered precisely because it did not grasp at the final reward before the time of merit was completed. He knew God without the obscurity that sin imposes, but not without the inherent limitation of knowing God through creatures rather than directly.

refashioned:
Could Adam see angels in their essence? No, and for a principled reason. The human soul, even in the state of innocence, was adapted to govern the body and therefore naturally understood by turning to images derived from sense experience. This mode of knowing proceeds in three stages: the soul turns from external things to concentrate on itself, then rises toward association with higher intelligences, then ascends toward God.

The first stage yields genuine self-knowledge. But the second stage does not yield direct knowledge of angels, because angelic understanding operates on entirely different principles than ours. Knowing how we ourselves think does not tell us what it is like to think as an angel does. The third stage falls even shorter of knowing God directly.

Adam knew the angels better than we do, because his intellectual life was clearer and less obstructed. But "better" does not mean "directly." He had a more excellent apprehension of intelligible realities, but the gap between human and angelic cognition remained.
elegant:
Adam could not see angels in their essence. The human soul, even unfallen, is adapted to govern the body and therefore naturally understands by turning to images drawn from sense experience. This mode of knowing proceeds in three movements: inward from external things to self-knowledge, upward toward association with higher intelligences, and higher still toward God.

The first movement yields genuine self-knowledge. The second falls short of knowing angels directly, because angelic thought operates on entirely different principles. Knowing how we ourselves think tells us nothing about what it means to think as an angel does. The third movement falls even shorter of knowing God.

Adam knew angels better than we do, because his intellectual life was clearer and less obstructed. But "better" is not "directly." The gap between human and angelic cognition remained.

refashioned: Innocence did not change the soul's fundamental nature or mode of operation. The state of innocence differs from our current state not by giving the soul a different kind of existence but by removing corruption while preserving natural integrity. The soul still understood by turning to sense-derived images. Adam's limitation in knowing angels was not because his body weighed down his soul (as ours does now) but because the soul's natural cognitive object simply falls short of angelic reality. We have both obstacles; Adam had only the second.
elegant: Innocence did not change the soul's nature. It removed corruption while preserving natural integrity. The soul still understood by turning to sense-derived images. Adam's inability to know angels directly was not because his body burdened his soul (as ours does) but because the soul's natural cognitive object simply falls short of angelic reality. We suffer both limitations. Adam suffered only the second.

refashioned:
Adam possessed knowledge of everything humans have a natural aptitude to know, plus the supernatural truths necessary for directing human life. The reasoning: perfection precedes imperfection in the natural order, as act precedes potentiality. God created things not only for themselves but as principles of other things. Since Adam was to be the father and teacher of the entire human race, he needed comprehensive knowledge from the start.

This included everything virtually contained in self-evident principles (the full range of natural knowledge) and enough revealed truth to direct human life toward its supernatural end. What he did not know were things that exceed human capacity and are unnecessary for life's direction: other people's private thoughts, future contingent events, trivial individual facts.

This knowledge came through divinely infused species, but it was not different in kind from natural knowledge, just as miraculously given eyes are not different from natural ones. Adam would have advanced in natural knowledge, not by learning new truths but by experiencing practically what he already knew theoretically. In supernatural knowledge, he would have advanced through further revelation.
elegant:
Adam possessed knowledge of everything humans can naturally know, plus the supernatural truths necessary for directing human life. The reasoning: perfection precedes imperfection, act precedes potentiality. God created things not only for their own existence but as principles of other things. The first man was the father and teacher of the whole human race. He needed comprehensive knowledge from the start.

What he did not know: other people's private thoughts, future contingent events, trivial particulars like the number of pebbles in a stream. What he would have gained over time: not new truths, but experiential confirmation of what he already knew theoretically. His knowledge came through divinely infused species, but it was not different in kind from natural knowledge, as miraculously given eyes are no different from natural ones.

refashioned:
Could Adam have been deceived? No. As long as the state of innocence persisted, it was impossible for the human intellect to assent to falsehood. Truth is the good of the intellect; falsehood is its evil. In a state where no evil could exist (because sin was absent), no false judgment could exist either.

The deeper reason is structural. The rectitude of the original state meant the soul was subject to God, and the lower faculties were subject to reason. Deception in the intellect never comes from the intellect itself (which, regarding its proper object, is always true) but from interference by lower faculties like imagination. When reason's natural judgment is free and undistorted, we are not deceived by phantasms, as we see from the fact that waking people are not fooled by dream-images. In the state of innocence, reason was fully free.

Some argued Adam could have been deceived in matters beyond his knowledge, as long as he did not firmly assent. But this is inconsistent with the integrity of the original state. Even a tentative entertaining of falsehood would be an evil of the intellect, incompatible with a condition where no evil existed.
elegant:
In the state of innocence, the intellect could not assent to falsehood. Truth is the good of the intellect; falsehood is its evil. In a state where no evil existed, no false judgment could exist either.

The deeper reason is structural. The rectitude of the original state meant the soul was subject to God and the lower faculties subject to reason. Deception never comes from the intellect itself, which regarding its proper object is always true. It comes from interference by imagination, sensation, or desire. When reason's natural judgment operates freely and undistorted, we are not deceived by phantasms. A waking person is not fooled by dream-images. In the state of innocence, reason was always fully awake.

refashioned:
The woman was deceived before she sinned outwardly, but not before she had already sinned inwardly through pride. She could not have believed the serpent unless she had already given in to the love of her own power. As for the serpent speaking, she may have thought it had received this ability supernaturally, not naturally.

If something false had been presented to Adam's senses or imagination, his reason would have judged correctly. He was not accountable for what might occur during sleep, since reason is not operative then. If someone had told him something false about future events or secret thoughts, he would not have believed it was true but might have considered it possible, which is not a false opinion. In every case, either reason would have corrected the error, or divine guidance would have prevented it. After he had already sinned in his heart, that guidance was forfeit.
elegant:
Eve was deceived, but not before she sinned. She could not have believed the serpent unless she had already given way to pride in her own heart. The external deception followed the internal fall, not the reverse.

If something false had been presented to Adam's senses, his reason would have judged correctly. During sleep, when reason is suspended, he was not accountable. If someone had told him something false about future events, he would not have believed it was so but might have considered it possible, which is not a false opinion. In every case, either reason would have corrected the error or divine guidance would have prevented it. After he sinned in his heart, that guidance was forfeit.

refashioned: Two topics under the first man's will: (1) grace and righteousness, (2) dominion over other things. On grace and righteousness, four questions: (1) Was man created in grace? (2) Did he have passions? (3) Did he have all virtues? (4) Were his actions less meritorious than ours?
elegant: The first man's will, examined in two dimensions: his grace and righteousness, and the dominion that flowed from them. Four questions open the first: Was Adam created in grace? Did he have passions? Did he possess all virtues? Were his actions less meritorious than ours?

refashioned:
The first man was created in grace, not in a state of mere nature. The argument is structural. The rectitude of the original state consisted in reason being subject to God, the lower powers subject to reason, and the body subject to the soul. The first subjection was the cause of the other two. When reason lost its subjection to God through sin, the lower powers immediately rebelled against reason, as Augustine notes: they "felt the impulse of disobedience in the flesh, as though it were a punishment corresponding to their own disobedience."

Now, the subjection of the lower powers to reason was clearly not natural; otherwise it would have survived sin (natural gifts remained even in fallen angels). Therefore the primary subjection of reason to God, which caused it, was also not natural but supernatural, a gift of grace. An effect cannot exceed its cause. If the loss of grace dissolved the lower powers' obedience, then grace must have been what sustained it. Therefore man was created with grace from the first moment.
elegant:
Adam was created in grace, not in bare nature. The argument is structural and irreversible. The rectitude of the original state consisted in reason being subject to God, the lower powers subject to reason, and the body subject to the soul. The first subjection caused the other two. When reason's submission to God was broken by sin, the lower powers immediately rebelled.

Now the subjection of body to soul and passion to reason was clearly not natural, because natural gifts survive sin, as they did even in the fallen angels. Therefore the primary subjection that caused these others was also not natural but supernatural, a gift of grace. An effect cannot exceed its cause. If the loss of grace dissolved the body's obedience, grace must have been what sustained it. Adam was created with grace from the first moment of his existence.

refashioned:
Paul's contrast between Adam as "living soul" and Christ as "life-giving spirit" refers to the body's mode of life, not the soul's spiritual condition. Adam had spiritual life in his soul (grace) but not the glorified spiritual body that Christ inaugurates. Augustine clarifies that Adam possessed the Holy Spirit to some degree, though not in the way the faithful do now, who are admitted to eternal happiness directly after death.

The objection that God wanted to show what free will could do before grace was given misunderstands Augustine's point: God showed what free will could do before being confirmed in grace, not before receiving grace at all. And while man was created in grace, it was by virtue of that grace, not the nature in which he was created, that he could advance by merit.
elegant:
Paul's contrast between Adam as "living soul" and Christ as "life-giving spirit" refers to the body's mode of existence, not the soul's spiritual condition. Adam had spiritual life in his soul through grace; what he lacked was the glorified, spiritual body that Christ inaugurates. He possessed the Holy Spirit, though not in the way the blessed do who enter eternal happiness immediately after death.

Grace was given not to nature as such but to the person within nature. Man was created in grace, but it was by virtue of that grace, not the nature in which he was created, that he could advance by merit.

refashioned:
Adam had passions in the state of innocence, but only certain kinds and in a distinctive way. He had no passions directed toward present or imminent evil (like fear or sorrow), because no evil was present or threatening. He had no burning concupiscence for goods not yet possessed. But he did have passions directed toward present good (joy and love) and future good expected at the proper time (measured desire and hope).

The crucial difference: in our current state, passions often run ahead of reason or resist it, because the sensory appetite is not fully subject to rational control. In Adam, the lower appetite was entirely governed by reason. Passions arose only as consequences of rational judgment, never as disruptions of it. This was not the absence of passion but its perfect ordering. As Aristotle notes, virtue does not eliminate desire; the temperate person desires what is appropriate, in the appropriate way.
elegant:
Adam had passions, but only certain kinds and in a distinctive mode. He had no fear, because no evil threatened. No sorrow, because no evil was present. No burning concupiscence, because no good was denied. But he had joy, love, measured desire, and hope that does not disappoint.

The crucial difference between then and now: in our present state, passions often run ahead of reason or resist it. In Adam, the sensory appetite was entirely governed by reason. Passions arose only as consequences of rational judgment, never as disruptions of it. This was not the absence of feeling but its perfect ordering. As Aristotle noted, the temperate person desires what is appropriate, in the appropriate way, at the appropriate time.

refashioned:
Adam possessed all the virtues, but in a nuanced way. The rectitude of the original state required that reason be subject to God and the lower powers to reason. Virtues are precisely the perfections that accomplish this ordering. So Adam necessarily had every virtue.

But a distinction is needed. Virtues that involve no inherent imperfection (like charity and justice) existed fully, both as habits and in act. Virtues that imply imperfection compatible with innocence also existed in both habit and act: faith (of things not yet seen) and hope (of beatitude not yet possessed), since Adam did not yet have the beatific vision.

Virtues that imply imperfection incompatible with innocence existed only as dispositions, not in act. Penance (sorrow for sin committed) and mercy (sorrow for another's suffering) could not be exercised where there was no sin or suffering. But Adam was so disposed that he would have repented if there had been cause, and would have helped anyone in need if need had existed. Think of how a virtuous person would feel ashamed if they did something wrong, even though they never do. The disposition is real; only the occasion is absent.
elegant:
Adam possessed all the virtues, but in a carefully calibrated way. The rectitude of the original state required that reason be subject to God and the lower powers subject to reason. Virtues are precisely the perfections that accomplish this. So Adam had every one.

Virtues that involve no inherent imperfection, like charity and justice, existed fully in both habit and act. Virtues whose imperfection is compatible with innocence, like faith and hope, also existed in habit and act, since Adam had not yet seen God face to face. But virtues whose imperfection is incompatible with innocence, like penance and mercy, existed only as dispositions, never activated. He was so disposed that he would have repented if there had been cause, and would have helped anyone in need if need had existed. The disposition was real; only the occasion was absent.

refashioned:
Temperance and fortitude do not essentially require excessive passions to subdue; they are per se competent to moderate passions of any intensity. In innocence, they operated on well-ordered passions rather than disordered ones.

Passions regarding evil in another (like hating the demons' malice) were compatible with innocence. So virtues related to such passions could exist fully. Passions regarding evil in the same subject (like sorrow over one's own suffering) were incompatible with innocence, so virtues exclusively related to these existed only as habits.

Adam had perseverance as a virtue (the habitual choice to continue in good) but not perseverance as an uninterrupted fact (since he eventually fell). Faith existed because the perfection of innocence did not include the beatific vision.
elegant:
Temperance and fortitude do not essentially require excess to subdue. They moderate passions of any intensity. In innocence, they operated on well-ordered passions rather than disordered ones. Passions regarding evil in another, like hating the demons' malice, were compatible with innocence. Passions regarding evil in oneself, like sorrow over personal suffering, were not.
