Adam had perseverance as a virtue, the habitual choice to continue in good, but not perseverance as an accomplished fact, since he eventually fell. The virtuous person may possess the disposition to remain standing without in fact remaining standing forever.

refashioned:
Were Adam's actions less meritorious than ours? Merit can be measured two ways: by its root (grace and charity) and by the degree of the action itself (either absolutely or proportionally).

By the root of grace, Adam's works were more meritorious. His grace met no obstacle in human nature and would have been more copious. Absolutely, his works were also greater because his virtue was greater. But proportionally, our works may merit more, because a small deed done with great difficulty exceeds our capacity more dramatically than a great deed done easily exceeds the capacity of someone strong.

The bottom line: Adam's advantage was absolute; ours is relative. We need grace for more things (remission of sin, support in weakness) but not more grace. Difficulty and struggle increase proportional merit and can satisfy for sin, but they do not prove our actions are simply more meritorious. Adam had no interior impulse toward evil, so he could resist temptation more easily, even without grace.
elegant:
Were Adam's actions less meritorious than ours, since he faced no struggle? Merit can be measured two ways. By its root in grace and charity, Adam's works were more meritorious: his grace met no obstacle and was more copious. Absolutely, his works were greater because his virtue was greater.

But proportionally, our works may merit more: a small deed done with great difficulty exceeds our capacity more dramatically than a great deed done effortlessly exceeds the capacity of someone strong. The widow's mite outweighs the rich man's gold in proportion to what each possesses.

Adam's advantage was absolute. Ours is relative. We need grace for more things, but we do not need it more. Difficulty increases proportional merit, but it does not make our actions simply greater than those of an unfallen will operating at full strength.

refashioned: Four questions about human dominion in the state of innocence: (1) Was man master over the animals? (2) Over all creatures? (3) Were all people equal? (4) Would some have governed others?
elegant: Human dominion in the state of innocence: four questions. Was man master of the animals? Of all creatures? Were all people equal? Would some have governed others?

refashioned:
Before sin, all animals were subject to man. Three arguments. First, natural order: the imperfect exists for the use of the perfect. Plants use earth, animals use plants, and man uses both. Dominion over animals is the natural culmination of this hierarchy. Second, divine providence: God governs inferior things through superior ones. Man, made in God's image, is the natural governor of animals. Third, the gap between human and animal cognition: animals have particular instinctive prudence regarding specific situations, while man has universal practical reason covering all situations. Whatever is partial is naturally subject to what is universal.

Before sin, man had not forfeited this natural authority through disobedience. As punishment for disobeying God, creatures that should have been subject to man disobeyed him in turn. The disorder we see now, animals fleeing or attacking humans, is the consequence, not the original condition.
elegant:
Before sin, all animals were subject to man. Three arguments converge. First, the order of nature: the imperfect exists for the use of the perfect. Plants use earth, animals use plants, man uses all. Second, divine providence: God governs the lower through the higher. Man, made in God's image, is the natural governor of what lacks that image. Third, the gap between universal and particular reason: animals have instinctive prudence for specific situations; man has universal practical reason for all situations. What is partial is naturally subject to what is comprehensive.

Before sin, man had not forfeited this authority through disobedience. The rebellion of creatures against human rule is the mirror of human rebellion against God. The disorder we observe now is consequence, not original condition.

refashioned:
Angels may have helped gather animals to Adam for naming, not because man lacked authority but because angels naturally have greater power over certain material tasks. Predatory animals were always predatory (sin did not change animal nature) but remained subject to man's governance nonetheless, much as domesticated predators today serve human purposes. Lions did not eat grass before the Fall.

In innocence, man had no bodily need of animals (no need for clothing, transport, or animal food) but used them for knowledge, studying their natures. Animals would have obeyed man voluntarily through their natural participation in instinctive prudence, as domestic animals obey their human masters now.
elegant: Predatory animals were always predatory. Sin did not change animal nature. Lions did not eat grass before the Fall. But natural antipathy between species did not exempt them from human governance, just as it does not exempt them from divine providence now. In innocence, man had no bodily need of animals, neither for clothing (they were naked without shame) nor for food (they ate from the trees) nor for transport (the body was strong enough). He needed them for one thing: knowledge. Animals would have obeyed voluntarily through their natural participation in instinctive prudence, as domestic animals obey now.

refashioned:
Man's dominion over creation corresponded to his dominion over himself. He contains all levels of reality: reason (shared with angels), sensation (shared with animals), vegetative life (shared with plants), and material body (shared with inanimate things).

Over angels, man had no authority, since reason is not their master but their peer or inferior. Over animals, man ruled by command, since the sensitive powers obey reason to some degree. Over plants and inanimate things, man ruled not by commanding but by using, since vegetative processes and material nature do not obey commands but can be employed without hindrance. Man had no power to alter the course of celestial bodies; that belongs to God alone.
elegant: Man's dominion over creation mirrored his dominion over himself. He contains all levels of reality: reason, sensation, vegetative life, and material body. Over angels, he had no authority; reason is not their master. Over animals, he ruled by command, since the sensitive powers obey reason. Over plants and inanimate things, he ruled not by commanding but by using, since vegetative processes and physical matter do not respond to commands but can be employed without hindrance. The course of the heavenly bodies he could not alter. That belongs to God alone.

refashioned:
Perfect equality would not have existed even in innocence. At minimum, there would have been diversity of sex (required for generation) and age (since children would be born over time). Beyond these, free will would have produced differences in virtue and knowledge, since people would have applied themselves to varying degrees. Even bodies would have differed in robustness, beauty, and size, since the human body was still subject to natural laws and influenced by climate, nutrition, and other factors.

But none of these inequalities would have involved defect or fault. The differences would have been in degree of perfection, not between perfection and imperfection. Order requires inequality; as Augustine says, order arranges equal and unequal things in their proper place. The beauty of ordered diversity was part of God's design, not a punishment.
elegant:
Even in paradise, perfect equality would not have existed. At minimum, diversity of sex and age would have obtained. Beyond these, free will would have produced differences in virtue and knowledge, since people would have applied themselves in varying degrees. Bodies would have differed in robustness, beauty, and size, shaped by climate, nutrition, and other natural factors.

But none of these inequalities would have involved defect or fault. The differences would have been in degree of excellence, not between excellence and deficiency. Order requires inequality. As Augustine says, order arranges equal and unequal things in their proper place. The beauty of ordered diversity was part of the original design, not a punishment for its failure.

refashioned:
There are two kinds of authority. The first is servile: one person uses another for his own benefit. This kind of dominion only began after sin and is inherently grievous, since no one willingly yields what should be his own. In innocence, this kind of mastership could not have existed.

The second is governance for the common good: directing free people toward their own welfare and the welfare of the community. This kind of authority would have existed even in innocence, for two reasons. First, man is naturally social, and any social group requires someone to attend to the common good. Many individuals, left to themselves, pursue many different ends; one person looking after the common interest is essential to organized life. Second, differences in knowledge and virtue would have existed, and it would have been wasteful for these advantages not to benefit others.

As Augustine says, just men command not through love of domination but through the service of counsel. Leadership in innocence would have been an act of service, not exploitation.
elegant:
There are two kinds of authority. Servile authority, where one person uses another for his own advantage, is inherently grievous and began only after sin. No one willingly yields what should be his own.

But governance for the common good, directing free people toward their own welfare and the welfare of all, would have existed even in innocence. Two reasons compel this. First, man is naturally social, and any society requires someone to attend to the common interest. Left to themselves, many individuals pursue many different ends; one person looking after the whole is essential. Second, natural differences in knowledge and virtue would have been wasted if they did not benefit others.

Just men command not through love of domination but through the service of counsel. In paradise, leadership would have been the purest form of service.

refashioned: Four questions about the first man's bodily condition: (1) Was he immortal? (2) Was he impassible? (3) Did he need food? (4) Would the tree of life have given immortality?
elegant: The first man's body, examined in four aspects: Was he immortal? Was he immune to suffering? Did he need food? And what role did the tree of life play in preserving him?

refashioned:
Man in the state of innocence was immortal, but not by nature. His body was not intrinsically incorruptible like an angel (which has no matter) or a celestial body (whose matter admits only one form). Rather, God gave the soul a supernatural power to preserve the body from all corruption, so long as the soul remained subject to God.

This makes theological sense: the rational soul surpasses the capacity of corporeal matter, so it was fitting that it received a power surpassing what matter could naturally sustain. When that supernatural gift was forfeited through sin, the body's natural corruptibility reasserted itself. Death entered through sin, not through nature.

This immortality was different from the immortality of glory promised as a reward. The original immortality was conditional, maintained by supernatural grace as long as obedience continued. The promised immortality of glory is permanent and absolute.
elegant:
Man was immortal in the state of innocence, but not by nature. His body was not intrinsically incorruptible like an angel, which has no matter, or a celestial body, whose matter admits only one form. God gave the soul a supernatural power to preserve the body from corruption, effective only so long as the soul remained subject to God.

This makes perfect sense. The rational soul surpasses the capacity of corporeal matter; it was fitting that it received a power surpassing what matter could naturally sustain. When that supernatural gift was forfeited through sin, the body's natural corruptibility reasserted itself. Death entered through disobedience, not through nature.

This immortality differed from the immortality of glory promised as a reward. The first was conditional, maintained by grace as long as obedience continued. The second will be permanent and absolute.

refashioned:
"Passion" has two senses. In the broad sense, any change counts (including sensation and sleep). In the strict sense, passion means being altered away from your natural disposition. Adam was passible in the broad sense (he could sense, sleep, and undergo natural processes) but impassible in the strict sense. No change contrary to his natural well-being could affect him, just as no corruption leading to death could occur. He could control passion as he could avoid death, so long as he refrained from sin.

His body was protected from external harm partly by his own reason (avoiding dangerous situations) and partly by divine providence, which ensured nothing harmful came upon him unexpectedly. The removal of the rib for Eve's creation was not a natural deterioration but was analogous to seminal emission: material provided for the generation of the species, not a loss to the individual.
elegant:
"Passion" in the strict sense means being altered away from your natural disposition. Adam could sense, sleep, and undergo natural processes, but nothing contrary to his natural well-being could touch him. He could control passion as he could avoid death, so long as he refrained from sin.

His body was protected from external harm partly by his own reason and partly by divine providence, ensuring nothing dangerous came upon him unawares. The removal of the rib was not a natural deterioration but was analogous to the provision of seed: material given for the generation of the species, not a loss to the individual.

refashioned:
Adam needed food. His immortality was not intrinsic but sustained by a supernatural power in the soul. Natural heat still consumed the body's moisture, and food was needed to replace what was lost. In the primitive state, man had an animal life requiring nourishment; after the resurrection, he will have a spiritual life needing none.

The distinction maps onto the soul's dual identity. As "soul" (what it shares with other living things), it gives animal life to the body, which requires the vegetative functions of nutrition, growth, and generation. As "spirit" (what is unique to it), it will eventually communicate immortality, impassibility, and glory to the body. In innocence, the first mode applied. After resurrection, the second.

Not eating would have been sinful, since God commanded Adam to eat from every tree except the forbidden one. Digestion would have produced waste, which God would have disposed in a way fitting to the state.
elegant:
Adam needed food. His immortality was not intrinsic but sustained by a supernatural power in the soul. Natural heat still consumed the body's moisture, and food was required to replace what was lost. In the primitive state, man had an animal life requiring nourishment; after the resurrection, he will have a spiritual life needing none.

The soul has two identities. As soul, what it shares with other living things, it gives animal life requiring the vegetative functions of nutrition, growth, and generation. As spirit, what is unique to it, it will eventually communicate immortality, glory, and power to the body. In paradise, the first mode governed. After resurrection, the second will.

refashioned:
The tree of life was a real cause of extended life but not of absolute immortality. The body faced two threats. First, natural heat consuming moisture, remedied by ordinary food from the other trees. Second, the gradual weakening of the body's assimilative power over time (the same process that causes aging: added nutrients progressively dilute the original vital force, as water added to wine eventually overwhelms the wine's character).

The tree of life addressed this second threat by strengthening the body's vital force against this progressive weakening. But its power was finite. It could preserve the body for a definite period, not forever. After that period elapsed, man would either need to eat from it again or be transferred to the spiritual life.

So the tree of life was genuinely medicinal but not a source of natural immortality. The soul's supernatural power of preservation remained the primary cause; the tree was a bodily supplement against one specific mode of decay.
elegant:
The tree of life was genuinely medicinal but not a source of absolute immortality. The body faced two threats: the ongoing loss of moisture to natural heat (remedied by ordinary food from the other trees), and the gradual weakening of the body's assimilative power over time, the process we call aging, where added nutrients progressively dilute the original vital force like water added to wine.

The tree of life addressed the second threat, strengthening the body's vital force against progressive decay. But its power was finite. It could preserve the body for a definite time, after which man would either eat from it again or be transferred to the spiritual life. The soul's supernatural power of preservation remained the primary cause; the tree was a bodily supplement against one specific mode of decline.

refashioned: Two questions about reproduction in the state of innocence: (1) Would generation have occurred? (2) Would it have been through sexual union?
elegant: Reproduction in the state of innocence: two questions. Would generation have occurred at all? And would it have been through sexual union?

refashioned:
Generation in the state of innocence would have been through sexual union, not through some alternative angelic or spiritual process. Some early thinkers, troubled by the nature of concupiscence, imagined the human race would have multiplied by divine power alone, without sex. But this is unreasonable. What is natural to man by virtue of his animal life was neither acquired nor lost through sin. Sexual generation is natural to man as an animal, as the design of the body makes obvious. These organs would not have been purposeless before the Fall.

Two elements must be distinguished. The first is the natural union of male and female, with active and passive generative principles. This belongs to the order of nature and would have existed in innocence. The second is the disordered excess of concupiscence that currently accompanies the act. This is a consequence of the lower powers' rebellion against reason and would not have existed in innocence, when the sensory appetite was fully subject to rational control.

As Augustine says, the reproductive organs would have been moved by the will like any other limb, without ardent or disordered incentive, with calmness of soul and body.
elegant:
Generation in innocence would have been through sexual union. Some early thinkers, troubled by the nature of concupiscence, imagined the human race would have multiplied by divine power alone, as angels do. But what is natural to man by virtue of his animal life was neither acquired nor lost through sin. The body's reproductive design is evident. These organs would not have been purposeless before the Fall.

Two elements must be distinguished. First, the natural union of male and female with their complementary generative powers, which belongs to the order of nature and would have existed in innocence. Second, the disordered excess of concupiscence that currently accompanies the act, which is a consequence of the lower powers' rebellion against reason and would not have existed.

As Augustine says: the reproductive organs would have been moved by the will like any other limb, without ardent or disordered incentive, with calmness of soul and body.

refashioned:
The comparison to angels fails: in paradise, man was angel-like in mind but still animal in body. Only after the resurrection will both soul and body be spiritualized. Our first parents did not have intercourse in paradise, either because they were expelled too soon after Eve's creation or because they awaited a specific divine command about timing.

The claim that sexual union makes man beast-like is precisely backward. What makes sexual behavior bestial is the absence of rational control over concupiscence, not the act itself. In innocence, reason would not have diminished sensory pleasure (indeed, Augustine notes that with purer nature and greater bodily sensitivity, pleasure would have been greater) but would have prevented concupiscence from dominating. A sober person enjoys food no less than a glutton; the difference is that the glutton's appetite controls him.

Virginal integrity would not have been compromised by intercourse in that state. As Augustine says, the union would have been one of deliberate action, not lustful desire, and conception and birth would have been free of the distortions that accompany them now.
elegant:
The comparison to angels fails. In paradise, man was angel-like in mind but still animal in body. Only after the resurrection will both be spiritualized.

What makes sexual behavior bestial is not the act but the absence of rational control. In innocence, reason would not have diminished pleasure. Augustine suggests it would have been greater, with purer nature and keener sensitivity. But concupiscence would not have dominated. A sober person enjoys food no less than a glutton. The difference is that the glutton's appetite controls him.

Continence would not have been praiseworthy in that state, because there would have been nothing disordered to restrain. Virginal integrity would not have been compromised by the act. Conception and birth would have been free of the distortions that accompany them now. The union would have been one of deliberate action, not lustful urgency.

refashioned: Two questions about children's bodies in the state of innocence: (1) Would infants have had full physical strength from birth? (2) Would all children have been male?
elegant: Two questions about the bodies of children born in innocence: Would they have had full physical strength from birth? And would all children have been male?

refashioned:
Children in the state of innocence would not have had full adult physical strength at birth, but they would not have had the helplessness we observe in infants now. Where revelation is silent, we must follow the natural order, and it is natural for human infants to lack strength because of the brain's large size and high moisture content, which impedes the nerves that control movement.

The original righteousness meant the body could not do anything contrary to the well-ordered will. But a well-ordered will desires only what is appropriate to its stage of life. An infant's will would not desire to perform adult actions. So children would have had enough strength for age-appropriate activities (nursing and the like) but not for everything an adult can do.

The weakness of infancy is natural and follows from birth, unlike the weakness of old age, which leads to corruption. Since man in innocence was born but not subject to corruption, infantile limitations would have existed but senile decline would not.
elegant:
Children would not have possessed adult strength at birth, but neither would they have suffered the helplessness we observe in infants now. It is natural for human newborns to lack strength because the brain is proportionally large and its high moisture content impedes the nerves that control movement.

The original righteousness meant the body could do nothing contrary to the well-ordered will. But a well-ordered will desires only what is fitting for its stage of life. An infant's will would not reach for adult actions. Children would have had strength enough for the activities of infancy but not for everything an adult can do.

Infancy's weakness is natural and follows from birth. Old age's weakness leads to corruption. Man in innocence was born but not subject to corruption. The limitations of beginning would have existed; the decline of ending would not.

refashioned:
Both sexes would have been born in the state of innocence. Diversity of sex belongs to the perfection of human nature, just as different grades of being belong to the perfection of the universe.

Aristotle's claim that the female is a "misbegotten male" refers only to the individual case, where the male seed aims to produce a male likeness. At the level of universal nature, the generation of females is fully intended. The cause of female births is not defect or failure but natural variation: climatic conditions, celestial influences, and even the parents' own will. In innocence, where the body was more subject to the soul, parents might have been able to determine the sex of their children by will alone.

Since all children would have been born to live animal lives requiring food and generation, all would have needed to reproduce, not just the first couple. This implies roughly equal numbers of males and females.
elegant:
Both sexes would have been born. Diversity of sex belongs to the perfection of human nature, just as different levels of being belong to the perfection of the universe. Aristotle's claim that woman is a "misbegotten male" refers only to the individual case. At the level of universal nature, the generation of females is fully intended.

In innocence, where the body was more subject to the soul, parents might have been able to determine the sex of their children by will alone. Since all children would have needed to reproduce, both sexes in roughly equal numbers would have been necessary.

refashioned: Two questions about children's moral condition: (1) Would they have been born righteous? (2) Would they have been born confirmed in righteousness, unable to sin?
elegant: The moral condition of children in innocence: two questions. Would they have been born righteous? And would they have been born confirmed in righteousness, unable to sin?

refashioned:
Children in the state of innocence would have been born in a state of original righteousness, including sanctifying grace. Original righteousness was not an individual trait of Adam's but an endowment of the entire human nature, a gift pertaining to the species. This is clear from the fact that original sin, its opposite, is transmitted precisely as a condition of the nature. If the corruption passes through nature, so would the righteousness have.

This grace would not have been natural in the sense of being transmitted through biological generation. Rather, God would have conferred it directly on each soul at the moment of its creation, just as the rational soul itself is not transmitted by the parents but infused by God when the body is ready to receive it. The mechanism is parallel: nature provides the body; God provides the soul and its grace.
elegant:
Children would have been born in a state of original righteousness, including sanctifying grace. The argument turns on what original righteousness is: not a personal trait of Adam's but an endowment of the entire human nature, a gift pertaining to the species. This is clear from the fact that its opposite, original sin, is transmitted as a condition of nature. If the corruption passes through nature, the righteousness would have as well.

The grace would not have been transmitted biologically. God would have conferred it directly on each soul at the moment of its creation, just as the rational soul itself is not generated by the parents but infused by God when the body is ready. Nature provides the body. God provides the soul and its grace.

refashioned:
Children would not have been born confirmed in righteousness. Confirmation in righteousness comes only through the beatific vision: once a rational creature sees God as He is, it cannot turn away, because God is the essence of goodness and nothing is desired except under the aspect of good. But Adam had not yet attained the beatific vision. Had he attained it, his animal life (and with it, the capacity for generation) would have ceased, replaced by the spiritual life. So no children could have been born to parents who were already confirmed.

This means children, like their parents, could have sinned by their own free will. They would not have inherited the necessity of sinning (as we inherit original sin's effects), but neither would they have been guaranteed sinlessness. Their preservation from sin would have depended on divine providence and their own choices, not on an irrevocable state.

The angel analogy does not apply: angelic free will becomes fixed after its first choice, while human free will remains changeable throughout mortal life.
elegant:
Children would not have been born confirmed in righteousness. Confirmation comes only through the beatific vision: once a rational creature sees God as He is, it cannot turn away, because God is the essence of goodness and nothing is desired except under the aspect of good. But Adam had not yet attained the beatific vision, and had he done so, his animal life, and with it the capacity for generation, would have ceased.

So no children could have been born to parents already confirmed. Children, like their parents, could have sinned by their own free will. Their preservation from sin would have depended on divine providence and their own choices, not on an irrevocable state. The angel analogy does not apply: angelic free will becomes fixed after its first choice, while human free will remains changeable throughout mortal life.

refashioned: Two questions about children's knowledge in the state of innocence: (1) Would they have been born with perfect knowledge? (2) Would they have had full use of reason from birth?
elegant: Two questions about the knowledge of children born in innocence: Would they have entered the world with perfect knowledge? And would they have had full use of reason from the moment of birth?

refashioned:
Children would not have been born with perfect knowledge. The soul naturally acquires knowledge through the senses; that is why it is united to a body in the first place. If the soul were fully equipped with knowledge at creation, the body would serve no cognitive purpose, which contradicts the soul-body union's rationale.

Adam's comprehensive knowledge was a special endowment for his unique role as father and teacher of the human race, not a feature of human nature as such. His children would have inherited the natural and gracious features of the species but not his individual privileges. They would have acquired knowledge over time through experience and learning, but more easily and without the obstacles we face.

This would not have constituted ignorance. Ignorance is the absence of knowledge that should be present at a given time. Children would have possessed whatever knowledge was appropriate to their stage of development. They would have had sufficient knowledge of universal moral principles to act rightly, and this natural moral knowledge would have been more complete than what we possess by nature now.
elegant:
Children would not have been born knowing everything. The soul naturally acquires knowledge through the senses; that is why it is united to a body in the first place. If the soul arrived fully equipped with knowledge, the body would serve no cognitive purpose, which contradicts the very reason for the union.

Adam's comprehensive knowledge was a special endowment for his unique role as father and teacher of humanity, not a feature of human nature as such. His children would have inherited the species' natural and gracious gifts but not his individual privileges. They would have learned over time, easily and without the obstacles we face.

This would not have been ignorance. Ignorance is the absence of knowledge that should be present. Children would have possessed whatever knowledge was appropriate to their stage. Even the holy angels have nescience of things not yet revealed to them.

refashioned:
Children would not have had full use of reason at birth, because the use of reason depends on the sensitive powers, which depend on bodily organs, and in infants the brain's high moisture content impedes neural function. This is a natural condition, not a punishment for sin.

However, children in innocence would have had more use of reason than infants do now, relative to the activities appropriate to their age. The general principle holds: nature proceeds from imperfect to perfect, and even in innocence, human development would have followed this trajectory. Some animals can walk at birth, but this reflects simpler neural requirements, not greater perfection. The most complex organisms need the most development time.
elegant:
Children would not have had full use of reason at birth. The use of reason depends on the sensitive powers, which depend on bodily organs, and in newborns the brain's high moisture content impedes neural function. This is a natural condition, not a punishment.

Nature proceeds from imperfect to perfect. Even in innocence, human development would have followed this arc. Some animals walk at birth, but this reflects simpler neural demands, not greater perfection. The most complex organisms need the most time to develop. Children in innocence would have had more use of reason than infants do now, but still only what their stage of growth allowed.

refashioned: Four questions about paradise: (1) Was it a physical place? (2) Was it suited to human habitation? (3) Why was man placed there? (4) Was man created there?
elegant: Paradise: four questions. Was it a physical place? Was it suited to human habitation? Why was man placed there? And was man created there, or brought there afterward?

refashioned:
Paradise was a real, physical place. Augustine identifies three positions: purely corporeal, purely spiritual, or both. The strongest view is both. Scripture narrates events in paradise as history (the planting of trees, the formation of Eve, the rivers), and historical narrative demands a real location.

Paradise was a garden in the east, situated in the most excellent part of the earth. The "east" carries symbolic weight (it is the "right hand" of the heavens, the nobler side), but the place itself was genuinely physical. The tree of life was a real tree whose fruit had life-preserving power, though it also carried spiritual significance. The tree of knowledge of good and evil was equally real, named for what would result from eating it.
elegant:
Paradise was a real, physical place. Scripture narrates events there as history: the planting of trees, the formation of Eve, the rivers that flowed through it. Historical narrative demands a real location. The strongest view, which Augustine endorsed, holds that paradise was both corporeal and spiritual: a genuine garden in the east, situated in the most excellent part of the earth, carrying also a spiritual significance that does not cancel its physical reality.

The tree of life was a real tree with genuine life-preserving power. The tree of knowledge was equally real, named not for a magical property but for what would result from eating it: the experiential knowledge of the difference between obedience and rebellion.

refashioned:
The claim that paradise reached to the moon should not be taken literally. It may mean figuratively that its atmosphere had the constant, temperate quality associated with celestial bodies. The rivers of paradise may have flowed underground for some distance before emerging at their known sources, a common phenomenon in geography.

The reason no explorer has found paradise is that it is cut off from the habitable world by impassable barriers: mountains, seas, or extreme climate. Its inaccessibility does not disprove its existence.
elegant: The claim that paradise reached the moon should not be taken literally. It may mean its atmosphere had the constant, temperate quality we associate with celestial bodies. The rivers may have flowed underground before emerging at their known sources, a common phenomenon. The reason no explorer has found it is that impassable barriers, mountains, seas, or extreme climate, cut it off from the habitable world. Inaccessibility does not disprove existence.

refashioned:
Paradise was perfectly suited to man's original state. The human body could be corrupted in two ways: internally by aging (loss of moisture, weakening of vital force) and externally by hostile environmental conditions. Paradise addressed both. Its temperate, pure atmosphere protected against external corruption. Its trees, including the tree of life, provided nourishment against internal decay.

Why not the empyrean heaven, where angels dwell? Because the empyrean suits angelic nature (purely spiritual, governing all corporeal creation from above) and the state of beatitude (maximally stable). Man's nature is not purely spiritual, and his original state was not yet beatitude. Paradise suited man as he was: embodied, immortal by grace, and on the way toward beatitude rather than already possessing it.
elegant:
Paradise was perfectly fitted to man's original state. The human body faced two kinds of corruption: internal aging and external environmental damage. Paradise addressed both. Its temperate, pure atmosphere shielded against external assault. Its trees, including the tree of life, nourished against internal decay.

Why not the empyrean heaven, where angels dwell? Because it suits angelic nature, which is purely spiritual and governs all corporeal creation from above, and the state of beatitude, which is maximally stable. Man's nature is not purely spiritual, and his original state was not yet beatitude. Paradise suited man as he was: embodied, immortal by grace, and on the way toward beatitude rather than already possessing it.

refashioned:
The empyrean heaven fits the angel's nature directly and serves as the abode of beatitude. It would have been man's final destination but not his starting point. Paradise was adapted to man as a composite of soul and body, providing what the body needed (temperate conditions) precisely because the soul's preserving power worked through bodily conditions.

No irrational animal naturally inhabited paradise, though God brought animals there for Adam to study and name, and the serpent entered through diabolical agency. Paradise did not become useless after man's expulsion; it demonstrated God's original generosity and what man lost through sin.
elegant:
The empyrean heaven was man's destination, not his starting point. Paradise was adapted to a composite being of soul and body, providing what the body needed precisely because the soul's preserving power worked through bodily conditions.

No irrational animal naturally inhabited paradise, though God brought animals there for Adam to study and the serpent entered through diabolical agency. Paradise did not become useless after man's expulsion. It stands as testimony to divine generosity and a measure of what was lost.

refashioned:
Man was placed in paradise "to dress it and keep it." This can be read two ways. First, God worked in man and kept him: sanctifying him (since without God's ongoing work, man falls into darkness) and preserving him from corruption and evil. Second, man cultivated and guarded paradise, but this cultivation would have been pleasant, not laborious. Before sin, work was a form of knowledge and delight, not toil. And "keeping" paradise meant keeping it for himself by not sinning, not guarding it against external trespassers.

Paradise was for man's benefit, not the reverse. Man was not created to serve the garden; the garden was created to serve man.
elegant:
Man was placed in paradise "to dress it and keep it." Read one way: God worked in man and kept him, sanctifying and preserving him, since without God's ongoing work the soul falls into darkness as air grows dark when light withdraws. Read another way: man tended the garden, but this labor would have been pleasant, not toilsome, a form of delighted knowledge rather than grudging work. And "keeping" paradise meant keeping it for himself by not sinning, not guarding it against trespassers.

Paradise was for man. Man was not for paradise.

refashioned:
Man was not created in paradise but was created outside it and then placed there by God. This arrangement makes a theological point: the incorruptibility of man's original state was not natural to him but was a supernatural gift. By creating man outside paradise and then transferring him there, God made clear that the blessed condition was His doing, not something inherent in human nature.

Angels, by contrast, were created in their proper abode (the empyrean heaven) because that place suits their nature directly. For man, paradise suited his supernatural endowment, not his bare nature. Woman was made in paradise not because of her own dignity but because of the dignity of the material from which she was formed (Adam's rib, which was already in paradise). Similarly, children would have been born in paradise because their parents were already there.
elegant:
Man was not created in paradise but was made outside it and placed there by God. The arrangement carries a theological truth: the blessed condition was God's gift, not something inherent in human nature. By making man elsewhere and transferring him, God made clear that the incorruptibility of the original state was supernatural, not natural.

Angels were created in their proper abode because the empyrean suits their nature directly. For man, paradise suited his supernatural endowment, not his bare nature. Woman was made in paradise not for her own dignity but because her material, Adam's rib, was already there. Children would have been born there because their parents already were. The place followed the gift, not the nature.

refashioned: Having addressed creation and the diversity of things, we turn to divine governance: first in general terms, then its specific effects. Eight questions frame the discussion: Does the world have a governor? What is the goal of that governance? Is there one governor or many? What are the effects of governance? Is everything subject to it? Does God govern everything directly? Can anything fall outside divine governance? Can anything resist it?
elegant: Does the universe drift, or is it steered? Aquinas opens the question of divine governance -- whether the world has a governor, what that governance aims at, whether it admits of exception, and whether anything can resist it.

refashioned:
The world is governed. The objection that natural things lack knowledge of their purpose, and therefore cannot be governed, misunderstands governance. An arrow does not know its target, yet its flight toward it proves an archer aimed it. The consistent patterns we observe in nature -- things regularly achieving beneficial outcomes -- are evidence of direction, not accident. A well-ordered house implies someone ordered it.

This follows also from God's goodness. If God brought things into existence, it would be inconsistent with supreme goodness to abandon them without guiding them toward their fulfillment. A thing's ultimate perfection lies in reaching its proper end. So the same goodness that caused creation also sustains governance.

Even things that seem stable still require governance. Created from nothing, they would collapse back into nothing without sustaining direction. And the natural necessity that determines how things behave is itself a kind of divine imprint -- like the archer's force impressed on the arrow. Natural law does not replace governance; it is governance operating through the natures of things.
elegant: An arrow does not know its target, yet its flight proves an archer aimed it. The regularity of nature -- things consistently reaching beneficial outcomes -- is not a lucky accident. It is evidence of direction. The same goodness that brought things into being sustains them toward their proper ends. Natural law is not an alternative to governance; it is governance written into the fabric of things.

refashioned:
The ultimate goal of the world's governance lies outside the world itself. The reasoning is straightforward: the cause of everything is external to the universe -- God -- and the end of a thing corresponds to its beginning. Since nothing within creation is good by its own essence but only by participation in goodness, the ultimate good toward which everything is directed must be the Universal Good that is good in itself: God.

This does not mean creatures gain nothing internally. A thing can attain an external good by possessing or participating in it, not only by containing it. The internal order of the universe is real and valuable, but it is not the final end. It is itself ordered to something beyond, the way the order of an army exists for the sake of the general's objective, not for its own sake.
elegant: The goal of the world's governance lies beyond the world itself. Nothing inside creation is good by its own essence -- every created good participates in a goodness it did not originate. The internal order of the universe is real and valuable, but it is not the final destination. It exists for the sake of something beyond it, the way the order of an army exists for the sake of the victory, not for its own beauty.

refashioned:
The world must be governed by one. Since the end of governance is the supreme good, governance must be of the highest quality. And the best governance is governance by one.

The reason: governance means directing things toward their end, and that end is some form of good. But goodness and unity are inseparable -- everything that desires good also desires unity, because a thing exists only insofar as it is one. Things resist division; dissolution comes from internal failure. So the goal of any ruler over a multitude is unity and peace, and one ruler is better suited to produce unity than many acting in coordination, since the many must first be united among themselves before they can unify anything else.

The diversity of movements and operations in the world does not indicate multiple governors. That diversity belongs to the things governed, not the governing principle. And when contraries oppose each other in their immediate effects, they still serve one overarching order in the universe.
elegant: The world must be governed by one, because unity is the condition of everything good. A thing exists only insofar as it is one; dissolution is internal failure. One ruler produces unity more directly than many acting in coordination, since the many must first be united among themselves before they can unify anything else. The diversity of the world's operations does not indicate multiple governors -- it belongs to the things governed, not to the governing principle.

refashioned: Divine governance produces three levels of effect. At the most fundamental level, there is one effect: everything becomes like the supreme good through participation. At a structural level, there are two effects: God preserves things in their existing goodness, and God moves things toward greater good. The creature becomes like God both by being good and by causing goodness in others. At the level of individual creatures, the effects are without number, since every particular thing governed represents a distinct instance of divine direction.
elegant: Divine governance produces a single, cascading effect: everything becomes like the supreme good through participation. God preserves things in their existing goodness and moves them toward greater good. The creature becomes like God not only by being good but by causing goodness in others -- a perfection within a perfection.

refashioned:
Everything without exception is subject to divine governance. The same reason God is the cause of all being is the reason He governs all being. God is not the cause of one particular kind of existence but of existence universally. Therefore nothing can exist outside His governance, just as nothing can exist without being created by Him.

This also follows from the nature of governance's end. God's governance aims at the divine goodness, and since everything is ordered to divine goodness as its ultimate end, nothing can escape that ordering.

The opinion that God does not govern corruptible things, individual things, or human affairs is therefore mistaken.
elegant: Nothing escapes divine governance, for the same reason nothing escapes divine creation. God is not the cause of one particular kind of existence but of existence universally. Whatever exists falls within His government, as surely as whatever burns falls within the domain of fire.

refashioned:
The fact that chance events occur in the natural world does not disprove divine governance -- it actually confirms it. Chance means that something happens outside the order of a particular cause, and this is only possible if those causes are themselves governed by something higher. Without governance, nothing would tend toward any definite outcome, and without definite outcomes, chance (which is deviation from expected outcomes) could not exist.

As for the objection that God has no care for irrational creatures: the point is not that they fall outside governance entirely, but that they are governed differently than rational creatures. God governs rational beings both by moving them internally and by addressing them through commands, rewards, and consequences. Irrational creatures are governed through their natures alone, without moral address.

And the rational creature's ability to govern itself through intellect and will does not exempt it from divine governance. Both intellect and will themselves require perfecting by the divine intellect and will. Self-governance operates beneath, not outside, God's governance.
elegant: Chance does not disprove governance -- it confirms it. Chance means deviation from a particular cause's expected outcome. But without definite outcomes, there could be no deviation. Without governance, nothing would tend anywhere, and without tendency, chance is meaningless. Even the random presupposes the ordered.

refashioned:
God governs all things directly in design but not in execution. The distinction matters.

Regarding the plan of governance -- providence itself -- God governs every detail immediately. The best governor is not the one who handles only broad strokes but the one who knows every particular. A great physician does not just know general medical principles; she attends to the specifics of each patient.

Regarding execution, however, God governs some things through intermediaries. This is not a deficiency but a mark of superior governance. A thing's perfection is greater when it is not only good in itself but also a cause of goodness in others. So God delegates causal roles to creatures, the way a master teacher not only teaches students but equips them to teach others.

Plato was wrong to divide providence itself among multiple levels of deity. An earthly king's use of ministers does not indicate weakness -- it magnifies the reach of royal authority. Similarly, God's use of secondary causes enhances rather than diminishes the order of creation.
elegant: God governs all things directly in design but not in execution. He knows every particular -- the best governor is not the one who handles only broad strokes but the one who attends to every detail. Yet He delegates causal roles to creatures, not from weakness but from generosity. A thing's perfection is greater when it is not only good in itself but also a cause of goodness in others.

refashioned:
Nothing can happen outside the order of divine governance taken as a whole, though things can happen outside the order of any particular cause within it.

The principle: every exception to a particular cause is itself caused by something else, and that something else is itself reducible to the universal first cause. If indigestion occurs despite the nutritive power, it is because of the coarseness of the food, which itself has a cause, and so on back to God. Since God is the universal cause of all being -- not just one type -- nothing can escape the total order.

This does not eliminate evil, chance, or contingency. Evil exists because things deviate from particular goods, but anything that entirely escaped divine order would cease to exist altogether. Chance is real relative to particular causes but does not exist relative to the universal cause. And contingent events are contingent relative to their immediate causes, which can fail, but every such failure itself falls within the broader divine order.
elegant: Nothing escapes the total order of divine governance, though things constantly escape particular orders within it. Every exception to a local cause is itself caused by something else, and that chain leads back to the universal first cause. Evil, chance, and contingency are real -- but they are real within the divine order, not outside it. Anything that entirely escaped that order would cease to exist.

refashioned:
Nothing can truly resist the order of divine governance, though things can resist particular goods within it.

Two reasons establish this. First, divine governance is entirely directed toward good, and everything by nature seeks good -- no agent acts intending evil as such. Second, every natural or voluntary inclination is an impression from the first mover, like an arrow's trajectory is an impression from the archer. Every agent, whether acting by nature or by free choice, ultimately reaches its divinely appointed end.

When people act "against God," they resist some particular good that belongs to their nature or situation, not the total order of providence. A sinner pursues a genuine good (pleasure, power, security) but in a disordered way. The resistance is real at the level of particular goods, which is why punishment is just. But the overall order of divine governance absorbs even rebellion into its total design.
elegant: Nothing truly resists divine governance, because everything by nature seeks good, and divine governance is entirely directed toward good. A sinner pursues a genuine good -- pleasure, power, security -- but in a disordered way. The resistance is real at the level of particular goods, which is why punishment is just. But the overall order absorbs even rebellion into its total design, the way an archer's force encompasses even the arrow that falls short.

refashioned: We now examine the specific effects of divine governance through four questions: Do creatures need God to sustain their existence? Does God preserve them directly? Can God reduce anything to nothingness? Does anything actually get annihilated?
elegant: Can creatures stand on their own once made, or do they need God's continuous hand to keep existing? Aquinas examines whether creation is a one-time event or an ongoing act.

refashioned:
Do creatures need God to keep them in existence, or can they persist on their own once created?

Several objections suggest they can. Some beings -- like angels or celestial bodies -- seem by nature incapable of ceasing to exist. A builder's house stands after the builder leaves, so surely God's creations can persist after the act of creation. And nothing violent happens without an active cause, yet no active cause can corrupt a spiritual substance.

But these objections misunderstand the relationship between creature and Creator.
elegant: A house stands after the builder leaves. Surely God's creations can persist after the act of creation. But this analogy conceals a fundamental difference between building and creating -- a difference that changes everything about the creature's dependence on its Maker.

refashioned:
Both reason and faith require us to say that creatures are kept in being by God continuously.

The key distinction: a cause can relate to its effect in two ways. Some causes produce the becoming of a thing but not its ongoing being. A builder causes a house to come into existence, but the house's continued existence depends on the nature of its materials, not on the builder. Once built, the house stands on its own because the builder did not cause the form of stone and wood as such -- he only arranged pre-existing materials.

But when a cause is responsible not just for a thing's becoming but for its very being -- for the form itself, not merely for its arrangement in matter -- then the effect cannot persist without the cause's ongoing action. The analogy: air is lit up by the sun. The sun does not merely start the light and leave; the air has no capacity to hold light on its own. The moment the sun stops acting, the air goes dark. Hot water, by contrast, retains heat after the fire is removed, because water can receive heat as its own quality.

Every creature relates to God as the air relates to the sun. God alone exists by His own essence -- His essence is His existence. Every creature has existence only by participation, borrowed from God's ongoing creative act. Withdraw that act for a single moment, and the creature would collapse into nothing. God's preservation of things is not a separate act from creation; it is the continuation of the same act by which existence is given.
elegant: The builder arranges materials whose existence he did not cause. Remove the builder, and the materials persist by their own nature. But God caused the existence itself. He is not the arranger of being but its source. Every creature relates to God as air relates to the sun: the sun does not merely start the light and leave -- the air has no capacity to hold light on its own. The moment the sun stops shining, the air goes dark. Withdraw God's sustaining act for a single instant, and the creature collapses into nothing. Preservation is not a separate act from creation; it is creation continued.

refashioned: The fact that some creatures seem incapable of corruption does not mean they are self-sustaining. Their potentiality for non-being lies not in their own nature but in God, who could withdraw His sustaining influence. God cannot make a creature that does not need Him, because the creature's dependence on God for being is not a feature that could be removed -- it is constitutive of what it means to be a creature rather than God. And God's preservation is not an additional action that adds something new to the creature; it is the continuous pouring-out of existence, the same act that first gave being.
elegant: God cannot make a creature that does not need Him, because needing God is not an accidental feature of creaturehood -- it is constitutive of it. To be a creature rather than God just is to have borrowed existence. The dependence cannot be removed without removing the thing.

refashioned:
God does not preserve every creature immediately. He preserves some things through other things.

This works in two ways. First, indirectly: certain creatures prevent other creatures from being corrupted, the way salt preserves meat. Second, directly: when an effect depends on a chain of causes, each intermediate cause plays a role in sustaining the effect. The first cause remains the primary preserver, but the middle causes genuinely contribute to preservation in a secondary way.

God created all things directly but established an order of dependence among them in the act of creation itself. Some creatures depend on other creatures for their continued existence, though all ultimately depend on God as the principal sustaining cause. This layered preservation is not a deficiency in God's power but the deliberate structure of a creation in which creatures participate in sustaining one another.
elegant: God preserves some things through other things -- the way salt preserves meat, the way a chain of causes sustains its final effect. This layered preservation is not a deficiency in divine power but the deliberate structure of a creation in which creatures participate in sustaining one another.

refashioned:
God can annihilate anything. Since God created freely -- not out of natural necessity -- He is equally free to stop sustaining a creature in existence. God's preservation of things is a continuous act, like the sun's continuous illumination of the air. Just as He was free not to create in the first place, He is free to withdraw His sustaining action, at which point the creature would simply cease to be.

This does not make God a direct cause of non-existence. Nothing directly causes non-being, since causation presupposes being. Rather, annihilation would be an indirect result: God ceasing to act, rather than God acting to destroy. Nor does this contradict God's goodness, since His goodness does not depend on creatures and was not compelled to produce them.
elegant: God can annihilate anything. Since He created freely, He is equally free to stop sustaining. Annihilation would not be God acting to destroy but God ceasing to act -- the withdrawal of a gift, not the infliction of a blow. Just as He was free not to create, He is free to uncreate.

refashioned:
In practice, God does not annihilate anything. While He has the power to do so, nothing in the natural order of things tends toward annihilation, and miracles serve the manifestation of grace rather than destruction.

Nature shows no tendency toward annihilation. Matter persists through all transformations -- forms come and go, but the underlying substrate endures. Even when forms and qualities cease to exist in one thing, they do not become nothing; the matter takes on new forms. And spiritual substances, which have no matter, are by nature incorruptible.

From the side of grace, annihilation would serve no purpose. The manifestation of divine power and goodness is better demonstrated by preserving things in existence than by reducing them to nothing. Scripture confirms this: "All the works that God has made continue forever."
elegant: In practice, God annihilates nothing. Nature shows no tendency toward annihilation -- matter persists through every transformation, forms come and go but the substrate endures. And from the side of grace, divine power is better displayed by preserving than by reducing to nothing. The works of God continue forever.

refashioned: We now consider how God acts on creatures -- not just sustaining them in existence but actively changing and moving them. Eight questions: Does God directly move the created intellect? Can God move the will? Does God work in every agent? Can God do anything outside the natural order? What are miracles? Why are there different grades of miracle? Can God perform miracles through creatures? And: can God do anything beyond what He has established as the natural order?
elegant: God does not merely sustain creation in static existence. He moves it. Aquinas now asks how -- examining divine action on intellect, will, natural agents, and the possibility of miracles.

refashioned:
God can directly illuminate the created intellect. The intellect is a power of the soul, and like all created powers, it exists as potentiality that requires activation. Just as the senses need sensible objects and a medium (like light for sight), the intellect needs both intelligible content and intellectual light to understand.

A created intellect can fail to grasp something for two reasons: lack of the relevant intellectual light, or lack of the intelligible content itself. God can supply both. He can strengthen the intellect's natural light -- granting greater capacity to understand -- and He can impress intelligible forms directly, providing content the intellect could not derive from sense experience alone.

God does not thereby violate the intellect's nature. He acts as the source of all intellectual light, supplementing what the natural light cannot reach on its own. Prophetic knowledge is an example: the prophet receives both enhanced light and new intelligible content directly from God.
elegant: God can directly illuminate the created mind. The intellect, like sight, needs both content and light to operate. God can supply both -- strengthening the mind's natural capacity and impressing truths that experience alone could never reach. He acts not as a foreign invader but as the source of all intellectual light, extending what the natural lamp cannot reach on its own.

refashioned:
Can God move the human will? Yes -- and only God can move the will directly from within, because the will's proper object is the universal good, and only the universal good itself (God) is proportionate to that object.

Any created good can present itself as desirable under some aspect, but no created good can necessitate the will, because the will is oriented toward goodness as such. Only what perfectly fulfills that orientation can move the will from within as a sufficient cause. External objects and other agents can present goods to the will, but they move it only indirectly -- by presenting something attractive. God alone moves the will as an interior principle, inclining it from within without destroying its freedom.
elegant: Only God can move the will from within. Every created good attracts the will under some particular aspect, but no created good can satisfy it entirely, because the will is oriented toward goodness as such. Only the universal good itself -- God -- is proportionate to that infinite appetite. External objects propose; God alone disposes.

refashioned:
Being moved by God does not destroy freedom. The will is by nature a voluntary, self-moving power, and God moves it in accordance with its nature -- not by force but by interior inclination. Just as God moves natural things according to their natures (heavy things downward, light things upward) without making their motion violent, so He moves the will according to its nature as a free power, without making its motion involuntary.

God is the universal cause who works through the particular natures of things. He does not override the will any more than He overrides gravity. He is the reason the will can act at all, and His motion is what makes voluntary action possible rather than what threatens it.
elegant: Being moved by God does not destroy freedom. God moves the will according to its nature -- as a free power, not a forced one. He does not override the will any more than He overrides gravity. He moves heavy things downward and free things freely. His motion is what makes voluntary action possible, not what threatens it.

refashioned:
God works in every agent that operates. Not alongside the agent as a separate cause, but as the foundational cause that makes the agent's own causality possible.

This happens in three ways. First, God gives and sustains the forms and powers by which agents act. A thing acts through its form, and that form exists only because God gives and maintains it. Second, God sustains the forms and powers in their activity -- they would lose their operative capacity the moment God withdrew His sustaining influence, just as a thing loses existence when God ceases to uphold it. Third, God applies every agent to its action. No secondary cause moves from potency to act except by being moved by a prior cause, and this chain leads ultimately to God as the first mover.

So God is not merely the originator who set things in motion and stepped back. He is actively involved in every operation: giving the power, maintaining the power, and applying the power to its act. The secondary cause genuinely acts -- it is not an illusion -- but it acts by a power that derives from and depends on God at every moment.
elegant: God works in every agent that operates -- not alongside it as a separate cause but as the foundation that makes its causality possible. He gives the power, sustains the power, and applies the power to its act. The secondary cause genuinely acts. But it acts by a power that derives from and depends on God at every moment, the way a pen genuinely writes but only by the hand that moves it.

refashioned:
God can produce effects in the natural world without using natural causes -- that is, He can act outside the established natural order. This follows from two considerations.

First, the natural order is not imposed on God; it flows from God. Just as someone who establishes a system can also operate outside that system when there is reason to, God who established nature can act beyond it. Second, the natural order reflects the current arrangement of created causes, but God's power is not limited to what those causes can produce. He can produce effects that no natural cause could produce (like creating something from nothing), effects that natural causes could produce but in a different order or manner, and effects in matter that is not naturally disposed to receive them.

This does not mean God acts arbitrarily or contradicts Himself. Nature's order reflects divine wisdom, and acts outside that order also reflect divine wisdom -- serving purposes (like the manifestation of grace) that the regular order alone cannot accomplish.
elegant: God can act outside the natural order because the natural order is not imposed on Him -- it flows from Him. The one who established the system can operate beyond it. Nature's order reflects divine wisdom, and exceptions to that order also reflect divine wisdom, serving purposes the regular pattern alone cannot accomplish.

refashioned: When God acts outside the natural order in a way that is observable, we call it a miracle. The word itself comes from wonder -- what provokes astonishment because its cause is hidden. A miracle in the strict sense is something that surpasses the entire order of created nature, not just the capacity of one particular natural cause.
elegant: A miracle is what happens when God acts outside the natural order in a way we can observe. The word comes from wonder. In the strict sense, a miracle surpasses not just one natural cause but the entire order of created nature.

refashioned:
Miracles are not all of the same rank. They differ in three ways, corresponding to three degrees of exceeding natural power.

The highest miracles involve things that nature can never do under any circumstances -- like two bodies occupying the same space, or the sun reversing its course, or a human body being glorified. These exceed nature absolutely.

The second rank involves things nature can do but not in this particular subject. Nature can produce life, but not life in a dead body. Nature can produce sight, but not in a person born blind. The effect itself is natural; its occurrence in this subject is not.

The third rank involves things nature could produce in this subject but not in this way -- healing that nature could eventually accomplish but that happens instantaneously, or rain that comes without any natural meteorological cause.

Within each rank, miracles vary further according to how far they surpass the natural power involved.
elegant: Miracles come in three ranks. The highest: what nature can never do -- two bodies occupying one space, a dead body glorified. The second: what nature can do but not here -- life restored to a corpse, sight to the born blind. The third: what nature could eventually accomplish but not this way -- instant healing, rain without clouds. Within each rank, the further the miracle surpasses natural power, the greater it is.

refashioned: Strictly speaking, only God can work miracles, because miracles by definition exceed the entire order of created nature, and only God acts above that order. Created agents -- even angels -- operate within the order of creation, not above it.
elegant: Only God can work miracles. Angels and demons, however powerful, remain part of the created order. They cannot exceed it. What seems supernatural to us may be merely superhuman.

refashioned:
Can God do things beyond the natural order He has established? Absolutely. The natural order of things was established by God freely, not out of necessity. And there are purposes that transcend what the natural order alone can display -- specifically, the manifestation of grace and divine power. Miracles serve these purposes.

This does not mean God acts against nature in the sense of contradicting it. Rather, God acts above nature. The natural order is embedded within a larger divine order, and when God acts miraculously, He is not breaking His own system -- He is operating at a level that the natural system was always subordinate to.

Nor does this generate disorder. Every miracle, however surprising from the perspective of natural causes, is perfectly orderly from the perspective of divine wisdom. Things only seem irregular when judged by the standard of a particular cause; judged by the universal cause, everything is ordered.
elegant: God is not breaking His own system when He works miracles. He is operating at a level the system was always subordinate to. The natural order is embedded within a larger divine order. What seems irregular from the perspective of a particular cause is perfectly orderly from the perspective of the universal cause.

refashioned:
Can God do anything beyond what He has in fact ordained? Yes. God's power is not limited to what He has actually chosen to do. His wisdom and power exceed the created order, which represents one possible arrangement among many that divine wisdom could have established.

But God cannot do anything that involves a logical contradiction -- not because of a limitation in power, but because contradictions are not real possibilities. The phrase "God cannot make a square circle" does not describe a limit on God; it describes the fact that "square circle" is not a coherent thing to make. Setting aside contradictions, God's power extends beyond any created order.

This also means that God is not bound to maintain the current natural order. He freely established it and could freely establish a different one. What He does is always consistent with His wisdom, but His wisdom is not constrained to a single possible expression.
elegant: God's power is not limited to what He has actually chosen to do. He cannot do what involves a logical contradiction -- not because of weakness, but because contradictions are not real possibilities. "Square circle" is not a coherent thing to make. Setting aside nonsense, God's power extends beyond any created order.

refashioned:
God never acts against His own wisdom and goodness, even when He acts outside the natural order. What appears contrary to nature from a limited perspective is not contrary to the divine order. Just as a household servant might find the master's instructions puzzling without seeing the larger plan, what seems unusual relative to a particular natural cause makes perfect sense relative to the universal cause.

God does not change His mind or correct course. His eternal plan encompasses both the natural order and every exception to it. Miracles are not afterthoughts or patches; they are part of the original design, serving purposes that the routine order of nature alone could not fulfill.
elegant: God never acts against His own wisdom, even when He acts outside the natural order. Miracles are not afterthoughts or patches. They are part of the original design, serving purposes that the routine order of nature alone could not fulfill. What seems puzzling to the servant makes perfect sense in the master's plan.

refashioned: We now turn to how creatures influence one another -- beginning with purely spiritual creatures (angels), then physical bodies, then human beings (who are both). Regarding angels, three topics arise: how angels act on other angels, how angels act on physical nature, and how angels act on human beings. The first of these covers enlightenment and speech among angels, plus the coordination of both good and bad angels. On enlightenment specifically, four questions: Can one angel enlighten another? Can one angel move another's will? Can a lower angel enlighten a higher one? Does a higher angel share everything it knows with a lower one?
elegant: How do creatures influence one another? Aquinas begins with the purest case -- angel acting on angel -- examining how understanding flows downward through spiritual hierarchies, how angels communicate, and how the fallen mirror and distort the order of the faithful.

refashioned:
One angel can enlighten another. The objection that angels in beatitude have no need for teaching from one another mistakes the nature of angelic enlightenment. In the state of glory, no one needs external instruction about God Himself -- all see God directly. But understanding how divine truth unfolds in its effects and applications admits of degrees, and here higher angels can clarify for lower ones what the lower ones grasp only in more general or obscure terms.

Angelic enlightenment is not the transfer of natural light, grace, or glory -- those come from God alone. Rather, one angel strengthens another's capacity to grasp a truth, the way a teacher does not give a student new eyes but helps the student see what was there to be seen.
elegant: One angel can enlighten another -- not by giving it new eyes, but by helping it see what was there to be seen. The higher angel grasps truths in more universal, unified forms. The lower needs them broken down. Enlightenment is the act of proportioning truth to the receiver's capacity.

refashioned:
The mechanism of angelic enlightenment works through proportioning truth to the receiver's capacity. Higher angels grasp truths in more unified, universal forms. Lower angels need those truths broken down into more specific, particular forms to understand them. The higher angel "enlightens" the lower by presenting truth in a form proportioned to the lower angel's intellect -- not dumbing it down, but structuring it so the lesser intellect can receive it.

This is analogous to how a brilliant theorist translates an insight for someone who cannot yet grasp the full abstraction. The content is the same; the mode of presentation changes to match the receiver.
elegant: Angelic illumination works like a brilliant theorist translating an insight for someone who cannot yet grasp the full abstraction. The content is the same; the mode of presentation changes to match the receiver. The higher angel does not dumb truth down -- it structures truth so a lesser intellect can take it in.

refashioned:
One angel cannot directly move another angel's will. The will can only be moved from within by its proper object -- the universal good -- and only God is that. An angel can present something desirable to another angel's intellect, which may indirectly influence the other's will by giving it something new to consider. But no creature can reach into another's will and move it as an interior cause.

Angels can also influence each other by persuasion and by manifesting goods that attract the will. But the actual determination of the will -- the interior act of choosing -- remains inviolable to any external created agent. Only God moves the will from within, because only God is proportionate to the will's deepest object.
elegant: No angel can reach into another's will and move it. The will's interior chamber admits only one key: the universal good, which is God alone. An angel can present something desirable, stir consideration, offer persuasion. But the act of choosing remains inviolable to any external created agent.

refashioned:
